91 /* These options are deprecated */
92 bool C_IgnoreLinearWhiteSpace = false;

99 struct ConfigDef MuttVars[] = {
100  /*++*/
101 
102  { "abort_backspace", DT_BOOL, &C_AbortBackspace, true },
103  /*
104  ** .pp
105  ** If \fIset\fP, hitting backspace against an empty prompt aborts the
106  ** prompt.
107  */
108  { "abort_key", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_AbortKey, IP "\007" },
109  /*
110  ** .pp
111  ** Specifies the key that can be used to abort prompts. The format is the
112  ** same as used in "bind" commands. The default is equivalent to "\G".
113  ** Note that the specified key should not be used in other bindings, as the
114  ** abort operation has higher precedence and the binding will not have the
115  ** desired effect.
116  ** .pp
117  ** Example:
118  ** .ts
119  ** set abort_key = "<Esc>"
120  ** .te
121  ** .pp
122  ** Please note that when using <Esc> as the abort key, you may also want to
123  ** set the environment variable ESCDELAY to a low value or even 0 which will
124  ** reduce the time that ncurses waits to distinguish singular <Esc> key
125  ** presses from the start of a terminal escape sequence. The default time is
126  ** 1000 milliseconds and thus quite noticeable.
127  */
128  { "abort_noattach", DT_QUAD, &C_AbortNoattach, MUTT_NO },
129  /*
130  ** .pp
131  ** If set to \fIyes\fP, when composing messages containing the regular
132  ** expression specified by $$abort_noattach_regex and no attachments are
133  ** given, composition will be aborted. If set to \fIno\fP, composing messages
134  ** as such will never be aborted.
135  ** .pp
136  ** Example:
137  ** .ts
138  ** set abort_noattach_regex = "\<attach(|ed|ments?)\>"
139  ** .te
140  */
141  { "abort_noattach_regex", DT_REGEX, &C_AbortNoattachRegex, IP "\\<(attach|attached|attachments?)\\>" },
142  /*
143  ** .pp
144  ** Specifies a regular expression to match against the body of the message, to
145  ** determine if an attachment was mentioned but mistakenly forgotten. If it
146  ** matches, $$abort_noattach will be consulted to determine if message sending
147  ** will be aborted.
148  ** .pp
149  ** Like other regular expressions in NeoMutt, the search is case sensitive
150  ** if the pattern contains at least one upper case letter, and case
151  ** insensitive otherwise.
152  */
153  { "abort_nosubject", DT_QUAD, &C_AbortNosubject, MUTT_ASKYES },
154  /*
155  ** .pp
156  ** If set to \fIyes\fP, when composing messages and no subject is given
157  ** at the subject prompt, composition will be aborted. If set to
158  ** \fIno\fP, composing messages with no subject given at the subject
159  ** prompt will never be aborted.
160  */
161  { "abort_unmodified", DT_QUAD, &C_AbortUnmodified, MUTT_YES },
162  /*
163  ** .pp
164  ** If set to \fIyes\fP, composition will automatically abort after
165  ** editing the message body if no changes are made to the file (this
166  ** check only happens after the \fIfirst\fP edit of the file). When set
167  ** to \fIno\fP, composition will never be aborted.
168  */
169  { "alias_file", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_FILE, &C_AliasFile, IP "~/.neomuttrc" },
170  /*
171  ** .pp
172  ** The default file in which to save aliases created by the
173  ** \fC$<create-alias>\fP function. Entries added to this file are
174  ** encoded in the character set specified by $$config_charset if it
175  ** is \fIset\fP or the current character set otherwise.
176  ** .pp
177  ** \fBNote:\fP NeoMutt will not automatically source this file; you must
178  ** explicitly use the "$source" command for it to be executed in case
179  ** this option points to a dedicated alias file.
180  ** .pp
181  ** The default for this option is the currently used neomuttrc file, or
182  ** "~/.neomuttrc" if no user neomuttrc was found.
183  */
184  { "alias_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_AliasFormat, IP "%3n %f%t %-15a %-56r | %c" },
185  /*
186  ** .pp
187  ** Specifies the format of the data displayed for the "$alias" menu. The
188  ** following \fCprintf(3)\fP-style sequences are available:
189  ** .dl
190  ** .dt %a .dd Alias name
191  ** .dt %c .dd Comment
192  ** .dt %f .dd Flags - currently, a "d" for an alias marked for deletion
193  ** .dt %n .dd Index number
194  ** .dt %r .dd Address which alias expands to
195  ** .dt %t .dd Character which indicates if the alias is tagged for inclusion
196  ** .dt %>X .dd right justify the rest of the string and pad with character "X"
197  ** .dt %|X .dd pad to the end of the line with character "X"
198  ** .dt %*X .dd soft-fill with character "X" as pad
199  ** .de
200  */
201  { "allow_8bit", DT_BOOL, &C_Allow8bit, true },
202  /*
203  ** .pp
204  ** Controls whether 8-bit data is converted to 7-bit using either Quoted-
205  ** Printable or Base64 encoding when sending mail.
206  */
207  { "allow_ansi", DT_BOOL, &C_AllowAnsi, false },
208  /*
209  ** .pp
210  ** Controls whether ANSI color codes in messages (and color tags in
211  ** rich text messages) are to be interpreted.
212  ** Messages containing these codes are rare, but if this option is \fIset\fP,
213  ** their text will be colored accordingly. Note that this may override
214  ** your color choices, and even present a security problem, since a
215  ** message could include a line like
216  ** .ts
217  ** [-- PGP output follows ...
218  ** .te
219  ** .pp
220  ** and give it the same color as your attachment color (see also
221  ** $$crypt_timestamp).
222  */
223  { "arrow_cursor", DT_BOOL|R_MENU, &C_ArrowCursor, false },
224  /*
225  ** .pp
226  ** When \fIset\fP, an arrow ("->") will be used to indicate the current entry
227  ** in menus instead of highlighting the whole line. On slow network or modem
228  ** links this will make response faster because there is less that has to
229  ** be redrawn on the screen when moving to the next or previous entries
230  ** in the menu.
231  */
232  { "arrow_string", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_ArrowString, IP "->" },
233  /*
234  ** .pp
235  ** Specifies the string of arrow_cursor when arrow_cursor enabled.
236  */
237  { "ascii_chars", DT_BOOL|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_AsciiChars, false },
238  /*
239  ** .pp
240  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will use plain ASCII characters when displaying thread
241  ** and attachment trees, instead of the default \fIACS\fP characters.
242  */
243 #ifdef USE_NNTP
244  { "ask_follow_up", DT_BOOL, &C_AskFollowUp, false },
245  /*
246  ** .pp
247  ** If set, NeoMutt will prompt you for follow-up groups before editing
248  ** the body of an outgoing message.
249  */
250  { "ask_x_comment_to", DT_BOOL, &C_AskXCommentTo, false },
251  /*
252  ** .pp
253  ** If set, NeoMutt will prompt you for x-comment-to field before editing
254  ** the body of an outgoing message.
255  */
256 #endif
257  { "askbcc", DT_BOOL, &C_Askbcc, false },
258  /*
259  ** .pp
260  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will prompt you for blind-carbon-copy (Bcc) recipients
261  ** before editing an outgoing message.
262  */
263  { "askcc", DT_BOOL, &C_Askcc, false },
264  /*
265  ** .pp
266  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will prompt you for carbon-copy (Cc) recipients before
267  ** editing the body of an outgoing message.
268  */
269  { "assumed_charset", DT_STRING, &C_AssumedCharset, 0, 0, charset_validator },
270  /*
271  ** .pp
272  ** This variable is a colon-separated list of character encoding
273  ** schemes for messages without character encoding indication.
274  ** Header field values and message body content without character encoding
275  ** indication would be assumed that they are written in one of this list.
276  ** By default, all the header fields and message body without any charset
277  ** indication are assumed to be in "us-ascii".
278  ** .pp
279  ** For example, Japanese users might prefer this:
280  ** .ts
281  ** set assumed_charset="iso-2022-jp:euc-jp:shift_jis:utf-8"
282  ** .te
283  ** .pp
284  ** However, only the first content is valid for the message body.
285  */
286  { "attach_charset", DT_STRING, &C_AttachCharset, 0, 0, charset_validator },
287  /*
288  ** .pp
289  ** This variable is a colon-separated list of character encoding
290  ** schemes for text file attachments. NeoMutt uses this setting to guess
291  ** which encoding files being attached are encoded in to convert them to
292  ** a proper character set given in $$send_charset.
293  ** .pp
294  ** If \fIunset\fP, the value of $$charset will be used instead.
295  ** For example, the following configuration would work for Japanese
296  ** text handling:
297  ** .ts
298  ** set attach_charset="iso-2022-jp:euc-jp:shift_jis:utf-8"
299  ** .te
300  ** .pp
301  ** Note: for Japanese users, "iso-2022-*" must be put at the head
302  ** of the value as shown above if included.
303  */
304  { "attach_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_AttachFormat, IP "%u%D%I %t%4n %T%.40d%> [%.7m/%.10M, %.6e%?C?, %C?, %s] " },
305  /*
306  ** .pp
307  ** This variable describes the format of the "attachment" menu. The
308  ** following \fCprintf(3)\fP-style sequences are understood:
309  ** .dl
310  ** .dt %C .dd Charset
311  ** .dt %c .dd Requires charset conversion ("n" or "c")
312  ** .dt %D .dd Deleted flag
313  ** .dt %d .dd Description (if none, falls back to %F)
314  ** .dt %e .dd MIME content-transfer-encoding
315  ** .dt %f .dd Filename
316  ** .dt %F .dd Filename in content-disposition header (if none, falls back to %f)
317  ** .dt %I .dd Disposition ("I" for inline, "A" for attachment)
318  ** .dt %m .dd Major MIME type
319  ** .dt %M .dd MIME subtype
320  ** .dt %n .dd Attachment number
321  ** .dt %Q .dd "Q", if MIME part qualifies for attachment counting
322  ** .dt %s .dd Size (see $formatstrings-size)
323  ** .dt %T .dd Graphic tree characters
324  ** .dt %t .dd Tagged flag
325  ** .dt %u .dd Unlink (=to delete) flag
326  ** .dt %X .dd Number of qualifying MIME parts in this part and its children
327  ** (please see the "$attachments" section for possible speed effects)
328  ** .dt %>X .dd Right justify the rest of the string and pad with character "X"
329  ** .dt %|X .dd Pad to the end of the line with character "X"
330  ** .dt %*X .dd Soft-fill with character "X" as pad
331  ** .de
332  ** .pp
333  ** For an explanation of "soft-fill", see the $$index_format documentation.
334  */
335  { "attach_save_dir", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_DIR, &C_AttachSaveDir, IP "./" },
336  /*
337  ** .pp
338  ** The directory where attachments are saved.
339  */
340  { "attach_save_without_prompting", DT_BOOL, &C_AttachSaveWithoutPrompting, false },
341  /*
342  ** .pp
343  ** This variable, when set to true, will cause attachments to be saved to
344  ** the 'attach_save_dir' location without prompting the user for the filename.
345  ** .pp
346  */
347  { "attach_sep", DT_STRING, &C_AttachSep, IP "\n" },
348  /*
349  ** .pp
350  ** The separator to add between attachments when operating (saving,
351  ** printing, piping, etc) on a list of tagged attachments.
352  */
353  { "attach_split", DT_BOOL, &C_AttachSplit, true },
354  /*
355  ** .pp
356  ** If this variable is \fIunset\fP, when operating (saving, printing, piping,
357  ** etc) on a list of tagged attachments, NeoMutt will concatenate the
358  ** attachments and will operate on them as a single attachment. The
359  ** $$attach_sep separator is added after each attachment. When \fIset\fP,
360  ** NeoMutt will operate on the attachments one by one.
361  */
362  { "attribution", DT_STRING, &C_Attribution, IP "On %d, %n wrote:" },
363  /*
364  ** .pp
365  ** This is the string that will precede a message which has been included
366  ** in a reply. For a full listing of defined \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences see
367  ** the section on $$index_format.
368  */
369  { "attribution_locale", DT_STRING, &C_AttributionLocale, 0 },
370  /*
371  ** .pp
372  ** The locale used by \fCstrftime(3)\fP to format dates in the
373  ** $attribution string. Legal values are the strings your system
374  ** accepts for the locale environment variable \fC$$$LC_TIME\fP.
375  ** .pp
376  ** This variable is to allow the attribution date format to be
377  ** customized by recipient or folder using hooks. By default, NeoMutt
378  ** will use your locale environment, so there is no need to set
379  ** this except to override that default.
380  */
381  { "auto_subscribe", DT_BOOL, &C_AutoSubscribe, false },
382  /*
383  ** .pp
384  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt assumes the presence of a List-Post header
385  ** means the recipient is subscribed to the list. Unless the mailing list
386  ** is in the "unsubscribe" or "unlist" lists, it will be added
387  ** to the "$subscribe" list. Parsing and checking these things slows
388  ** header reading down, so this option is disabled by default.
389  */
390  { "auto_tag", DT_BOOL, &C_AutoTag, false },
391  /*
392  ** .pp
393  ** When \fIset\fP, functions in the \fIindex\fP menu which affect a message
394  ** will be applied to all tagged messages (if there are any). When
395  ** unset, you must first use the \fC<tag-prefix>\fP function (bound to ";"
396  ** by default) to make the next function apply to all tagged messages.
397  */
398 #ifdef USE_AUTOCRYPT
399  { "autocrypt", DT_BOOL, &C_Autocrypt, false },
400  /*
401  ** .pp
402  ** When \fIset\fP, enables autocrypt, which provides
403  ** passive encryption protection with keys exchanged via headers.
404  ** See ``$autocryptdoc'' for more details.
405  ** (Autocrypt only)
406  */
407  { "autocrypt_acct_format", DT_STRING|R_MENU, &C_AutocryptAcctFormat, IP "%4n %-30a %20p %10s" },
408  /*
409  ** .pp
410  ** This variable describes the format of the ``autocrypt account'' menu.
411  ** The following \fCprintf(3)\fP-style sequences are understood
412  ** .dl
413  ** .dt %a .dd email address
414  ** .dt %k .dd gpg keyid
415  ** .dt %n .dd current entry number
416  ** .dt %p .dd prefer-encrypt flag
417  ** .dt %s .dd status flag (active/inactive)
418  ** .de
419  ** .pp
420  ** (Autocrypt only)
421  */
422  { "autocrypt_dir", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_DIR, &C_AutocryptDir, IP "~/.mutt/autocrypt" },
423  /*
424  ** .pp
425  ** This variable sets where autocrypt files are stored, including the GPG
426  ** keyring and SQLite database. See ``$autocryptdoc'' for more details.
427  ** (Autocrypt only)
428  */
429  { "autocrypt_reply", DT_BOOL, &C_AutocryptReply, true },
430  /*
431  ** .pp
432  ** When \fIset\fP, replying to an autocrypt email automatically
433  ** enables autocrypt in the reply. You may want to unset this if you're using
434  ** the same key for autocrypt as normal web-of-trust, so that autocrypt
435  ** isn't forced on for all encrypted replies.
436  ** (Autocrypt only)
437  */
438 #endif
439  { "autoedit", DT_BOOL, &C_Autoedit, false },
440  /*
441  ** .pp
442  ** When \fIset\fP along with $$edit_headers, NeoMutt will skip the initial
443  ** send-menu (prompting for subject and recipients) and allow you to
444  ** immediately begin editing the body of your
445  ** message. The send-menu may still be accessed once you have finished
446  ** editing the body of your message.
447  ** .pp
448  ** \fBNote:\fP when this option is \fIset\fP, you can't use send-hooks that depend
449  ** on the recipients when composing a new (non-reply) message, as the initial
450  ** list of recipients is empty.
451  ** .pp
452  ** Also see $$fast_reply.
453  */
454  { "beep", DT_BOOL, &C_Beep, true },
455  /*
456  ** .pp
457  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will beep when an error occurs.
458  */
459  { "beep_new", DT_BOOL, &C_BeepNew, false },
460  /*
461  ** .pp
462  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will beep whenever it prints a message
463  ** notifying you of new mail. This is independent of the setting of the
464  ** $$beep variable.
465  */
466  { "bounce", DT_QUAD, &C_Bounce, MUTT_ASKYES },
467  /*
468  ** .pp
469  ** Controls whether you will be asked to confirm bouncing messages.
470  ** If set to \fIyes\fP you don't get asked if you want to bounce a
471  ** message. Setting this variable to \fIno\fP is not generally useful,
472  ** and thus not recommended, because you are unable to bounce messages.
473  */
474  { "bounce_delivered", DT_BOOL, &C_BounceDelivered, true },
475  /*
476  ** .pp
477  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will include Delivered-To headers when
478  ** bouncing messages. Postfix users may wish to \fIunset\fP this variable.
479  */
480  { "braille_friendly", DT_BOOL, &C_BrailleFriendly, false },
481  /*
482  ** .pp
483  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will place the cursor at the beginning
484  ** of the current line in menus, even when the $$arrow_cursor variable
485  ** is \fIunset\fP, making it easier for blind persons using Braille displays to
486  ** follow these menus. The option is \fIunset\fP by default because many
487  ** visual terminals don't permit making the cursor invisible.
488  */
489  { "browser_abbreviate_mailboxes", DT_BOOL, &C_BrowserAbbreviateMailboxes, true },
490  /*
491  ** .pp
492  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will abbreviate mailbox
493  ** names in the browser mailbox list, using '~' and '='
494  ** shortcuts.
495  ** .pp
496  ** The default \fC"alpha"\fP setting of $$sort_browser uses
497  ** locale-based sorting (using \fCstrcoll(3)\fP), which ignores some
498  ** punctuation. This can lead to some situations where the order
499  ** doesn't make intuitive sense. In those cases, it may be
500  ** desirable to \fIunset\fP this variable.
501  */
502 #ifdef USE_NNTP
503  { "catchup_newsgroup", DT_QUAD, &C_CatchupNewsgroup, MUTT_ASKYES },
504  /*
505  ** .pp
506  ** If this variable is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will mark all articles in newsgroup
507  ** as read when you quit the newsgroup (catchup newsgroup).
508  */
509 #endif
510 #ifdef USE_SSL
511  { "certificate_file", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_FILE, &C_CertificateFile, IP "~/.mutt_certificates" },
512  /*
513  ** .pp
514  ** This variable specifies the file where the certificates you trust
515  ** are saved. When an unknown certificate is encountered, you are asked
516  ** if you accept it or not. If you accept it, the certificate can also
517  ** be saved in this file and further connections are automatically
518  ** accepted.
519  ** .pp
520  ** You can also manually add CA certificates in this file. Any server
521  ** certificate that is signed with one of these CA certificates is
522  ** also automatically accepted.
523  ** .pp
524  ** Example:
525  ** .ts
526  ** set certificate_file=~/.neomutt/certificates
527  ** .te
528  */
529 #endif
530  { "change_folder_next", DT_BOOL, &C_ChangeFolderNext, false },
531  /*
532  ** .pp
533  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP, the \fC<change-folder>\fP function
534  ** mailbox suggestion will start at the next folder in your "$mailboxes"
535  ** list, instead of starting at the first folder in the list.
536  */
537  { "charset", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_Charset, 0, 0, charset_validator },
538  /*
539  ** .pp
540  ** Character set your terminal uses to display and enter textual data.
541  ** It is also the fallback for $$send_charset.
542  ** .pp
543  ** Upon startup NeoMutt tries to derive this value from environment variables
544  ** such as \fC$$$LC_CTYPE\fP or \fC$$$LANG\fP.
545  ** .pp
546  ** \fBNote:\fP It should only be set in case NeoMutt isn't able to determine the
547  ** character set used correctly.
548  */
549  { "check_mbox_size", DT_BOOL, &C_CheckMboxSize, false },
550  /*
551  ** .pp
552  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will use file size attribute instead of
553  ** access time when checking for new mail in mbox and mmdf folders.
554  ** .pp
555  ** This variable is \fIunset\fP by default and should only be enabled when
556  ** new mail detection for these folder types is unreliable or doesn't work.
557  ** .pp
558  ** Note that enabling this variable should happen before any "$mailboxes"
559  ** directives occur in configuration files regarding mbox or mmdf folders
560  ** because NeoMutt needs to determine the initial new mail status of such a
561  ** mailbox by performing a fast mailbox scan when it is defined.
562  ** Afterwards the new mail status is tracked by file size changes.
563  */
564  { "check_new", DT_BOOL, &C_CheckNew, true },
565  /*
566  ** .pp
567  ** \fBNote:\fP this option only affects \fImaildir\fP and \fIMH\fP style
568  ** mailboxes.
569  ** .pp
570  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will check for new mail delivered while the
571  ** mailbox is open. Especially with MH mailboxes, this operation can
572  ** take quite some time since it involves scanning the directory and
573  ** checking each file to see if it has already been looked at. If
574  ** this variable is \fIunset\fP, no check for new mail is performed
575  ** while the mailbox is open.
576  */
577  { "collapse_all", DT_BOOL, &C_CollapseAll, false },
578  /*
579  ** .pp
580  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will collapse all threads when entering a folder.
581  */
582  { "collapse_flagged", DT_BOOL, &C_CollapseFlagged, true },
583  /*
584  ** .pp
585  ** When \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will not collapse a thread if it contains any
586  ** flagged messages.
587  */
588  { "collapse_unread", DT_BOOL, &C_CollapseUnread, true },
589  /*
590  ** .pp
591  ** When \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will not collapse a thread if it contains any
592  ** unread messages.
593  */
594  { "compose_format", DT_STRING|R_MENU, &C_ComposeFormat, IP "-- NeoMutt: Compose [Approx. msg size: %l Atts: %a]%>-" },
595  /*
596  ** .pp
597  ** Controls the format of the status line displayed in the "compose"
598  ** menu. This string is similar to $$status_format, but has its own
599  ** set of \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences:
600  ** .dl
601  ** .dt %a .dd Total number of attachments
602  ** .dt %h .dd Local hostname
603  ** .dt %l .dd Approximate size (in bytes) of the current message (see $formatstrings-size)
604  ** .dt %v .dd NeoMutt version string
605  ** .dt %>X .dd right justify the rest of the string and pad with character "X"
606  ** .dt %|X .dd pad to the end of the line with character "X"
607  ** .dt %*X .dd soft-fill with character "X" as pad
608  ** .de
609  ** .pp
610  ** See the text describing the $$status_format option for more
611  ** information on how to set $$compose_format.
612  */
613  { "config_charset", DT_STRING, &C_ConfigCharset, 0, 0, charset_validator },
614  /*
615  ** .pp
616  ** When defined, NeoMutt will recode commands in rc files from this
617  ** encoding to the current character set as specified by $$charset
618  ** and aliases written to $$alias_file from the current character set.
619  ** .pp
620  ** Please note that if setting $$charset it must be done before
621  ** setting $$config_charset.
622  ** .pp
623  ** Recoding should be avoided as it may render unconvertable
624  ** characters as question marks which can lead to undesired
625  ** side effects (for example in regular expressions).
626  */
627  { "confirmappend", DT_BOOL, &C_Confirmappend, true },
628  /*
629  ** .pp
630  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will prompt for confirmation when appending messages to
631  ** an existing mailbox.
632  */
633  { "confirmcreate", DT_BOOL, &C_Confirmcreate, true },
634  /*
635  ** .pp
636  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will prompt for confirmation when saving messages to a
637  ** mailbox which does not yet exist before creating it.
638  */
639  { "connect_timeout", DT_NUMBER, &C_ConnectTimeout, 30 },
640  /*
641  ** .pp
642  ** Causes NeoMutt to timeout a network connection (for IMAP, POP or SMTP) after this
643  ** many seconds if the connection is not able to be established. A negative
644  ** value causes NeoMutt to wait indefinitely for the connection attempt to succeed.
645  */
646  { "content_type", DT_STRING, &C_ContentType, IP "text/plain" },
647  /*
648  ** .pp
649  ** Sets the default Content-Type for the body of newly composed messages.
650  */
651  { "copy", DT_QUAD, &C_Copy, MUTT_YES },
652  /*
653  ** .pp
654  ** This variable controls whether or not copies of your outgoing messages
655  ** will be saved for later references. Also see $$record,
656  ** $$save_name, $$force_name and "$fcc-hook".
657  */
658  { "crypt_autoencrypt", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptAutoencrypt, false },
659  /*
660  ** .pp
661  ** Setting this variable will cause NeoMutt to always attempt to PGP
662  ** encrypt outgoing messages. This is probably only useful in
663  ** connection to the "$send-hook" command. It can be overridden
664  ** by use of the pgp menu, when encryption is not required or
665  ** signing is requested as well. If $$smime_is_default is \fIset\fP,
666  ** then OpenSSL is used instead to create S/MIME messages and
667  ** settings can be overridden by use of the smime menu instead.
668  ** (Crypto only)
669  */
670  { "crypt_autopgp", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptAutopgp, true },
671  /*
672  ** .pp
673  ** This variable controls whether or not NeoMutt may automatically enable
674  ** PGP encryption/signing for messages. See also $$crypt_autoencrypt,
675  ** $$crypt_replyencrypt,
676  ** $$crypt_autosign, $$crypt_replysign and $$smime_is_default.
677  */
678  { "crypt_autosign", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptAutosign, false },
679  /*
680  ** .pp
681  ** Setting this variable will cause NeoMutt to always attempt to
682  ** cryptographically sign outgoing messages. This can be overridden
683  ** by use of the pgp menu, when signing is not required or
684  ** encryption is requested as well. If $$smime_is_default is \fIset\fP,
685  ** then OpenSSL is used instead to create S/MIME messages and settings can
686  ** be overridden by use of the smime menu instead of the pgp menu.
687  ** (Crypto only)
688  */
689  { "crypt_autosmime", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptAutosmime, true },
690  /*
691  ** .pp
692  ** This variable controls whether or not NeoMutt may automatically enable
693  ** S/MIME encryption/signing for messages. See also $$crypt_autoencrypt,
694  ** $$crypt_replyencrypt,
695  ** $$crypt_autosign, $$crypt_replysign and $$smime_is_default.
696  */
697  { "crypt_chars", DT_MBTABLE|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_CryptChars, IP "SPsK " },
698  /*
699  ** .pp
700  ** Controls the characters used in cryptography flags.
701  ** .dl
702  ** .dt \fBCharacter\fP .dd \fBDefault\fP .dd \fBDescription\fP
703  ** .dt 1 .dd S .dd The mail is signed, and the signature is successfully verified.
704  ** .dt 2 .dd P .dd The mail is PGP encrypted.
705  ** .dt 3 .dd s .dd The mail is signed.
706  ** .dt 4 .dd K .dd The mail contains a PGP public key.
707  ** .dt 5 .dd <space> .dd The mail has no crypto info.
708  ** .de
709  */
710  { "crypt_confirmhook", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptConfirmhook, true },
711  /*
712  ** .pp
713  ** If set, then you will be prompted for confirmation of keys when using
714  ** the \fIcrypt-hook\fP command. If unset, no such confirmation prompt will
715  ** be presented. This is generally considered unsafe, especially where
716  ** typos are concerned.
717  */
718  { "crypt_opportunistic_encrypt", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptOpportunisticEncrypt, false },
719  /*
720  ** .pp
721  ** Setting this variable will cause NeoMutt to automatically enable and
722  ** disable encryption, based on whether all message recipient keys
723  ** can be located by NeoMutt.
724  ** .pp
725  ** When this option is enabled, NeoMutt will enable/disable encryption
726  ** each time the TO, CC, and BCC lists are edited. If
727  ** $$edit_headers is set, NeoMutt will also do so each time the message
728  ** is edited.
729  ** .pp
730  ** While this is set, encryption can't be manually enabled/disabled.
731  ** The pgp or smime menus provide a selection to temporarily disable
732  ** this option for the current message.
733  ** .pp
734  ** If $$crypt_autoencrypt or $$crypt_replyencrypt enable encryption for
735  ** a message, this option will be disabled for that message. It can
736  ** be manually re-enabled in the pgp or smime menus.
737  ** (Crypto only)
738  */
739  { "crypt_opportunistic_encrypt_strong_keys", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptOpportunisticEncryptStrongKeys, false },
740  /*
741  ** .pp
742  ** When set, this modifies the behavior of $$crypt_opportunistic_encrypt
743  ** to only search for "strong keys", that is, keys with full validity
744  ** according to the web-of-trust algorithm. A key with marginal or no
745  ** validity will not enable opportunistic encryption.
746  ** .pp
747  ** For S/MIME, the behavior depends on the backend. Classic S/MIME will
748  ** filter for certificates with the 't'(trusted) flag in the .index file.
749  ** The GPGME backend will use the same filters as with OpenPGP, and depends
750  ** on GPGME's logic for assigning the GPGME_VALIDITY_FULL and
751  ** GPGME_VALIDITY_ULTIMATE validity flag.
752  */
753  { "crypt_protected_headers_read", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptProtectedHeadersRead, true },
754  /*
755  ** .pp
756  ** When set, NeoMutt will display protected headers ("Memory Hole") in the pager,
757  ** and will update the index and header cache with revised headers.
758  ** .pp
759  ** Protected headers are stored inside the encrypted or signed part of an
760  ** an email, to prevent disclosure or tampering.
761  ** For more information see https://github.com/autocrypt/memoryhole.
762  ** Currently NeoMutt only supports the Subject header.
763  ** .pp
764  ** Encrypted messages using protected headers often substitute the exposed
765  ** Subject header with a dummy value (see $$crypt_protected_headers_subject).
766  ** NeoMutt will update its concept of the correct subject \fBafter\fP the
767  ** message is opened, i.e. via the \fC<display-message>\fP function.
768  ** If you reply to a message before opening it, NeoMutt will end up using
769  ** the dummy Subject header, so be sure to open such a message first.
770  ** (Crypto only)
771  */
772  { "crypt_protected_headers_save", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptProtectedHeadersSave, false },
773  /*
774  ** .pp
775  ** When $$crypt_protected_headers_read is set, and a message with a
776  ** protected Subject is opened, NeoMutt will save the updated Subject
777  ** into the header cache by default. This allows searching/limiting
778  ** based on the protected Subject header if the mailbox is
779  ** re-opened, without having to re-open the message each time.
780  ** However, for mbox/mh mailbox types, or if header caching is not
781  ** set up, you would need to re-open the message each time the
782  ** mailbox was reopened before you could see or search/limit on the
783  ** protected subject again.
784  ** .pp
785  ** When this variable is set, NeoMutt additionally saves the protected
786  ** Subject back \fBin the clear-text message headers\fP. This
787  ** provides better usability, but with the tradeoff of reduced
788  ** security. The protected Subject header, which may have
789  ** previously been encrypted, is now stored in clear-text in the
790  ** message headers. Copying the message elsewhere, via NeoMutt or
791  ** external tools, could expose this previously encrypted data.
792  ** Please make sure you understand the consequences of this before
793  ** you enable this variable.
794  ** (Crypto only)
795  */
796  { "crypt_protected_headers_subject", DT_STRING, &C_CryptProtectedHeadersSubject, IP "Encrypted subject" },
797  /*
798  ** .pp
799  ** When $$crypt_protected_headers_write is set, and the message is marked
800  ** for encryption, this will be substituted into the Subject field in the
801  ** message headers.
802  ** .pp
803  ** To prevent a subject from being substituted, unset this variable, or set it
804  ** to the empty string.
805  ** (Crypto only)
806  */
807  { "crypt_protected_headers_write", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptProtectedHeadersWrite, false },
808  /*
809  ** .pp
810  ** When set, NeoMutt will generate protected headers ("Memory Hole") for
811  ** signed and encrypted emails.
812  ** .pp
813  ** Protected headers are stored inside the encrypted or signed part of an
814  ** an email, to prevent disclosure or tampering.
815  ** For more information see https://github.com/autocrypt/memoryhole.
816  ** .pp
817  ** Currently NeoMutt only supports the Subject header.
818  ** (Crypto only)
819  */
820  { "crypt_replyencrypt", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptReplyencrypt, true },
821  /*
822  ** .pp
823  ** If \fIset\fP, automatically PGP or OpenSSL encrypt replies to messages which are
824  ** encrypted.
825  ** (Crypto only)
826  */
827  { "crypt_replysign", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptReplysign, false },
828  /*
829  ** .pp
830  ** If \fIset\fP, automatically PGP or OpenSSL sign replies to messages which are
831  ** signed.
832  ** .pp
833  ** \fBNote:\fP this does not work on messages that are encrypted
834  ** \fIand\fP signed!
835  ** (Crypto only)
836  */
837  { "crypt_replysignencrypted", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptReplysignencrypted, false },
838  /*
839  ** .pp
840  ** If \fIset\fP, automatically PGP or OpenSSL sign replies to messages
841  ** which are encrypted. This makes sense in combination with
842  ** $$crypt_replyencrypt, because it allows you to sign all
843  ** messages which are automatically encrypted. This works around
844  ** the problem noted in $$crypt_replysign, that NeoMutt is not able
845  ** to find out whether an encrypted message is also signed.
846  ** (Crypto only)
847  */
848  { "crypt_timestamp", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptTimestamp, true },
849  /*
850  ** .pp
851  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will include a time stamp in the lines surrounding
852  ** PGP or S/MIME output, so spoofing such lines is more difficult.
853  ** If you are using colors to mark these lines, and rely on these,
854  ** you may \fIunset\fP this setting.
855  ** (Crypto only)
856  */
857 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_GPGME
858  { "crypt_use_gpgme", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptUseGpgme, true },
859  /*
860  ** .pp
861  ** This variable controls the use of the GPGME-enabled crypto backends.
862  ** If it is \fIset\fP and NeoMutt was built with GPGME support, the gpgme code for
863  ** S/MIME and PGP will be used instead of the classic code. Note that
864  ** you need to set this option in .neomuttrc; it won't have any effect when
865  ** used interactively.
866  ** .pp
867  ** Note that the GPGME backend does not support creating old-style inline
868  ** (traditional) PGP encrypted or signed messages (see $$pgp_autoinline).
869  */
870  { "crypt_use_pka", DT_BOOL, &C_CryptUsePka, false },
871  /*
872  ** .pp
873  ** Controls whether NeoMutt uses PKA
874  ** (see http://www.g10code.de/docs/pka-intro.de.pdf) during signature
875  ** verification (only supported by the GPGME backend).
876  */
877 #endif
878  { "crypt_verify_sig", DT_QUAD, &C_CryptVerifySig, MUTT_YES },
879  /*
880  ** .pp
881  ** If \fI"yes"\fP, always attempt to verify PGP or S/MIME signatures.
882  ** If \fI"ask-*"\fP, ask whether or not to verify the signature.
883  ** If \fI"no"\fP, never attempt to verify cryptographic signatures.
884  ** (Crypto only)
885  */
886  { "date_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY|R_MENU, &C_DateFormat, IP "!%a, %b %d, %Y at %I:%M:%S%p %Z" },
887  /*
888  ** .pp
889  ** This variable controls the format of the date printed by the "%d"
890  ** sequence in $$index_format. This is passed to the \fCstrftime(3)\fP
891  ** function to process the date, see the man page for the proper syntax.
892  ** .pp
893  ** Unless the first character in the string is a bang ("!"), the month
894  ** and week day names are expanded according to the locale.
895  ** If the first character in the string is a
896  ** bang, the bang is discarded, and the month and week day names in the
897  ** rest of the string are expanded in the \fIC\fP locale (that is in US
898  ** English).
899  */
900  { "debug_file", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_FILE, &C_DebugFile, IP "~/.neomuttdebug" },
901  /*
902  ** .pp
903  ** Debug logging is controlled by the variables \fC$$debug_file\fP and \fC$$debug_level\fP.
904  ** \fC$$debug_file\fP specifies the root of the filename. NeoMutt will add "0" to the end.
905  ** Each time NeoMutt is run with logging enabled, the log files are rotated.
906  ** A maximum of five log files are kept, numbered 0 (most recent) to 4 (oldest).
907  ** .pp
908  ** This option can be enabled on the command line, "neomutt -l mylog"
909  ** .pp
910  ** See also: \fC$$debug_level\fP
911  */
912  { "debug_level", DT_NUMBER, &C_DebugLevel, 0, 0, level_validator },
913  /*
914  ** .pp
915  ** Debug logging is controlled by the variables \fC$$debug_file\fP and \fC$$debug_level\fP.
916  ** .pp
917  ** The debug level controls how much information is saved to the log file.
918  ** If you have a problem with NeoMutt, then enabling logging may help find the cause.
919  ** Levels 1-3 will usually provide enough information for writing a bug report.
920  ** Levels 4,5 will be extremely verbose.
921  ** .pp
922  ** Warning: Logging at high levels may save private information to the file.
923  ** .pp
924  ** This option can be enabled on the command line, "neomutt -d 2"
925  ** .pp
926  ** See also: \fC$$debug_file\fP
927  */
928  { "default_hook", DT_STRING, &C_DefaultHook, IP "~f %s !~P | (~P ~C %s)" },
929  /*
930  ** .pp
931  ** This variable controls how "$message-hook", "$reply-hook", "$send-hook",
932  ** "$send2-hook", "$save-hook", and "$fcc-hook" will
933  ** be interpreted if they are specified with only a simple regex,
934  ** instead of a matching pattern. The hooks are expanded when they are
935  ** declared, so a hook will be interpreted according to the value of this
936  ** variable at the time the hook is declared.
937  ** .pp
938  ** The default value matches
939  ** if the message is either from a user matching the regular expression
940  ** given, or if it is from you (if the from address matches
941  ** "$alternates") and is to or cc'ed to a user matching the given
942  ** regular expression.
943  */
944  { "delete", DT_QUAD, &C_Delete, MUTT_ASKYES },
945  /*
946  ** .pp
947  ** Controls whether or not messages are really deleted when closing or
948  ** synchronizing a mailbox. If set to \fIyes\fP, messages marked for
949  ** deleting will automatically be purged without prompting. If set to
950  ** \fIno\fP, messages marked for deletion will be kept in the mailbox.
951  */
952  { "delete_untag", DT_BOOL, &C_DeleteUntag, true },
953  /*
954  ** .pp
955  ** If this option is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will untag messages when marking them
956  ** for deletion. This applies when you either explicitly delete a message,
957  ** or when you save it to another folder.
958  */
959  { "digest_collapse", DT_BOOL, &C_DigestCollapse, true },
960  /*
961  ** .pp
962  ** If this option is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt's received-attachments menu will not show the subparts of
963  ** individual messages in a multipart/digest. To see these subparts, press "v" on that menu.
964  */
965  { "display_filter", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND|R_PAGER, &C_DisplayFilter, 0 },
966  /*
967  ** .pp
968  ** When set, specifies a command used to filter messages. When a message
969  ** is viewed it is passed as standard input to $$display_filter, and the
970  ** filtered message is read from the standard output.
971  ** .pp
972  ** When preparing the message, NeoMutt inserts some escape sequences into the
973  ** text. They are of the form: \fC<esc>]9;XXX<bel>\fP where "XXX" is a random
974  ** 64-bit number.
975  ** .pp
976  ** If these escape sequences interfere with your filter, they can be removed
977  ** using a tool like \fCansifilter\fP or \fCsed 's/^\x1b]9;[0-9]\+\x7//'\fP
978  ** .pp
979  ** If they are removed, then PGP and MIME headers will no longer be coloured.
980  ** This can be fixed by adding this to your config:
981  ** \fCcolor body magenta default '^\[-- .* --\]$$$'\fP.
982  */
983  { "dsn_notify", DT_STRING, &C_DsnNotify, 0 },
984  /*
985  ** .pp
986  ** This variable sets the request for when notification is returned. The
987  ** string consists of a comma separated list (no spaces!) of one or more
988  ** of the following: \fInever\fP, to never request notification,
989  ** \fIfailure\fP, to request notification on transmission failure,
990  ** \fIdelay\fP, to be notified of message delays, \fIsuccess\fP, to be
991  ** notified of successful transmission.
992  ** .pp
993  ** Example:
994  ** .ts
995  ** set dsn_notify="failure,delay"
996  ** .te
997  ** .pp
998  ** \fBNote:\fP when using $$sendmail for delivery, you should not enable
999  ** this unless you are either using Sendmail 8.8.x or greater or a MTA
1000  ** providing a \fCsendmail(1)\fP-compatible interface supporting the \fC-N\fP option
1001  ** for DSN. For SMTP delivery, DSN support is auto-detected so that it
1002  ** depends on the server whether DSN will be used or not.
1003  */
1004  { "dsn_return", DT_STRING, &C_DsnReturn, 0 },
1005  /*
1006  ** .pp
1007  ** This variable controls how much of your message is returned in DSN
1008  ** messages. It may be set to either \fIhdrs\fP to return just the
1009  ** message header, or \fIfull\fP to return the full message.
1010  ** .pp
1011  ** Example:
1012  ** .ts
1013  ** set dsn_return=hdrs
1014  ** .te
1015  ** .pp
1016  ** \fBNote:\fP when using $$sendmail for delivery, you should not enable
1017  ** this unless you are either using Sendmail 8.8.x or greater or a MTA
1018  ** providing a \fCsendmail(1)\fP-compatible interface supporting the \fC-R\fP option
1019  ** for DSN. For SMTP delivery, DSN support is auto-detected so that it
1020  ** depends on the server whether DSN will be used or not.
1021  */
1022  { "duplicate_threads", DT_BOOL|R_RESORT|R_RESORT_INIT|R_INDEX, &C_DuplicateThreads, true, 0, pager_validator },
1023  /*
1024  ** .pp
1025  ** This variable controls whether NeoMutt, when $$sort is set to \fIthreads\fP, threads
1026  ** messages with the same Message-Id together. If it is \fIset\fP, it will indicate
1027  ** that it thinks they are duplicates of each other with an equals sign
1028  ** in the thread tree.
1029  */
1030  { "edit_headers", DT_BOOL, &C_EditHeaders, false },
1031  /*
1032  ** .pp
1033  ** This option allows you to edit the header of your outgoing messages
1034  ** along with the body of your message.
1035  ** .pp
1036  ** Although the compose menu may have localized header labels, the
1037  ** labels passed to your editor will be standard RFC2822 headers,
1038  ** (e.g. To:, Cc:, Subject:). Headers added in your editor must
1039  ** also be RFC2822 headers, or one of the pseudo headers listed in
1040  ** "$edit-header". NeoMutt will not understand localized header
1041  ** labels, just as it would not when parsing an actual email.
1042  ** .pp
1043  ** \fBNote\fP that changes made to the References: and Date: headers are
1044  ** ignored for interoperability reasons.
1045  */
1046  { "editor", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_Editor, IP "vi" },
1047  /*
1048  ** .pp
1049  ** This variable specifies which editor is used by NeoMutt.
1050  ** It defaults to the value of the \fC$$$VISUAL\fP, or \fC$$$EDITOR\fP, environment
1051  ** variable, or to the string "vi" if neither of those are set.
1052  ** .pp
1053  ** The \fC$$editor\fP string may contain a \fI%s\fP escape, which will be replaced by the name
1054  ** of the file to be edited. If the \fI%s\fP escape does not appear in \fC$$editor\fP, a
1055  ** space and the name to be edited are appended.
1056  ** .pp
1057  ** The resulting string is then executed by running
1058  ** .ts
1059  ** sh -c 'string'
1060  ** .te
1061  ** .pp
1062  ** where \fIstring\fP is the expansion of \fC$$editor\fP described above.
1063  */
1064  { "empty_subject", DT_STRING, &C_EmptySubject, IP "Re: your mail" },
1065  /*
1066  ** .pp
1067  ** This variable specifies the subject to be used when replying to an email
1068  ** with an empty subject. It defaults to "Re: your mail".
1069  */
1070  { "encode_from", DT_BOOL, &C_EncodeFrom, false },
1071  /*
1072  ** .pp
1073  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will quoted-printable encode messages when
1074  ** they contain the string "From " (note the trailing space) in the beginning of a line.
1075  ** This is useful to avoid the tampering certain mail delivery and transport
1076  ** agents tend to do with messages (in order to prevent tools from
1077  ** misinterpreting the line as a mbox message separator).
1078  */
1079 #ifdef USE_SSL_OPENSSL
1080  { "entropy_file", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_FILE, &C_EntropyFile, 0 },
1081  /*
1082  ** .pp
1083  ** The file which includes random data that is used to initialize SSL
1084  ** library functions.
1085  */
1086 #endif
1087  { "envelope_from_address", DT_ADDRESS, &C_EnvelopeFromAddress, 0 },
1088  /*
1089  ** .pp
1090  ** Manually sets the \fIenvelope\fP sender for outgoing messages.
1091  ** This value is ignored if $$use_envelope_from is \fIunset\fP.
1092  */
1093  { "escape", DT_STRING, &C_Escape, IP "~" },
1094  /*
1095  ** .pp
1096  ** Escape character to use for functions in the built-in editor.
1097  */
1098  { "external_search_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_ExternalSearchCommand, 0 },
1099  /*
1100  ** .pp
1101  ** If set, contains the name of the external program used by "~I" patterns.
1102  ** This will usually be a wrapper script around mairix, mu, or similar
1103  ** indexers other than notmuch (for which there is optional special support).
1104  ** .pp
1105  ** Here is an example how it works. Let's assume $$external_search_command
1106  ** is set to "mairix_filter", and mairix_filter is a script which
1107  ** runs the old but well loved mairix indexer with the arguments
1108  ** given to mairix_filter, in the "raw" mode of mairix, producing
1109  ** on the standard output a list of Message-IDs, one per line.
1110  ** .pp
1111  ** If possible, it also filters down the results coming from mairix
1112  ** such that only messages in the current folder remain. It can do
1113  ** this because it gets a hidden first argument which is the path
1114  ** to the folder.
1115  ** (This can be the type of clean and simple script called a \fIone-liner\fP.)
1116  ** .pp
1117  ** Now if NeoMutt gets a limit or tag command followed by the pattern
1118  ** "~I '-t s:bleeping='", mairix_filter runs mairix with the
1119  ** arguments from inside the quotes (the quotes are needed because
1120  ** of the space after "-t"), mairix finds all messages with
1121  ** "bleeping" in the Subject plus all messages sharing threads
1122  ** with these and outputs their file names, and mairix_filter
1123  ** translates the file names into Message-IDs. Finally, NeoMutt
1124  ** reads the Message-IDs and targets the matching messages with the
1125  ** command given to it.
1126  ** .pp
1127  ** You, the user, still have to rewrite the mairix_filter script to
1128  ** match the behavior of your indexer, but this should help users
1129  ** of indexers other than notmuch to integrate them cleanly with NeoMutt.
1130  */
1131  { "fast_reply", DT_BOOL, &C_FastReply, false },
1132  /*
1133  ** .pp
1134  ** When \fIset\fP, the initial prompt for recipients and subject are skipped
1135  ** when replying to messages, and the initial prompt for subject is
1136  ** skipped when forwarding messages.
1137  ** .pp
1138  ** \fBNote:\fP this variable has no effect when the $$autoedit
1139  ** variable is \fIset\fP.
1140  */
1141  { "fcc_attach", DT_QUAD, &C_FccAttach, MUTT_YES },
1142  /*
1143  ** .pp
1144  ** This variable controls whether or not attachments on outgoing messages
1145  ** are saved along with the main body of your message.
1146  */
1147  { "fcc_before_send", DT_BOOL, &C_FccBeforeSend, false },
1148  /*
1149  ** .pp
1150  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP, FCCs will occur before sending
1151  ** the message. Before sending, the message cannot be manipulated,
1152  ** so it will be stored the exact same as sent:
1153  ** $$fcc_attach and $$fcc_clear will be ignored (using their default
1154  ** values).
1155  ** .pp
1156  ** When \fIunset\fP, the default, FCCs will occur after sending.
1157  ** Variables $$fcc_attach and $$fcc_clear will be respected, allowing
1158  ** it to be stored without attachments or encryption/signing if
1159  ** desired.
1160  */
1161  { "fcc_clear", DT_BOOL, &C_FccClear, false },
1162  /*
1163  ** .pp
1164  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP, FCCs will be stored unencrypted and
1165  ** unsigned, even when the actual message is encrypted and/or
1166  ** signed.
1167  ** (PGP only)
1168  */
1169  { "flag_chars", DT_MBTABLE|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_FlagChars, IP "*!DdrONon- " },
1170  /*
1171  ** .pp
1172  ** Controls the characters used in several flags.
1173  ** .dl
1174  ** .dt \fBCharacter\fP .dd \fBDefault\fP .dd \fBDescription\fP
1175  ** .dt 1 .dd * .dd The mail is tagged.
1176  ** .dt 2 .dd ! .dd The mail is flagged as important.
1177  ** .dt 3 .dd D .dd The mail is marked for deletion.
1178  ** .dt 4 .dd d .dd The mail has attachments marked for deletion.
1179  ** .dt 5 .dd r .dd The mail has been replied to.
1180  ** .dt 6 .dd O .dd The mail is Old (Unread but seen).
1181  ** .dt 7 .dd N .dd The mail is New (Unread but not seen).
1182  ** .dt 8 .dd o .dd The mail thread is Old (Unread but seen).
1183  ** .dt 9 .dd n .dd The mail thread is New (Unread but not seen).
1184  ** .dt 10 .dd - .dd The mail is read - %S expando.
1185  ** .dt 11 .dd <space> .dd The mail is read - %Z expando.
1186  ** .de
1187  */
1188  { "flag_safe", DT_BOOL, &C_FlagSafe, false },
1189  /*
1190  ** .pp
1191  ** If set, flagged messages can't be deleted.
1192  */
1193  { "folder", DT_STRING|DT_MAILBOX, &C_Folder, IP "~/Mail" },
1194  /*
1195  ** .pp
1196  ** Specifies the default location of your mailboxes. A "+" or "=" at the
1197  ** beginning of a pathname will be expanded to the value of this
1198  ** variable. Note that if you change this variable (from the default)
1199  ** value you need to make sure that the assignment occurs \fIbefore\fP
1200  ** you use "+" or "=" for any other variables since expansion takes place
1201  ** when handling the "$mailboxes" command.
1202  */
1203  { "folder_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY|R_MENU, &C_FolderFormat, IP "%2C %t %N %F %2l %-8.8u %-8.8g %8s %d %i" },
1204  /*
1205  ** .pp
1206  ** This variable allows you to customize the file browser display to your
1207  ** personal taste. This string is similar to $$index_format, but has
1208  ** its own set of \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences:
1209  ** .dl
1210  ** .dt %C .dd .dd Current file number
1211  ** .dt %d .dd .dd Date/time folder was last modified
1212  ** .dt %D .dd .dd Date/time folder was last modified using $$date_format.
1213  ** .dt %f .dd .dd Filename ("/" is appended to directory names,
1214  ** "@" to symbolic links and "*" to executable files)
1215  ** .dt %F .dd .dd File permissions
1216  ** .dt %g .dd .dd Group name (or numeric gid, if missing)
1217  ** .dt %i .dd .dd Description of the folder
1218  ** .dt %l .dd .dd Number of hard links
1219  ** .dt %m .dd * .dd Number of messages in the mailbox
1220  ** .dt %n .dd * .dd Number of unread messages in the mailbox
1221  ** .dt %N .dd .dd "N" if mailbox has new mail, blank otherwise
1222  ** .dt %s .dd .dd Size in bytes (see $formatstrings-size)
1223  ** .dt %t .dd .dd "*" if the file is tagged, blank otherwise
1224  ** .dt %u .dd .dd Owner name (or numeric uid, if missing)
1225  ** .dt %>X .dd .dd Right justify the rest of the string and pad with character "X"
1226  ** .dt %|X .dd .dd Pad to the end of the line with character "X"
1227  ** .dt %*X .dd .dd Soft-fill with character "X" as pad
1228  ** .de
1229  ** .pp
1230  ** For an explanation of "soft-fill", see the $$index_format documentation.
1231  ** .pp
1232  ** * = can be optionally printed if nonzero
1233  ** .pp
1234  ** %m, %n, and %N only work for monitored mailboxes.
1235  ** %m requires $$mail_check_stats to be set.
1236  ** %n requires $$mail_check_stats to be set (except for IMAP mailboxes).
1237  */
1238  { "followup_to", DT_BOOL, &C_FollowupTo, true },
1239  /*
1240  ** .pp
1241  ** Controls whether or not the "Mail-Followup-To:" header field is
1242  ** generated when sending mail. When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will generate this
1243  ** field when you are replying to a known mailing list, specified with
1244  ** the "$subscribe" or "$lists" commands.
1245  ** .pp
1246  ** This field has two purposes. First, preventing you from
1247  ** receiving duplicate copies of replies to messages which you send
1248  ** to mailing lists, and second, ensuring that you do get a reply
1249  ** separately for any messages sent to known lists to which you are
1250  ** not subscribed.
1251  ** .pp
1252  ** The header will contain only the list's address
1253  ** for subscribed lists, and both the list address and your own
1254  ** email address for unsubscribed lists. Without this header, a
1255  ** group reply to your message sent to a subscribed list will be
1256  ** sent to both the list and your address, resulting in two copies
1257  ** of the same email for you.
1258  */
1259 #ifdef USE_NNTP
1260  { "followup_to_poster", DT_QUAD, &C_FollowupToPoster, MUTT_ASKYES },
1261  /*
1262  ** .pp
1263  ** If this variable is \fIset\fP and the keyword "poster" is present in
1264  ** \fIFollowup-To\fP header, follow-up to newsgroup function is not
1265  ** permitted. The message will be mailed to the submitter of the
1266  ** message via mail.
1267  */
1268 #endif
1269  { "force_name", DT_BOOL, &C_ForceName, false },
1270  /*
1271  ** .pp
1272  ** This variable is similar to $$save_name, except that NeoMutt will
1273  ** store a copy of your outgoing message by the username of the address
1274  ** you are sending to even if that mailbox does not exist.
1275  ** .pp
1276  ** Also see the $$record variable.
1277  */
1278  { "forward_attachments", DT_QUAD, &C_ForwardAttachments, MUTT_ASKYES },
1279  /*
1280  ** .pp
1281  ** When forwarding inline (i.e. $$mime_forward \fIunset\fP or
1282  ** answered with ``no'' and $$forward_decode \fIset\fP), attachments
1283  ** which cannot be decoded in a reasonable manner will be attached
1284  ** to the newly composed message if this quadoption is \fIset\fP or
1285  ** answered with ``yes''.
1286  */
1287  { "forward_attribution_intro", DT_STRING, &C_ForwardAttributionIntro, IP "----- Forwarded message from %f -----" },
1288  /*
1289  ** .pp
1290  ** This is the string that will precede a message which has been forwarded
1291  ** in the main body of a message (when $$mime_forward is unset).
1292  ** For a full listing of defined \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences see
1293  ** the section on $$index_format. See also $$attribution_locale.
1294  */
1295  { "forward_attribution_trailer", DT_STRING, &C_ForwardAttributionTrailer, IP "----- End forwarded message -----" },
1296  /*
1297  ** .pp
1298  ** This is the string that will follow a message which has been forwarded
1299  ** in the main body of a message (when $$mime_forward is unset).
1300  ** For a full listing of defined \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences see
1301  ** the section on $$index_format. See also $$attribution_locale.
1302  */
1303  { "forward_decode", DT_BOOL, &C_ForwardDecode, true },
1304  /*
1305  ** .pp
1306  ** Controls the decoding of complex MIME messages into \fCtext/plain\fP when
1307  ** forwarding a message. The message header is also RFC2047 decoded.
1308  ** This variable is only used, if $$mime_forward is \fIunset\fP,
1309  ** otherwise $$mime_forward_decode is used instead.
1310  */
1311  { "forward_decrypt", DT_BOOL, &C_ForwardDecrypt, true },
1312  /*
1313  ** .pp
1314  ** Controls the handling of encrypted messages when forwarding a message.
1315  ** When \fIset\fP, the outer layer of encryption is stripped off. This
1316  ** variable is only used if $$mime_forward is \fIset\fP and
1317  ** $$mime_forward_decode is \fIunset\fP.
1318  ** (PGP only)
1319  */
1320  { "forward_edit", DT_QUAD, &C_ForwardEdit, MUTT_YES },
1321  /*
1322  ** .pp
1323  ** This quadoption controls whether or not the user is automatically
1324  ** placed in the editor when forwarding messages. For those who always want
1325  ** to forward with no modification, use a setting of "no".
1326  */
1327  { "forward_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_ForwardFormat, IP "[%a: %s]" },
1328  /*
1329  ** .pp
1330  ** This variable controls the default subject when forwarding a message.
1331  ** It uses the same format sequences as the $$index_format variable.
1332  */
1333  { "forward_quote", DT_BOOL, &C_ForwardQuote, false },
1334  /*
1335  ** .pp
1336  ** When \fIset\fP, forwarded messages included in the main body of the
1337  ** message (when $$mime_forward is \fIunset\fP) will be quoted using
1338  ** $$indent_string.
1339  */
1340  { "forward_references", DT_BOOL, &C_ForwardReferences, false },
1341  /*
1342  ** .pp
1343  ** When \fIset\fP, forwarded messages set the "In-Reply-To:" and
1344  ** "References:" headers in the same way as normal replies would. Hence the
1345  ** forwarded message becomes part of the original thread instead of starting
1346  ** a new one.
1347  */
1348  { "from", DT_ADDRESS, &C_From, 0 },
1349  /*
1350  ** .pp
1351  ** When \fIset\fP, this variable contains a default "from" address. It
1352  ** can be overridden using "$my_hdr" (including from a "$send-hook") and
1353  ** $$reverse_name. This variable is ignored if $$use_from is \fIunset\fP.
1354  ** .pp
1355  ** If not specified, then it may be read from the environment variable \fC$$$EMAIL\fP.
1356  */
1357  { "from_chars", DT_MBTABLE|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_FromChars, 0 },
1358  /*
1359  ** .pp
1360  ** Controls the character used to prefix the %F and %L fields in the
1361  ** index.
1362  ** .dl
1363  ** .dt \fBCharacter\fP .dd \fBDescription\fP
1364  ** .dt 1 .dd Mail is written by you and has a To address, or has a known mailing list in the To address.
1365  ** .dt 2 .dd Mail is written by you and has a Cc address, or has a known mailing list in the Cc address.
1366  ** .dt 3 .dd Mail is written by you and has a Bcc address.
1367  ** .dt 4 .dd All remaining cases.
1368  ** .de
1369  ** .pp
1370  ** If this is empty or unset (default), the traditional long "To ",
1371  ** "Cc " and "Bcc " prefixes are used. If set but too short to
1372  ** include a character for a particular case, a single space will be
1373  ** prepended to the field. To prevent any prefix at all from being
1374  ** added in a particular case, use the special value CR (aka ^M)
1375  ** for the corresponding character.
1376  ** .pp
1377  ** This slightly odd interface is necessitated by NeoMutt's handling of
1378  ** string variables; one can't tell a variable that is unset from one
1379  ** that is set to the empty string.
1380  */
1381  { "gecos_mask", DT_REGEX, &C_GecosMask, IP "^[^,]*" },
1382  /*
1383  ** .pp
1384  ** A regular expression used by NeoMutt to parse the GECOS field of a password
1385  ** entry when expanding the alias. The default value
1386  ** will return the string up to the first "," encountered.
1387  ** If the GECOS field contains a string like "lastname, firstname" then you
1388  ** should set it to "\fC.*\fP".
1389  ** .pp
1390  ** This can be useful if you see the following behavior: you address an e-mail
1391  ** to user ID "stevef" whose full name is "Steve Franklin". If NeoMutt expands
1392  ** "stevef" to '"Franklin" stevef@foo.bar' then you should set the $$gecos_mask to
1393  ** a regular expression that will match the whole name so NeoMutt will expand
1394  ** "Franklin" to "Franklin, Steve".
1395  */
1396 #ifdef USE_NNTP
1397  { "group_index_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_GroupIndexFormat, IP "%4C %M%N %5s %-45.45f %d" },
1398  /*
1399  ** .pp
1400  ** This variable allows you to customize the newsgroup browser display to
1401  ** your personal taste. This string is similar to "$index_format", but
1402  ** has its own set of printf()-like sequences:
1403  ** .dl
1404  ** .dt %C .dd Current newsgroup number
1405  ** .dt %d .dd Description of newsgroup (becomes from server)
1406  ** .dt %f .dd Newsgroup name
1407  ** .dt %M .dd - if newsgroup not allowed for direct post (moderated for example)
1408  ** .dt %N .dd N if newsgroup is new, u if unsubscribed, blank otherwise
1409  ** .dt %n .dd Number of new articles in newsgroup
1410  ** .dt %s .dd Number of unread articles in newsgroup
1411  ** .dt %>X .dd Right justify the rest of the string and pad with character "X"
1412  ** .dt %|X .dd Pad to the end of the line with character "X"
1413  ** .de
1414  */
1415 #endif
1416  { "hdrs", DT_BOOL, &C_Hdrs, true },
1417  /*
1418  ** .pp
1419  ** When \fIunset\fP, the header fields normally added by the "$my_hdr"
1420  ** command are not created. This variable \fImust\fP be unset before
1421  ** composing a new message or replying in order to take effect. If \fIset\fP,
1422  ** the user defined header fields are added to every new message.
1423  */
1424  { "header", DT_BOOL, &C_Header, false },
1425  /*
1426  ** .pp
1427  ** When \fIset\fP, this variable causes NeoMutt to include the header
1428  ** of the message you are replying to into the edit buffer.
1429  ** The $$weed setting applies.
1430  */
1431 #ifdef USE_HCACHE
1432  { "header_cache", DT_PATH, &C_HeaderCache, 0 },
1433  /*
1434  ** .pp
1435  ** This variable points to the header cache database. If the path points to
1436  ** an existing directory, NeoMutt will create a dedicated header cache
1437  ** database per folder. Otherwise, the path points to a regular file, which
1438  ** will be created as needed and used as a shared global header cache for
1439  ** all folders.
1440  ** By default it is \fIunset\fP so no header caching will be used.
1441  ** .pp
1442  ** Header caching can greatly improve speed when opening POP, IMAP
1443  ** MH or Maildir folders, see "$caching" for details.
1444  */
1445  { "header_cache_backend", DT_STRING, &C_HeaderCacheBackend, 0, 0, hcache_validator },
1446  /*
1447  ** .pp
1448  ** This variable specifies the header cache backend. By default it is
1449  ** \fIunset\fP so no header caching will be used.
1450  */
1451 #if defined(USE_HCACHE_COMPRESSION)
1452  { "header_cache_compress_level", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_HeaderCacheCompressLevel, 1, 0, compress_level_validator },
1453  /*
1454  ** .pp
1455  ** When NeoMutt is compiled with lz4, zstd or zlib, this option can be used
1456  ** to setup the compression level.
1457  */
1458  { "header_cache_compress_method", DT_STRING, &C_HeaderCacheCompressMethod, 0, 0, compress_method_validator },
1459  /*
1460  ** .pp
1461  ** When NeoMutt is compiled with lz4, zstd or zlib, the header cache backend
1462  ** can use these compression methods for compressing the cache files.
1463  ** This results in much smaller cache file sizes and may even improve speed.
1464  */
1465 #endif /* USE_HCACHE_COMPRESSION */
1466 #endif /* USE_HCACHE */
1467  { "header_color_partial", DT_BOOL|R_PAGER_FLOW, &C_HeaderColorPartial, false },
1468  /*
1469  ** .pp
1470  ** When \fIset\fP, color header regexes behave like color body regexes:
1471  ** color is applied to the exact text matched by the regex. When
1472  ** \fIunset\fP, color is applied to the entire header.
1473  ** .pp
1474  ** One use of this option might be to apply color to just the header labels.
1475  ** .pp
1476  ** See "$color" for more details.
1477  */
1478  { "help", DT_BOOL|R_REFLOW, &C_Help, true },
1479  /*
1480  ** .pp
1481  ** When \fIset\fP, help lines describing the bindings for the major functions
1482  ** provided by each menu are displayed on the first line of the screen.
1483  ** .pp
1484  ** \fBNote:\fP The binding will not be displayed correctly if the
1485  ** function is bound to a sequence rather than a single keystroke. Also,
1486  ** the help line may not be updated if a binding is changed while NeoMutt is
1487  ** running. Since this variable is primarily aimed at new users, neither
1488  ** of these should present a major problem.
1489  */
1490  { "hidden_host", DT_BOOL, &C_HiddenHost, false },
1491  /*
1492  ** .pp
1493  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will skip the host name part of $$hostname variable
1494  ** when adding the domain part to addresses. This variable does not
1495  ** affect the generation of Message-IDs, and it will not lead to the
1496  ** cut-off of first-level domains.
1497  */
1498  { "hidden_tags", DT_SLIST|SLIST_SEP_COMMA, &C_HiddenTags, IP "unread,draft,flagged,passed,replied,attachment,signed,encrypted" },
1499  /*
1500  ** .pp
1501  ** This variable specifies private notmuch/imap tags which should not be printed
1502  ** on screen.
1503  */
1504  { "hide_limited", DT_BOOL|R_TREE|R_INDEX, &C_HideLimited, false },
1505  /*
1506  ** .pp
1507  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will not show the presence of messages that are hidden
1508  ** by limiting, in the thread tree.
1509  */
1510  { "hide_missing", DT_BOOL|R_TREE|R_INDEX, &C_HideMissing, true },
1511  /*
1512  ** .pp
1513  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will not show the presence of missing messages in the
1514  ** thread tree.
1515  */
1516  { "hide_thread_subject", DT_BOOL|R_TREE|R_INDEX, &C_HideThreadSubject, true },
1517  /*
1518  ** .pp
1519  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will not show the subject of messages in the thread
1520  ** tree that have the same subject as their parent or closest previously
1521  ** displayed sibling.
1522  */
1523  { "hide_top_limited", DT_BOOL|R_TREE|R_INDEX, &C_HideTopLimited, false },
1524  /*
1525  ** .pp
1526  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will not show the presence of messages that are hidden
1527  ** by limiting, at the top of threads in the thread tree. Note that when
1528  ** $$hide_limited is \fIset\fP, this option will have no effect.
1529  */
1530  { "hide_top_missing", DT_BOOL|R_TREE|R_INDEX, &C_HideTopMissing, true },
1531  /*
1532  ** .pp
1533  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will not show the presence of missing messages at the
1534  ** top of threads in the thread tree. Note that when $$hide_missing is
1535  ** \fIset\fP, this option will have no effect.
1536  */
1537  { "history", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_History, 10 },
1538  /*
1539  ** .pp
1540  ** This variable controls the size (in number of strings remembered) of
1541  ** the string history buffer per category. The buffer is cleared each time the
1542  ** variable is set.
1543  */
1544  { "history_file", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_FILE, &C_HistoryFile, IP "~/.mutthistory" },
1545  /*
1546  ** .pp
1547  ** The file in which NeoMutt will save its history.
1548  ** .pp
1549  ** Also see $$save_history.
1550  */
1551  { "history_remove_dups", DT_BOOL, &C_HistoryRemoveDups, false },
1552  /*
1553  ** .pp
1554  ** When \fIset\fP, all of the string history will be scanned for duplicates
1555  ** when a new entry is added. Duplicate entries in the $$history_file will
1556  ** also be removed when it is periodically compacted.
1557  */
1558  { "honor_disposition", DT_BOOL, &C_HonorDisposition, false },
1559  /*
1560  ** .pp
1561  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will not display attachments with a
1562  ** disposition of "attachment" inline even if it could
1563  ** render the part to plain text. These MIME parts can only
1564  ** be viewed from the attachment menu.
1565  ** .pp
1566  ** If \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will render all MIME parts it can
1567  ** properly transform to plain text.
1568  */
1569  { "honor_followup_to", DT_QUAD, &C_HonorFollowupTo, MUTT_YES },
1570  /*
1571  ** .pp
1572  ** This variable controls whether or not a Mail-Followup-To header is
1573  ** honored when group-replying to a message.
1574  */
1575  { "hostname", DT_STRING, &C_Hostname, 0 },
1576  /*
1577  ** .pp
1578  ** Specifies the fully-qualified hostname of the system NeoMutt is running on
1579  ** containing the host's name and the DNS domain it belongs to. It is used
1580  ** as the domain part (after "@") for local email addresses as well as
1581  ** Message-Id headers.
1582  ** .pp
1583  ** If not specified in a config file, then NeoMutt will try to determine the hostname itself.
1584  ** .pp
1585  ** Optionally, NeoMutt can be compiled with a fixed domain name.
1586  ** .pp
1587  ** Also see $$use_domain and $$hidden_host.
1588  */
1589 #ifdef HAVE_LIBIDN
1590  { "idn_decode", DT_BOOL|R_MENU, &C_IdnDecode, true },
1591  /*
1592  ** .pp
1593  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will show you international domain names decoded.
1594  ** Note: You can use IDNs for addresses even if this is \fIunset\fP.
1595  ** This variable only affects decoding. (IDN only)
1596  */
1597  { "idn_encode", DT_BOOL|R_MENU, &C_IdnEncode, true },
1598  /*
1599  ** .pp
1600  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will encode international domain names using
1601  ** IDN. Unset this if your SMTP server can handle newer (RFC6531)
1602  ** UTF-8 encoded domains. (IDN only)
1603  */
1604 #endif /* HAVE_LIBIDN */
1605  { "ignore_list_reply_to", DT_BOOL, &C_IgnoreListReplyTo, false },
1606  /*
1607  ** .pp
1608  ** Affects the behavior of the \fC<reply>\fP function when replying to
1609  ** messages from mailing lists (as defined by the "$subscribe" or
1610  ** "$lists" commands). When \fIset\fP, if the "Reply-To:" field is
1611  ** set to the same value as the "To:" field, NeoMutt assumes that the
1612  ** "Reply-To:" field was set by the mailing list to automate responses
1613  ** to the list, and will ignore this field. To direct a response to the
1614  ** mailing list when this option is \fIset\fP, use the \fC$<list-reply>\fP
1615  ** function; \fC<group-reply>\fP will reply to both the sender and the
1616  ** list.
1617  */
1618 #ifdef USE_IMAP
1619  { "imap_authenticators", DT_SLIST|SLIST_SEP_COLON, &C_ImapAuthenticators, 0 },
1620  /*
1621  ** .pp
1622  ** This is a colon-delimited list of authentication methods NeoMutt may
1623  ** attempt to use to log in to an IMAP server, in the order NeoMutt should
1624  ** try them. Authentication methods are either "login" or the right
1625  ** side of an IMAP "AUTH=xxx" capability string, e.g. "digest-md5", "gssapi"
1626  ** or "cram-md5". This option is case-insensitive. If it's
1627  ** \fIunset\fP (the default) NeoMutt will try all available methods,
1628  ** in order from most-secure to least-secure.
1629  ** .pp
1630  ** Example:
1631  ** .ts
1632  ** set imap_authenticators="gssapi:cram-md5:login"
1633  ** .te
1634  ** .pp
1635  ** \fBNote:\fP NeoMutt will only fall back to other authentication methods if
1636  ** the previous methods are unavailable. If a method is available but
1637  ** authentication fails, NeoMutt will not connect to the IMAP server.
1638  */
1639  { "imap_check_subscribed", DT_BOOL, &C_ImapCheckSubscribed, false },
1640  /*
1641  ** .pp
1642  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will fetch the set of subscribed folders from
1643  ** your server whenever a mailbox is \fBselected\fP, and add them to the set
1644  ** of mailboxes it polls for new mail just as if you had issued individual
1645  ** "$mailboxes" commands.
1646  */
1647  { "imap_condstore", DT_BOOL, &C_ImapCondstore, false },
1648  /*
1649  ** .pp
1650  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will use the CONDSTORE extension (RFC7162)
1651  ** if advertised by the server. NeoMutt's current implementation is basic,
1652  ** used only for initial message fetching and flag updates.
1653  ** .pp
1654  ** For some IMAP servers, enabling this will slightly speed up
1655  ** downloading initial messages. Unfortunately, Gmail is not one
1656  ** those, and displays worse performance when enabled. Your
1657  ** mileage may vary.
1658  */
1659 #ifdef USE_ZLIB
1660  { "imap_deflate", DT_BOOL, &C_ImapDeflate, true },
1661  /*
1662  ** .pp
1663  ** When \fIset\fP, mutt will use the COMPRESS=DEFLATE extension (RFC4978)
1664  ** if advertised by the server.
1665  ** .pp
1666  ** In general a good compression efficiency can be achieved, which
1667  ** speeds up reading large mailboxes also on fairly good connections.
1668  */
1669 #endif
1670  { "imap_delim_chars", DT_STRING, &C_ImapDelimChars, IP "/." },
1671  /*
1672  ** .pp
1673  ** This contains the list of characters that NeoMutt will use as folder
1674  ** separators for IMAP paths, when no separator is provided on the IMAP
1675  ** connection.
1676  */
1677  { "imap_fetch_chunk_size", DT_LONG|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_ImapFetchChunkSize, 0 },
1678  /*
1679  ** .pp
1680  ** When set to a value greater than 0, new headers will be downloaded
1681  ** in sets of this size. If you have a very large mailbox, this might
1682  ** prevent a timeout and disconnect when opening the mailbox, by sending
1683  ** a FETCH per set of this size instead of a single FETCH for all new
1684  ** headers.
1685  */
1686  { "imap_headers", DT_STRING|R_INDEX, &C_ImapHeaders, 0 },
1687  /*
1688  ** .pp
1689  ** NeoMutt requests these header fields in addition to the default headers
1690  ** ("Date:", "From:", "Sender:", "Subject:", "To:", "Cc:", "Message-Id:",
1691  ** "References:", "Content-Type:", "Content-Description:", "In-Reply-To:",
1692  ** "Reply-To:", "Lines:", "List-Post:", "X-Label:") from IMAP
1693  ** servers before displaying the index menu. You may want to add more
1694  ** headers for spam detection.
1695  ** .pp
1696  ** \fBNote:\fP This is a space separated list, items should be uppercase
1697  ** and not contain the colon, e.g. "X-BOGOSITY X-SPAM-STATUS" for the
1698  ** "X-Bogosity:" and "X-Spam-Status:" header fields.
1699  */
1700  { "imap_idle", DT_BOOL, &C_ImapIdle, false },
1701  /*
1702  ** .pp
1703  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will attempt to use the IMAP IDLE extension
1704  ** to check for new mail in the current mailbox. Some servers
1705  ** (dovecot was the inspiration for this option) react badly
1706  ** to NeoMutt's implementation. If your connection seems to freeze
1707  ** up periodically, try unsetting this.
1708  */
1709  { "imap_keepalive", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_ImapKeepalive, 300 },
1710  /*
1711  ** .pp
1712  ** This variable specifies the maximum amount of time in seconds that NeoMutt
1713  ** will wait before polling open IMAP connections, to prevent the server
1714  ** from closing them before NeoMutt has finished with them. The default is
1715  ** well within the RFC-specified minimum amount of time (30 minutes) before
1716  ** a server is allowed to do this, but in practice the RFC does get
1717  ** violated every now and then. Reduce this number if you find yourself
1718  ** getting disconnected from your IMAP server due to inactivity.
1719  */
1720  { "imap_list_subscribed", DT_BOOL, &C_ImapListSubscribed, false },
1721  /*
1722  ** .pp
1723  ** This variable configures whether IMAP folder browsing will look for
1724  ** only subscribed folders or all folders. This can be toggled in the
1725  ** IMAP browser with the \fC<toggle-subscribed>\fP function.
1726  */
1727  { "imap_login", DT_STRING|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_ImapLogin, 0 },
1728  /*
1729  ** .pp
1730  ** Your login name on the IMAP server.
1731  ** .pp
1732  ** This variable defaults to the value of $$imap_user.
1733  */
1734  { "imap_oauth_refresh_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_ImapOauthRefreshCommand, 0 },
1735  /*
1736  ** .pp
1737  ** The command to run to generate an OAUTH refresh token for
1738  ** authorizing your connection to your IMAP server. This command will be
1739  ** run on every connection attempt that uses the OAUTHBEARER authentication
1740  ** mechanism. See "$oauth" for details.
1741  */
1742  { "imap_pass", DT_STRING|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_ImapPass, 0 },
1743  /*
1744  ** .pp
1745  ** Specifies the password for your IMAP account. If \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will
1746  ** prompt you for your password when you invoke the \fC<imap-fetch-mail>\fP function
1747  ** or try to open an IMAP folder.
1748  ** .pp
1749  ** \fBWarning\fP: you should only use this option when you are on a
1750  ** fairly secure machine, because the superuser can read your neomuttrc even
1751  ** if you are the only one who can read the file.
1752  */
1753  { "imap_passive", DT_BOOL, &C_ImapPassive, true },
1754  /*
1755  ** .pp
1756  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will not open new IMAP connections to check for new
1757  ** mail. NeoMutt will only check for new mail over existing IMAP
1758  ** connections. This is useful if you don't want to be prompted for
1759  ** user/password pairs on NeoMutt invocation, or if opening the connection
1760  ** is slow.
1761  */
1762  { "imap_peek", DT_BOOL, &C_ImapPeek, true },
1763  /*
1764  ** .pp
1765  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will avoid implicitly marking your mail as read whenever
1766  ** you fetch a message from the server. This is generally a good thing,
1767  ** but can make closing an IMAP folder somewhat slower. This option
1768  ** exists to appease speed freaks.
1769  */
1770  { "imap_pipeline_depth", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_ImapPipelineDepth, 15 },
1771  /*
1772  ** .pp
1773  ** Controls the number of IMAP commands that may be queued up before they
1774  ** are sent to the server. A deeper pipeline reduces the amount of time
1775  ** NeoMutt must wait for the server, and can make IMAP servers feel much
1776  ** more responsive. But not all servers correctly handle pipelined commands,
1777  ** so if you have problems you might want to try setting this variable to 0.
1778  ** .pp
1779  ** \fBNote:\fP Changes to this variable have no effect on open connections.
1780  */
1781  { "imap_poll_timeout", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_ImapPollTimeout, 15 },
1782  /*
1783  ** .pp
1784  ** This variable specifies the maximum amount of time in seconds
1785  ** that NeoMutt will wait for a response when polling IMAP connections
1786  ** for new mail, before timing out and closing the connection. Set
1787  ** to 0 to disable timing out.
1788  */
1789  { "imap_qresync", DT_BOOL, &C_ImapQresync, false },
1790  /*
1791  ** .pp
1792  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will use the QRESYNC extension (RFC7162)
1793  ** if advertised by the server. NeoMutt's current implementation is basic,
1794  ** used only for initial message fetching and flag updates.
1795  ** .pp
1796  ** Note: this feature is currently experimental. If you experience
1797  ** strange behavior, such as duplicate or missing messages please
1798  ** file a bug report to let us know.
1799  */
1800  { "imap_rfc5161", DT_BOOL, &C_ImapRfc5161, true },
1801  /*
1802  ** .pp
1803  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will use the IMAP ENABLE extension (RFC5161) to
1804  ** select CAPABILITIES. Some servers (notably Coremail System IMap Server) do
1805  ** not properly respond to ENABLE commands, which might cause NeoMutt to hang.
1806  ** If your connection seems to freeze at login, try unsetting this. See also
1807  ** https://github.com/neomutt/neomutt/issues/1689
1808  */
1809  { "imap_servernoise", DT_BOOL, &C_ImapServernoise, true },
1810  /*
1811  ** .pp
1812  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will display warning messages from the IMAP
1813  ** server as error messages. Since these messages are often
1814  ** harmless, or generated due to configuration problems on the
1815  ** server which are out of the users' hands, you may wish to suppress
1816  ** them at some point.
1817  */
1818  { "imap_user", DT_STRING|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_ImapUser, 0 },
1819  /*
1820  ** .pp
1821  ** The name of the user whose mail you intend to access on the IMAP
1822  ** server.
1823  ** .pp
1824  ** This variable defaults to your user name on the local machine.
1825  */
1826 #endif
1827  { "implicit_autoview", DT_BOOL, &C_ImplicitAutoview, false },
1828  /*
1829  ** .pp
1830  ** If set to "yes", NeoMutt will look for a mailcap entry with the
1831  ** "\fCcopiousoutput\fP" flag set for \fIevery\fP MIME attachment it doesn't have
1832  ** an internal viewer defined for. If such an entry is found, NeoMutt will
1833  ** use the viewer defined in that entry to convert the body part to text
1834  ** form.
1835  */
1836  { "include", DT_QUAD, &C_Include, MUTT_ASKYES },
1837  /*
1838  ** .pp
1839  ** Controls whether or not a copy of the message(s) you are replying to
1840  ** is included in your reply.
1841  */
1842  { "include_encrypted", DT_BOOL, &C_IncludeEncrypted, false },
1843  /*
1844  ** .pp
1845  ** Controls whether or not NeoMutt includes separately encrypted attachment
1846  ** contents when replying.
1847  ** .pp
1848  ** This variable was added to prevent accidental exposure of encrypted
