1849  ** contents when replying to an attacker. If a previously encrypted message
1850  ** were attached by the attacker, they could trick an unwary recipient into
1851  ** decrypting and including the message in their reply.
1852  */
1853  { "include_onlyfirst", DT_BOOL, &C_IncludeOnlyfirst, false },
1854  /*
1855  ** .pp
1856  ** Controls whether or not NeoMutt includes only the first attachment
1857  ** of the message you are replying.
1858  */
1859  { "indent_string", DT_STRING, &C_IndentString, IP "> " },
1860  /*
1861  ** .pp
1862  ** Specifies the string to prepend to each line of text quoted in a
1863  ** message to which you are replying. You are strongly encouraged not to
1864  ** change this value, as it tends to agitate the more fanatical netizens.
1865  ** .pp
1866  ** The value of this option is ignored if $$text_flowed is set, because
1867  ** the quoting mechanism is strictly defined for format=flowed.
1868  ** .pp
1869  ** This option is a format string, please see the description of
1870  ** $$index_format for supported \fCprintf(3)\fP-style sequences.
1871  */
1872  { "index_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_IndexFormat, IP "%4C %Z %{%b %d} %-15.15L (%?l?%4l&%4c?) %s" },
1873  /*
1874  ** .pp
1875  ** This variable allows you to customize the message index display to
1876  ** your personal taste.
1877  ** .pp
1878  ** "Format strings" are similar to the strings used in the C
1879  ** function \fCprintf(3)\fP to format output (see the man page for more details).
1880  ** For an explanation of the %? construct, see the $status_format description.
1881  ** The following sequences are defined in NeoMutt:
1882  ** .dl
1883  ** .dt %a .dd Address of the author
1884  ** .dt %A .dd Reply-to address (if present; otherwise: address of author)
1885  ** .dt %b .dd Filename of the original message folder (think mailbox)
1886  ** .dt %B .dd The list to which the letter was sent, or else the folder name (%b).
1887  ** .dt %C .dd Current message number
1888  ** .dt %c .dd Number of characters (bytes) in the body of the message (see $formatstrings-size)
1889  ** .dt %cr .dd Number of characters (bytes) in the raw message, including the header (see $formatstrings-size)
1890  ** .dt %D .dd Date and time of message using $date_format and local timezone
1891  ** .dt %d .dd Date and time of message using $date_format and sender's timezone
1892  ** .dt %e .dd Current message number in thread
1893  ** .dt %E .dd Number of messages in current thread
1894  ** .dt %F .dd Author name, or recipient name if the message is from you
1895  ** .dt %Fp .dd Like %F, but plain. No contextual formatting is applied to recipient name
1896  ** .dt %f .dd Sender (address + real name), either From: or Return-Path:
1897  ** .dt %g .dd Newsgroup name (if compiled with NNTP support)
1898  ** .dt %g .dd Message tags (e.g. notmuch tags/imap flags)
1899  ** .dt %Gx .dd Individual message tag (e.g. notmuch tags/imap flags)
1900  ** .dt %H .dd Spam attribute(s) of this message
1901  ** .dt %I .dd Initials of author
1902  ** .dt %i .dd Message-id of the current message
1903  ** .dt %J .dd Message tags (if present, tree unfolded, and != parent's tags)
1904  ** .dt %K .dd The list to which the letter was sent (if any; otherwise: empty)
1905  ** .dt %L .dd If an address in the "To:" or "Cc:" header field matches an address
1906  ** Defined by the users "$subscribe" command, this displays
1907  ** "To <list-name>", otherwise the same as %F
1908  ** .dt %l .dd number of lines in the unprocessed message (may not work with
1909  ** maildir, mh, and IMAP folders)
1910  ** .dt %M .dd Number of hidden messages if the thread is collapsed
1911  ** .dt %m .dd Total number of message in the mailbox
1912  ** .dt %N .dd Message score
1913  ** .dt %n .dd Author's real name (or address if missing)
1914  ** .dt %O .dd Original save folder where NeoMutt would formerly have
1915  ** Stashed the message: list name or recipient name
1916  ** If not sent to a list
1917  ** .dt %P .dd Progress indicator for the built-in pager (how much of the file has been displayed)
1918  ** .dt %q .dd Newsgroup name (if compiled with NNTP support)
1919  ** .dt %R .dd Comma separated list of "Cc:" recipients
1920  ** .dt %r .dd Comma separated list of "To:" recipients
1921  ** .dt %S .dd Single character status of the message ("N"/"O"/"D"/"d"/"!"/"r"/"\(as")
1922  ** .dt %s .dd Subject of the message
1923  ** .dt %T .dd The appropriate character from the $$to_chars string
1924  ** .dt %t .dd "To:" field (recipients)
1925  ** .dt %u .dd User (login) name of the author
1926  ** .dt %v .dd First name of the author, or the recipient if the message is from you
1927  ** .dt %W .dd Name of organization of author ("Organization:" field)
1928  ** .dt %x .dd "X-Comment-To:" field (if present and compiled with NNTP support)
1929  ** .dt %X .dd Number of MIME attachments
1930  ** (please see the "$attachments" section for possible speed effects)
1931  ** .dt %Y .dd "X-Label:" field, if present, and \fI(1)\fP not at part of a thread tree,
1932  ** \fI(2)\fP at the top of a thread, or \fI(3)\fP "X-Label:" is different from
1933  ** Preceding message's "X-Label:"
1934  ** .dt %y .dd "X-Label:" field, if present
1935  ** .dt %Z .dd A three character set of message status flags.
1936  ** The first character is new/read/replied flags ("n"/"o"/"r"/"O"/"N").
1937  ** The second is deleted or encryption flags ("D"/"d"/"S"/"P"/"s"/"K").
1938  ** The third is either tagged/flagged ("\(as"/"!"), or one of the characters
1939  ** Listed in $$to_chars.
1940  ** .dt %zc .dd Message crypto flags
1941  ** .dt %zs .dd Message status flags
1942  ** .dt %zt .dd Message tag flags
1943  ** .dt %@name@ .dd insert and evaluate format-string from the matching
1944  ** ``$index-format-hook'' command
1945  ** .dt %{fmt} .dd the date and time of the message is converted to sender's
1946  ** time zone, and "fmt" is expanded by the library function
1947  ** \fCstrftime(3)\fP; a leading bang disables locales
1948  ** .dt %[fmt] .dd the date and time of the message is converted to the local
1949  ** time zone, and "fmt" is expanded by the library function
1950  ** \fCstrftime(3)\fP; a leading bang disables locales
1951  ** .dt %(fmt) .dd the local date and time when the message was received.
1952  ** "fmt" is expanded by the library function \fCstrftime(3)\fP;
1953  ** a leading bang disables locales
1954  ** .dt %>X .dd right justify the rest of the string and pad with character "X"
1955  ** .dt %|X .dd pad to the end of the line with character "X"
1956  ** .dt %*X .dd soft-fill with character "X" as pad
1957  ** .de
1958  ** .pp
1959  ** Date format expressions can be constructed based on relative dates. Using
1960  ** the date formatting operators along with nested conditionals, the date
1961  ** format can be modified based on how old a message is. See the section on
1962  ** "Conditional Dates" for an explanation and examples
1963  ** .pp
1964  ** Note that for mbox/mmdf, ``%l'' applies to the unprocessed message, and
1965  ** for maildir/mh, the value comes from the ``Lines:'' header field when
1966  ** present (the meaning is normally the same). Thus the value depends on
1967  ** the encodings used in the different parts of the message and has little
1968  ** meaning in practice.
1969  ** .pp
1970  ** "Soft-fill" deserves some explanation: Normal right-justification
1971  ** will print everything to the left of the "%>", displaying padding and
1972  ** whatever lies to the right only if there's room. By contrast,
1973  ** soft-fill gives priority to the right-hand side, guaranteeing space
1974  ** to display it and showing padding only if there's still room. If
1975  ** necessary, soft-fill will eat text leftwards to make room for
1976  ** rightward text.
1977  ** .pp
1978  ** Note that these expandos are supported in
1979  ** ``$save-hook'', ``$fcc-hook'' and ``$fcc-save-hook'', too.
1980  */
1981 #ifdef USE_NNTP
1982  { "inews", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_Inews, 0 },
1983  /*
1984  ** .pp
1985  ** If set, specifies the program and arguments used to deliver news posted
1986  ** by NeoMutt. Otherwise, NeoMutt posts article using current connection to
1987  ** news server. The following printf-style sequence is understood:
1988  ** .dl
1989  ** .dt %a .dd account url
1990  ** .dt %p .dd port
1991  ** .dt %P .dd port if specified
1992  ** .dt %s .dd news server name
1993  ** .dt %S .dd url schema
1994  ** .dt %u .dd username
1995  ** .de
1996  ** .pp
1997  ** Example:
1998  ** .ts
1999  ** set inews="/usr/local/bin/inews -hS"
2000  ** .te
2001  */
2002 #endif
2003  { "ispell", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_Ispell, IP ISPELL },
2004  /*
2005  ** .pp
2006  ** How to invoke ispell (GNU's spell-checking software).
2007  */
2008  { "keep_flagged", DT_BOOL, &C_KeepFlagged, false },
2009  /*
2010  ** .pp
2011  ** If \fIset\fP, read messages marked as flagged will not be moved
2012  ** from your spool mailbox to your $$mbox mailbox, or as a result of
2013  ** a "$mbox-hook" command.
2014  */
2015  { "mail_check", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_MailCheck, 5 },
2016  /*
2017  ** .pp
2018  ** This variable configures how often (in seconds) NeoMutt should look for
2019  ** new mail. Also see the $$timeout variable.
2020  */
2021  { "mail_check_recent", DT_BOOL, &C_MailCheckRecent, true },
2022  /*
2023  ** .pp
2024  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will only notify you about new mail that has been received
2025  ** since the last time you opened the mailbox. When \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will notify you
2026  ** if any new mail exists in the mailbox, regardless of whether you have visited it
2027  ** recently.
2028  ** .pp
2029  ** When \fI$$mark_old\fP is set, NeoMutt does not consider the mailbox to contain new
2030  ** mail if only old messages exist.
2031  */
2032  { "mail_check_stats", DT_BOOL, &C_MailCheckStats, false },
2033  /*
2034  ** .pp
2035  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will periodically calculate message
2036  ** statistics of a mailbox while polling for new mail. It will
2037  ** check for unread, flagged, and total message counts. Because
2038  ** this operation is more performance intensive, it defaults to
2039  ** \fIunset\fP, and has a separate option, $$mail_check_stats_interval, to
2040  ** control how often to update these counts.
2041  ** .pp
2042  ** Message statistics can also be explicitly calculated by invoking the
2043  ** \fC<check-stats>\fP function.
2044  */
2045  { "mail_check_stats_interval", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_MailCheckStatsInterval, 60 },
2046  /*
2047  ** .pp
2048  ** When $$mail_check_stats is \fIset\fP, this variable configures
2049  ** how often (in seconds) NeoMutt will update message counts.
2050  */
2051  { "mailcap_path", DT_SLIST|SLIST_SEP_COLON, &C_MailcapPath, IP "~/.mailcap:" PKGDATADIR "/mailcap:" SYSCONFDIR "/mailcap:/etc/mailcap:/usr/etc/mailcap:/usr/local/etc/mailcap" },
2052  /*
2053  ** .pp
2054  ** This variable specifies which files to consult when attempting to
2055  ** display MIME bodies not directly supported by NeoMutt. The default value
2056  ** is generated during startup: see the ``$mailcap'' section of the manual.
2057  ** .pp
2058  ** $$mailcap_path is overridden by the environment variable \fC$$$MAILCAPS\fP.
2059  ** .pp
2060  ** The default search path is from RFC1524.
2061  */
2062  { "mailcap_sanitize", DT_BOOL, &C_MailcapSanitize, true },
2063  /*
2064  ** .pp
2065  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will restrict possible characters in mailcap % expandos
2066  ** to a well-defined set of safe characters. This is the safe setting,
2067  ** but we are not sure it doesn't break some more advanced MIME stuff.
2068  ** .pp
2069  ** \fBDON'T CHANGE THIS SETTING UNLESS YOU ARE REALLY SURE WHAT YOU ARE
2070  ** DOING!\fP
2071  */
2072  { "maildir_check_cur", DT_BOOL, &C_MaildirCheckCur, false },
2073  /*
2074  ** .pp
2075  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will poll both the new and cur directories of
2076  ** a maildir folder for new messages. This might be useful if other
2077  ** programs interacting with the folder (e.g. dovecot) are moving new
2078  ** messages to the cur directory. Note that setting this option may
2079  ** slow down polling for new messages in large folders, since NeoMutt has
2080  ** to scan all cur messages.
2081  */
2082 #ifdef USE_HCACHE
2083  { "maildir_header_cache_verify", DT_BOOL, &C_MaildirHeaderCacheVerify, true },
2084  /*
2085  ** .pp
2086  ** Check for Maildir unaware programs other than NeoMutt having modified maildir
2087  ** files when the header cache is in use. This incurs one \fCstat(2)\fP per
2088  ** message every time the folder is opened (which can be very slow for NFS
2089  ** folders).
2090  */
2091 #endif
2092  { "maildir_trash", DT_BOOL, &C_MaildirTrash, false },
2093  /*
2094  ** .pp
2095  ** If \fIset\fP, messages marked as deleted will be saved with the maildir
2096  ** trashed flag instead of unlinked. \fBNote:\fP this only applies
2097  ** to maildir-style mailboxes. Setting it will have no effect on other
2098  ** mailbox types.
2099  */
2100  { "mark_macro_prefix", DT_STRING, &C_MarkMacroPrefix, IP "'" },
2101  /*
2102  ** .pp
2103  ** Prefix for macros created using mark-message. A new macro
2104  ** automatically generated with \fI<mark-message>a\fP will be composed
2105  ** from this prefix and the letter \fIa\fP.
2106  */
2107  { "mark_old", DT_BOOL|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_MarkOld, true },
2108  /*
2109  ** .pp
2110  ** Controls whether or not NeoMutt marks \fInew\fP \fBunread\fP
2111  ** messages as \fIold\fP if you exit a mailbox without reading them.
2112  ** With this option \fIset\fP, the next time you start NeoMutt, the messages
2113  ** will show up with an "O" next to them in the index menu,
2114  ** indicating that they are old.
2115  */
2116  { "markers", DT_BOOL|R_PAGER_FLOW, &C_Markers, true },
2117  /*
2118  ** .pp
2119  ** Controls the display of wrapped lines in the internal pager. If set, a
2120  ** "+" marker is displayed at the beginning of wrapped lines.
2121  ** .pp
2122  ** Also see the $$smart_wrap variable.
2123  */
2124  { "mask", DT_REGEX|DT_REGEX_MATCH_CASE|DT_REGEX_ALLOW_NOT|DT_REGEX_NOSUB, &C_Mask, IP "!^\\.[^.]" },
2125  /*
2126  ** .pp
2127  ** A regular expression used in the file browser, optionally preceded by
2128  ** the \fInot\fP operator "!". Only files whose names match this mask
2129  ** will be shown. The match is always case-sensitive.
2130  */
2131  { "mbox", DT_STRING|DT_MAILBOX|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_Mbox, IP "~/mbox" },
2132  /*
2133  ** .pp
2134  ** This specifies the folder into which read mail in your $$spoolfile
2135  ** folder will be appended.
2136  ** .pp
2137  ** Also see the $$move variable.
2138  */
2139  { "mbox_type", DT_ENUM, &C_MboxType, MUTT_MBOX, IP &MboxTypeDef },
2140  /*
2141  ** .pp
2142  ** The default mailbox type used when creating new folders. May be any of
2143  ** "mbox", "MMDF", "MH" or "Maildir".
2144  ** .pp
2145  ** This can also be set using the \fC-m\fP command-line option.
2146  */
2147  { "menu_context", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_MenuContext, 0 },
2148  /*
2149  ** .pp
2150  ** This variable controls the number of lines of context that are given
2151  ** when scrolling through menus. (Similar to $$pager_context.)
2152  */
2153  { "menu_move_off", DT_BOOL, &C_MenuMoveOff, true },
2154  /*
2155  ** .pp
2156  ** When \fIunset\fP, the bottom entry of menus will never scroll up past
2157  ** the bottom of the screen, unless there are less entries than lines.
2158  ** When \fIset\fP, the bottom entry may move off the bottom.
2159  */
2160  { "menu_scroll", DT_BOOL, &C_MenuScroll, false },
2161  /*
2162  ** .pp
2163  ** When \fIset\fP, menus will be scrolled up or down one line when you
2164  ** attempt to move across a screen boundary. If \fIunset\fP, the screen
2165  ** is cleared and the next or previous page of the menu is displayed
2166  ** (useful for slow links to avoid many redraws).
2167  */
2168 #if defined(USE_IMAP) || defined(USE_POP)
2169  { "message_cache_clean", DT_BOOL, &C_MessageCacheClean, false },
2170  /*
2171  ** .pp
2172  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will clean out obsolete entries from the message cache when
2173  ** the mailbox is synchronized. You probably only want to set it
2174  ** every once in a while, since it can be a little slow
2175  ** (especially for large folders).
2176  */
2177  { "message_cachedir", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_DIR, &C_MessageCachedir, 0 },
2178  /*
2179  ** .pp
2180  ** Set this to a directory and NeoMutt will cache copies of messages from
2181  ** your IMAP and POP servers here. You are free to remove entries at any
2182  ** time.
2183  ** .pp
2184  ** When setting this variable to a directory, NeoMutt needs to fetch every
2185  ** remote message only once and can perform regular expression searches
2186  ** as fast as for local folders.
2187  ** .pp
2188  ** Also see the $$message_cache_clean variable.
2189  */
2190 #endif
2191  { "message_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_MessageFormat, IP "%s" },
2192  /*
2193  ** .pp
2194  ** This is the string displayed in the "attachment" menu for
2195  ** attachments of type \fCmessage/rfc822\fP. For a full listing of defined
2196  ** \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences see the section on $$index_format.
2197  */
2198  { "meta_key", DT_BOOL, &C_MetaKey, false },
2199  /*
2200  ** .pp
2201  ** If \fIset\fP, forces NeoMutt to interpret keystrokes with the high bit (bit 8)
2202  ** set as if the user had pressed the Esc key and whatever key remains
2203  ** after having the high bit removed. For example, if the key pressed
2204  ** has an ASCII value of \fC0xf8\fP, then this is treated as if the user had
2205  ** pressed Esc then "x". This is because the result of removing the
2206  ** high bit from \fC0xf8\fP is \fC0x78\fP, which is the ASCII character
2207  ** "x".
2208  */
2209  { "metoo", DT_BOOL, &C_Metoo, false },
2210  /*
2211  ** .pp
2212  ** If \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will remove your address (see the "$alternates"
2213  ** command) from the list of recipients when replying to a message.
2214  */
2215  { "mh_purge", DT_BOOL, &C_MhPurge, false },
2216  /*
2217  ** .pp
2218  ** When \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will mimic mh's behavior and rename deleted messages
2219  ** to \fI,<old file name>\fP in mh folders instead of really deleting
2220  ** them. This leaves the message on disk but makes programs reading the folder
2221  ** ignore it. If the variable is \fIset\fP, the message files will simply be
2222  ** deleted.
2223  ** .pp
2224  ** This option is similar to $$maildir_trash for Maildir folders.
2225  */
2226  { "mh_seq_flagged", DT_STRING, &C_MhSeqFlagged, IP "flagged" },
2227  /*
2228  ** .pp
2229  ** The name of the MH sequence used for flagged messages.
2230  */
2231  { "mh_seq_replied", DT_STRING, &C_MhSeqReplied, IP "replied" },
2232  /*
2233  ** .pp
2234  ** The name of the MH sequence used to tag replied messages.
2235  */
2236  { "mh_seq_unseen", DT_STRING, &C_MhSeqUnseen, IP "unseen" },
2237  /*
2238  ** .pp
2239  ** The name of the MH sequence used for unseen messages.
2240  */
2241  { "mime_forward", DT_QUAD, &C_MimeForward, MUTT_NO },
2242  /*
2243  ** .pp
2244  ** When \fIset\fP, the message you are forwarding will be attached as a
2245  ** separate \fCmessage/rfc822\fP MIME part instead of included in the main body of the
2246  ** message. This is useful for forwarding MIME messages so the receiver
2247  ** can properly view the message as it was delivered to you. If you like
2248  ** to switch between MIME and not MIME from mail to mail, set this
2249  ** variable to "ask-no" or "ask-yes".
2250  ** .pp
2251  ** Also see $$forward_decode and $$mime_forward_decode.
2252  */
2253  { "mime_forward_decode", DT_BOOL, &C_MimeForwardDecode, false },
2254  /*
2255  ** .pp
2256  ** Controls the decoding of complex MIME messages into \fCtext/plain\fP when
2257  ** forwarding a message while $$mime_forward is \fIset\fP. Otherwise
2258  ** $$forward_decode is used instead.
2259  */
2260  { "mime_forward_rest", DT_QUAD, &C_MimeForwardRest, MUTT_YES },
2261  /*
2262  ** .pp
2263  ** When forwarding multiple attachments of a MIME message from the attachment
2264  ** menu, attachments which can't be decoded in a reasonable manner will
2265  ** be attached to the newly composed message if this option is \fIset\fP.
2266  */
2267 #ifdef USE_NNTP
2268  { "mime_subject", DT_BOOL, &C_MimeSubject, true },
2269  /*
2270  ** .pp
2271  ** If \fIunset\fP, 8-bit "subject:" line in article header will not be
2272  ** encoded according to RFC2047 to base64. This is useful when message
2273  ** is Usenet article, because MIME for news is nonstandard feature.
2274  */
2275 #endif
2276  { "mime_type_query_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_MimeTypeQueryCommand, 0 },
2277  /*
2278  ** .pp
2279  ** This specifies a command to run, to determine the mime type of a
2280  ** new attachment when composing a message. Unless
2281  ** $$mime_type_query_first is set, this will only be run if the
2282  ** attachment's extension is not found in the mime.types file.
2283  ** .pp
2284  ** The string may contain a "%s", which will be substituted with the
2285  ** attachment filename. NeoMutt will add quotes around the string substituted
2286  ** for "%s" automatically according to shell quoting rules, so you should
2287  ** avoid adding your own. If no "%s" is found in the string, NeoMutt will
2288  ** append the attachment filename to the end of the string.
2289  ** .pp
2290  ** The command should output a single line containing the
2291  ** attachment's mime type.
2292  ** .pp
2293  ** Suggested values are "xdg-mime query filetype" or
2294  ** "file -bi".
2295  */
2296  { "mime_type_query_first", DT_BOOL, &C_MimeTypeQueryFirst, false },
2297  /*
2298  ** .pp
2299  ** When \fIset\fP, the $$mime_type_query_command will be run before the
2300  ** mime.types lookup.
2301  */
2302 #ifdef MIXMASTER
2303  { "mix_entry_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_MixEntryFormat, IP "%4n %c %-16s %a" },
2304  /*
2305  ** .pp
2306  ** This variable describes the format of a remailer line on the mixmaster
2307  ** chain selection screen. The following \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences are
2308  ** supported:
2309  ** .dl
2310  ** .dt %a .dd The remailer's e-mail address
2311  ** .dt %c .dd Remailer capabilities
2312  ** .dt %n .dd The running number on the menu
2313  ** .dt %s .dd The remailer's short name
2314  ** .dt %>X .dd right justify the rest of the string and pad with character "X"
2315  ** .dt %|X .dd pad to the end of the line with character "X"
2316  ** .dt %*X .dd soft-fill with character "X" as pad
2317  ** .de
2318  */
2319  { "mixmaster", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_Mixmaster, IP MIXMASTER },
2320  /*
2321  ** .pp
2322  ** This variable contains the path to the Mixmaster binary on your
2323  ** system. It is used with various sets of parameters to gather the
2324  ** list of known remailers, and to finally send a message through the
2325  ** mixmaster chain.
2326  */
2327 #endif
2328  { "move", DT_QUAD, &C_Move, MUTT_NO },
2329  /*
2330  ** .pp
2331  ** Controls whether or not NeoMutt will move read messages
2332  ** from your spool mailbox to your $$mbox mailbox, or as a result of
2333  ** a "$mbox-hook" command.
2334  */
2335  { "narrow_tree", DT_BOOL|R_TREE|R_INDEX, &C_NarrowTree, false },
2336  /*
2337  ** .pp
2338  ** This variable, when \fIset\fP, makes the thread tree narrower, allowing
2339  ** deeper threads to fit on the screen.
2340  */
2341 #ifdef USE_SOCKET
2342  { "net_inc", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_NetInc, 10 },
2343  /*
2344  ** .pp
2345  ** Operations that expect to transfer a large amount of data over the
2346  ** network will update their progress every $$net_inc kilobytes.
2347  ** If set to 0, no progress messages will be displayed.
2348  ** .pp
2349  ** See also $$read_inc, $$write_inc and $$net_inc.
2350  */
2351 #endif
2352  { "new_mail_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_NewMailCommand, 0 },
2353  /*
2354  ** .pp
2355  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will call this command after a new message is received.
2356  ** See the $$status_format documentation for the values that can be formatted
2357  ** into this command.
2358  */
2359 #ifdef USE_NNTP
2360  { "news_cache_dir", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_DIR, &C_NewsCacheDir, IP "~/.neomutt" },
2361  /*
2362  ** .pp
2363  ** This variable pointing to directory where NeoMutt will save cached news
2364  ** articles and headers in. If \fIunset\fP, articles and headers will not be
2365  ** saved at all and will be reloaded from the server each time.
2366  */
2367  { "news_server", DT_STRING, &C_NewsServer, 0 },
2368  /*
2369  ** .pp
2370  ** This variable specifies domain name or address of NNTP server.
2371  ** .pp
2372  ** You can also specify username and an alternative port for each news server,
2373  ** e.g. \fC[[s]news://][username[:password]@]server[:port]\fP
2374  ** .pp
2375  ** This option can also be set using the command line option "-g", the
2376  ** environment variable \fC$$$NNTPSERVER\fP, or putting the server name in the
2377  ** file "/etc/nntpserver".
2378  */
2379  { "newsgroups_charset", DT_STRING, &C_NewsgroupsCharset, IP "utf-8", 0, charset_validator },
2380  /*
2381  ** .pp
2382  ** Character set of newsgroups descriptions.
2383  */
2384  { "newsrc", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_FILE, &C_Newsrc, IP "~/.newsrc" },
2385  /*
2386  ** .pp
2387  ** The file, containing info about subscribed newsgroups - names and
2388  ** indexes of read articles. The following printf-style sequence
2389  ** is understood:
2390  ** .dl
2391  ** .dt \fBExpando\fP .dd \fBDescription\fP .dd \fBExample\fP
2392  ** .dt %a .dd Account url .dd \fCnews:news.gmane.org\fP
2393  ** .dt %p .dd Port .dd \fC119\fP
2394  ** .dt %P .dd Port if specified .dd \fC10119\fP
2395  ** .dt %s .dd News server name .dd \fCnews.gmane.org\fP
2396  ** .dt %S .dd Url schema .dd \fCnews\fP
2397  ** .dt %u .dd Username .dd \fCusername\fP
2398  ** .de
2399  */
2400 #endif
2401 #ifdef USE_NOTMUCH
2402  { "nm_db_limit", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_NmDbLimit, 0 },
2403  /*
2404  ** .pp
2405  ** This variable specifies the default limit used in notmuch queries.
2406  */
2407  { "nm_default_url", DT_STRING, &C_NmDefaultUrl, 0 },
2408  /*
2409  ** .pp
2410  ** This variable specifies the default Notmuch database in format
2411  ** notmuch://<absolute path>.
2412  */
2413  { "nm_exclude_tags", DT_STRING, &C_NmExcludeTags, 0 },
2414  /*
2415  ** .pp
2416  ** The messages tagged with these tags are excluded and not loaded
2417  ** from notmuch DB to NeoMutt unless specified explicitly.
2418  */
2419  { "nm_flagged_tag", DT_STRING, &C_NmFlaggedTag, IP "flagged" },
2420  /*
2421  ** .pp
2422  ** This variable specifies notmuch tag which is used for flagged messages. The
2423  ** variable is used to count flagged messages in DB and set the flagged flag when
2424  ** modifying tags. All other NeoMutt commands use standard (e.g. maildir) flags.
2425  */
2426  { "nm_open_timeout", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_NmOpenTimeout, 5 },
2427  /*
2428  ** .pp
2429  ** This variable specifies the timeout for database open in seconds.
2430  */
2431  { "nm_query_type", DT_STRING, &C_NmQueryType, IP "messages" },
2432  /*
2433  ** .pp
2434  ** This variable specifies the default query type (threads or messages) used in notmuch queries.
2435  */
2436  { "nm_query_window_current_position", DT_NUMBER, &C_NmQueryWindowCurrentPosition, 0 },
2437  /*
2438  ** .pp
2439  ** This variable contains the position of the current search for window based vfolder.
2440  */
2441  { "nm_query_window_current_search", DT_STRING, &C_NmQueryWindowCurrentSearch, 0 },
2442  /*
2443  ** .pp
2444  ** This variable contains the currently setup notmuch search for window based vfolder.
2445  */
2446  { "nm_query_window_duration", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_NmQueryWindowDuration, 0 },
2447  /*
2448  ** .pp
2449  ** This variable sets the time duration of a windowed notmuch query.
2450  ** Accepted values all non negative integers. A value of 0 disables the feature.
2451  */
2452  { "nm_query_window_timebase", DT_STRING, &C_NmQueryWindowTimebase, IP "week" },
2453  /*
2454  ** .pp
2455  ** This variable sets the time base of a windowed notmuch query.
2456  ** Accepted values are 'minute', 'hour', 'day', 'week', 'month', 'year'
2457  */
2458  { "nm_record", DT_BOOL, &C_NmRecord, false },
2459  /*
2460  ** .pp
2461  ** This variable specifies if the NeoMutt record should indexed by notmuch.
2462  */
2463  { "nm_record_tags", DT_STRING, &C_NmRecordTags, 0 },
2464  /*
2465  ** .pp
2466  ** This variable specifies the default tags applied to messages stored to the NeoMutt record.
2467  ** When set to 0 this variable disable the window feature.
2468  */
2469  { "nm_replied_tag", DT_STRING, &C_NmRepliedTag, IP "replied" },
2470  /*
2471  ** .pp
2472  ** This variable specifies notmuch tag which is used for replied messages. The
2473  ** variable is used to set the replied flag when modifiying tags. All other NeoMutt
2474  ** commands use standard (e.g. maildir) flags.
2475  */
2476  { "nm_unread_tag", DT_STRING, &C_NmUnreadTag, IP "unread" },
2477  /*
2478  ** .pp
2479  ** This variable specifies notmuch tag which is used for unread messages. The
2480  ** variable is used to count unread messages in DB and set the unread flag when
2481  ** modifiying tags. All other NeoMutt commands use standard (e.g. maildir) flags.
2482  */
2483 #endif
2484 #ifdef USE_NNTP
2485  { "nntp_authenticators", DT_STRING, &C_NntpAuthenticators, 0 },
2486  /*
2487  ** .pp
2488  ** This is a colon-delimited list of authentication methods NeoMutt may
2489  ** attempt to use to log in to a news server, in the order NeoMutt should
2490  ** try them. Authentication methods are either "user" or any
2491  ** SASL mechanism, e.g. "digest-md5", "gssapi" or "cram-md5".
2492  ** This option is case-insensitive. If it's \fIunset\fP (the default)
2493  ** NeoMutt will try all available methods, in order from most-secure to
2494  ** least-secure.
2495  ** .pp
2496  ** Example:
2497  ** .ts
2498  ** set nntp_authenticators="digest-md5:user"
2499  ** .te
2500  ** .pp
2501  ** \fBNote:\fP NeoMutt will only fall back to other authentication methods if
2502  ** the previous methods are unavailable. If a method is available but
2503  ** authentication fails, NeoMutt will not connect to the IMAP server.
2504  */
2505  { "nntp_context", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_NntpContext, 1000 },
2506  /*
2507  ** .pp
2508  ** This variable defines number of articles which will be in index when
2509  ** newsgroup entered. If active newsgroup have more articles than this
2510  ** number, oldest articles will be ignored. Also controls how many
2511  ** articles headers will be saved in cache when you quit newsgroup.
2512  */
2513  { "nntp_listgroup", DT_BOOL, &C_NntpListgroup, true },
2514  /*
2515  ** .pp
2516  ** This variable controls whether or not existence of each article is
2517  ** checked when newsgroup is entered.
2518  */
2519  { "nntp_load_description", DT_BOOL, &C_NntpLoadDescription, true },
2520  /*
2521  ** .pp
2522  ** This variable controls whether or not descriptions for each newsgroup
2523  ** must be loaded when newsgroup is added to list (first time list
2524  ** loading or new newsgroup adding).
2525  */
2526  { "nntp_pass", DT_STRING|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_NntpPass, 0 },
2527  /*
2528  ** .pp
2529  ** Your password for NNTP account.
2530  */
2531  { "nntp_poll", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_NntpPoll, 60 },
2532  /*
2533  ** .pp
2534  ** The time in seconds until any operations on newsgroup except post new
2535  ** article will cause recheck for new news. If set to 0, NeoMutt will
2536  ** recheck newsgroup on each operation in index (stepping, read article,
2537  ** etc.).
2538  */
2539  { "nntp_user", DT_STRING|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_NntpUser, 0 },
2540  /*
2541  ** .pp
2542  ** Your login name on the NNTP server. If \fIunset\fP and NNTP server requires
2543  ** authentication, NeoMutt will prompt you for your account name when you
2544  ** connect to news server.
2545  */
2546 #endif
2547  { "pager", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_Pager, IP "builtin" },
2548  /*
2549  ** .pp
2550  ** This variable specifies which pager you would like to use to view
2551  ** messages. The value "builtin" means to use the built-in pager, otherwise this
2552  ** variable should specify the pathname of the external pager you would
2553  ** like to use.
2554  ** .pp
2555  ** Using an external pager may have some disadvantages: Additional
2556  ** keystrokes are necessary because you can't call NeoMutt functions
2557  ** directly from the pager, and screen resizes cause lines longer than
2558  ** the screen width to be badly formatted in the help menu.
2559  */
2560  { "pager_context", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_PagerContext, 0 },
2561  /*
2562  ** .pp
2563  ** This variable controls the number of lines of context that are given
2564  ** when displaying the next or previous page in the internal pager. By
2565  ** default, NeoMutt will display the line after the last one on the screen
2566  ** at the top of the next page (0 lines of context).
2567  ** .pp
2568  ** This variable also specifies the amount of context given for search
2569  ** results. If positive, this many lines will be given before a match,
2570  ** if 0, the match will be top-aligned.
2571  */
2572  { "pager_format", DT_STRING|R_PAGER, &C_PagerFormat, IP "-%Z- %C/%m: %-20.20n %s%* -- (%P)" },
2573  /*
2574  ** .pp
2575  ** This variable controls the format of the one-line message "status"
2576  ** displayed before each message in either the internal or an external
2577  ** pager. The valid sequences are listed in the $$index_format
2578  ** section.
2579  */
2580  { "pager_index_lines", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE|R_PAGER|R_REFLOW, &C_PagerIndexLines, 0 },
2581  /*
2582  ** .pp
2583  ** Determines the number of lines of a mini-index which is shown when in
2584  ** the pager. The current message, unless near the top or bottom of the
2585  ** folder, will be roughly one third of the way down this mini-index,
2586  ** giving the reader the context of a few messages before and after the
2587  ** message. This is useful, for example, to determine how many messages
2588  ** remain to be read in the current thread. One of the lines is reserved
2589  ** for the status bar from the index, so a setting of 6
2590  ** will only show 5 lines of the actual index. A value of 0 results in
2591  ** no index being shown. If the number of messages in the current folder
2592  ** is less than $$pager_index_lines, then the index will only use as
2593  ** many lines as it needs.
2594  */
2595  { "pager_stop", DT_BOOL, &C_PagerStop, false },
2596  /*
2597  ** .pp
2598  ** When \fIset\fP, the internal-pager will \fBnot\fP move to the next message
2599  ** when you are at the end of a message and invoke the \fC<next-page>\fP
2600  ** function.
2601  */
2602  { "pgp_auto_decode", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpAutoDecode, false },
2603  /*
2604  ** .pp
2605  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will automatically attempt to decrypt traditional PGP
2606  ** messages whenever the user performs an operation which ordinarily would
2607  ** result in the contents of the message being operated on. For example,
2608  ** if the user displays a pgp-traditional message which has not been manually
2609  ** checked with the \fC$<check-traditional-pgp>\fP function, NeoMutt will automatically
2610  ** check the message for traditional pgp.
2611  */
2612  { "pgp_autoinline", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpAutoinline, false },
2613  /*
2614  ** .pp
2615  ** This option controls whether NeoMutt generates old-style inline
2616  ** (traditional) PGP encrypted or signed messages under certain
2617  ** circumstances. This can be overridden by use of the pgp menu,
2618  ** when inline is not required. The GPGME backend does not support
2619  ** this option.
2620  ** .pp
2621  ** Note that NeoMutt might automatically use PGP/MIME for messages
2622  ** which consist of more than a single MIME part. NeoMutt can be
2623  ** configured to ask before sending PGP/MIME messages when inline
2624  ** (traditional) would not work.
2625  ** .pp
2626  ** Also see the $$pgp_mime_auto variable.
2627  ** .pp
2628  ** Also note that using the old-style PGP message format is \fBstrongly\fP
2629  ** \fBdeprecated\fP.
2630  ** (PGP only)
2631  */
2632 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_PGP
2633  { "pgp_check_exit", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpCheckExit, true },
2634  /*
2635  ** .pp
2636  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will check the exit code of the PGP subprocess when
2637  ** signing or encrypting. A non-zero exit code means that the
2638  ** subprocess failed.
2639  ** (PGP only)
2640  */
2641  { "pgp_check_gpg_decrypt_status_fd", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpCheckGpgDecryptStatusFd, true },
2642  /*
2643  ** .pp
2644  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will check the status file descriptor output
2645  ** of $$pgp_decrypt_command and $$pgp_decode_command for GnuPG status codes
2646  ** indicating successful decryption. This will check for the presence of
2647  ** DECRYPTION_OKAY, absence of DECRYPTION_FAILED, and that all
2648  ** PLAINTEXT occurs between the BEGIN_DECRYPTION and END_DECRYPTION
2649  ** status codes.
2650  ** .pp
2651  ** If \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will instead match the status fd output
2652  ** against $$pgp_decryption_okay.
2653  ** (PGP only)
2654  */
2655  { "pgp_clearsign_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpClearsignCommand, 0 },
2656  /*
2657  ** .pp
2658  ** This format is used to create an old-style "clearsigned" PGP
2659  ** message. Note that the use of this format is \fBstrongly\fP
2660  ** \fBdeprecated\fP.
2661  ** .pp
2662  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
2663  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
2664  ** Note that in this case, %r expands to the search string, which is a list of
2665  ** one or more quoted values such as email address, name, or keyid.
2666  ** (PGP only)
2667  */
2668  { "pgp_decode_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpDecodeCommand, 0 },
2669  /*
2670  ** .pp
2671  ** This format strings specifies a command which is used to decode
2672  ** application/pgp attachments.
2673  ** .pp
2674  ** The PGP command formats have their own set of \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences:
2675  ** .dl
2676  ** .dt %a .dd The value of $$pgp_sign_as if set, otherwise the value
2677  ** of $$pgp_default_key.
2678  ** .dt %f .dd Expands to the name of a file containing a message.
2679  ** .dt %p .dd Expands to PGPPASSFD=0 when a pass phrase is needed, to an empty
2680  ** string otherwise. Note: This may be used with a %? construct.
2681  ** .dt %r .dd One or more key IDs (or fingerprints if available).
2682  ** .dt %s .dd Expands to the name of a file containing the signature part
2683  ** of a \fCmultipart/signed\fP attachment when verifying it.
2684  ** .de
2685  ** .pp
2686  ** For examples on how to configure these formats for the various versions
2687  ** of PGP which are floating around, see the pgp and gpg sample configuration files in
2688  ** the \fCsamples/\fP subdirectory which has been installed on your system
2689  ** alongside the documentation.
2690  ** (PGP only)
2691  */
2692  { "pgp_decrypt_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpDecryptCommand, 0 },
2693  /*
2694  ** .pp
2695  ** This command is used to decrypt a PGP encrypted message.
2696  ** .pp
2697  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
2698  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
2699  ** (PGP only)
2700  ** .pp
2701  ** Note: When decrypting messages using \fCgpg\fP, a pinentry program needs to
2702  ** be invoked unless the password is cached within \fCgpg-agent\fP.
2703  ** Currently, the \fCpinentry-tty\fP program (usually distributed with
2704  ** \fCgpg\fP) isn't suitable for being invoked by NeoMutt. You are encouraged
2705  ** to use a different pinentry-program when running NeoMutt in order to avoid
2706  ** problems.
2707  ** .pp
2708  ** See also: https://github.com/neomutt/neomutt/issues/1014
2709  */
2710  { "pgp_decryption_okay", DT_REGEX, &C_PgpDecryptionOkay, 0 },
2711  /*
2712  ** .pp
2713  ** If you assign text to this variable, then an encrypted PGP
2714  ** message is only considered successfully decrypted if the output
2715  ** from $$pgp_decrypt_command contains the text. This is used to
2716  ** protect against a spoofed encrypted message, with multipart/encrypted
2717  ** headers but containing a block that is not actually encrypted.
2718  ** (e.g. simply signed and ascii armored text).
2719  ** .pp
2720  ** Note that if $$pgp_check_gpg_decrypt_status_fd is set, this variable
2721  ** is ignored.
2722  ** (PGP only)
2723  */
2724 #endif
2725  { "pgp_default_key", DT_STRING, &C_PgpDefaultKey, 0 },
2726  /*
2727  ** .pp
2728  ** This is the default key-pair to use for PGP operations. It will be
2729  ** used for encryption (see $$postpone_encrypt and $$pgp_self_encrypt).
2730  ** .pp
2731  ** It will also be used for signing unless $$pgp_sign_as is set.
2732  ** .pp
2733  ** The (now deprecated) \fIpgp_self_encrypt_as\fP is an alias for this
2734  ** variable, and should no longer be used.
2735  ** (PGP only)
2736  */
2737 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_PGP
2738  { "pgp_encrypt_only_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpEncryptOnlyCommand, 0 },
2739  /*
2740  ** .pp
2741  ** This command is used to encrypt a body part without signing it.
2742  ** .pp
2743  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
2744  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
2745  ** Note that in this case, %r expands to the search string, which is a list of
2746  ** one or more quoted values such as email address, name, or keyid.
2747  ** (PGP only)
2748  */
2749  { "pgp_encrypt_sign_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpEncryptSignCommand, 0 },
2750  /*
2751  ** .pp
2752  ** This command is used to both sign and encrypt a body part.
2753  ** .pp
2754  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
2755  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
2756  ** (PGP only)
2757  */
2758 #endif
2759  { "pgp_entry_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_PgpEntryFormat, IP "%4n %t%f %4l/0x%k %-4a %2c %u" },
2760  /*
2761  ** .pp
2762  ** This variable allows you to customize the PGP key selection menu to
2763  ** your personal taste. If $$crypt_use_gpgme is \fIset\fP, then it applies
2764  ** to S/MIME key selection menu also. This string is similar to $$index_format,
2765  ** but has its own set of \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences:
2766  ** .dl
2767  ** .dt %a .dd Algorithm
2768  ** .dt %c .dd Capabilities
2769  ** .dt %f .dd Flags
2770  ** .dt %k .dd Key id
2771  ** .dt %l .dd Key length
2772  ** .dt %n .dd Number
2773  ** .dt %p .dd Protocol
2774  ** .dt %t .dd Trust/validity of the key-uid association
2775  ** .dt %u .dd User id
2776  ** .dt %[<s>] .dd Date of the key where <s> is an \fCstrftime(3)\fP expression
2777  ** .de
2778  ** .pp
2779  ** (Crypto only) or (PGP only when GPGME disabled)
2780  */
2781 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_PGP
2782  { "pgp_export_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpExportCommand, 0 },
2783  /*
2784  ** .pp
2785  ** This command is used to export a public key from the user's
2786  ** key ring.
2787  ** .pp
2788  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
2789  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
2790  ** (PGP only)
2791  */
2792  { "pgp_getkeys_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpGetkeysCommand, 0 },
2793  /*
2794  ** .pp
2795  ** This command is invoked whenever NeoMutt needs to fetch the public key associated with
2796  ** an email address. Of the sequences supported by $$pgp_decode_command, %r is
2797  ** the only \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequence used with this format. Note that
2798  ** in this case, %r expands to the email address, not the public key ID (the key ID is
2799  ** unknown, which is why NeoMutt is invoking this command).
2800  ** (PGP only)
2801  */
2802  { "pgp_good_sign", DT_REGEX, &C_PgpGoodSign, 0 },
2803  /*
2804  ** .pp
2805  ** If you assign a text to this variable, then a PGP signature is only
2806  ** considered verified if the output from $$pgp_verify_command contains
2807  ** the text. Use this variable if the exit code from the command is 0
2808  ** even for bad signatures.
2809  ** (PGP only)
2810  */
2811 #endif
2812  { "pgp_ignore_subkeys", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpIgnoreSubkeys, true },
2813  /*
2814  ** .pp
2815  ** Setting this variable will cause NeoMutt to ignore OpenPGP subkeys. Instead,
2816  ** the principal key will inherit the subkeys' capabilities. \fIUnset\fP this
2817  ** if you want to play interesting key selection games.
2818  ** (PGP only)
2819  */
2820 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_PGP
2821  { "pgp_import_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpImportCommand, 0 },
2822  /*
2823  ** .pp
2824  ** This command is used to import a key from a message into
2825  ** the user's public key ring.
2826  ** .pp
2827  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
2828  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
2829  ** (PGP only)
2830  */
2831  { "pgp_list_pubring_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpListPubringCommand, 0 },
2832  /*
2833  ** .pp
2834  ** This command is used to list the public key ring's contents. The
2835  ** output format must be analogous to the one used by
2836  ** .ts
2837  ** gpg --list-keys --with-colons --with-fingerprint
2838  ** .te
2839  ** .pp
2840  ** Note: gpg's \fCfixed-list-mode\fP option should not be used. It
2841  ** produces a different date format which may result in NeoMutt showing
2842  ** incorrect key generation dates.
2843  ** .pp
2844  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
2845  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
2846  ** (PGP only)
2847  */
2848  { "pgp_list_secring_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpListSecringCommand, 0 },
2849  /*
2850  ** .pp
2851  ** This command is used to list the secret key ring's contents. The
2852  ** output format must be analogous to the one used by:
2853  ** .ts
2854  ** gpg --list-keys --with-colons --with-fingerprint
2855  ** .te
2856  ** .pp
2857  ** Note: gpg's \fCfixed-list-mode\fP option should not be used. It
2858  ** produces a different date format which may result in NeoMutt showing
2859  ** incorrect key generation dates.
2860  ** .pp
2861  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
2862  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
2863  ** (PGP only)
2864  */
2865 #endif
2866  { "pgp_long_ids", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpLongIds, true },
2867  /*
2868  ** .pp
2869  ** If \fIset\fP, use 64 bit PGP key IDs, if \fIunset\fP use the normal 32 bit key IDs.
2870  ** NOTE: Internally, NeoMutt has transitioned to using fingerprints (or long key IDs
2871  ** as a fallback). This option now only controls the display of key IDs
2872  ** in the key selection menu and a few other places.
2873  ** (PGP only)
2874  */
2875  { "pgp_mime_auto", DT_QUAD, &C_PgpMimeAuto, MUTT_ASKYES },
2876  /*
2877  ** .pp
2878  ** This option controls whether NeoMutt will prompt you for
2879  ** automatically sending a (signed/encrypted) message using
2880  ** PGP/MIME when inline (traditional) fails (for any reason).
2881  ** .pp
2882  ** Also note that using the old-style PGP message format is \fBstrongly\fP
2883  ** \fBdeprecated\fP.
2884  ** (PGP only)
2885  */
2886  { "pgp_replyinline", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpReplyinline, false },
2887  /*
2888  ** .pp
2889  ** Setting this variable will cause NeoMutt to always attempt to
2890  ** create an inline (traditional) message when replying to a
2891  ** message which is PGP encrypted/signed inline. This can be
2892  ** overridden by use of the pgp menu, when inline is not
2893  ** required. This option does not automatically detect if the
2894  ** (replied-to) message is inline; instead it relies on NeoMutt
2895  ** internals for previously checked/flagged messages.
2896  ** .pp
2897  ** Note that NeoMutt might automatically use PGP/MIME for messages
2898  ** which consist of more than a single MIME part. NeoMutt can be
2899  ** configured to ask before sending PGP/MIME messages when inline
2900  ** (traditional) would not work.
2901  ** .pp
2902  ** Also see the $$pgp_mime_auto variable.
2903  ** .pp
2904  ** Also note that using the old-style PGP message format is \fBstrongly\fP
2905  ** \fBdeprecated\fP.
2906  ** (PGP only)
2907  */
2908  { "pgp_retainable_sigs", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpRetainableSigs, false },
2909  /*
2910  ** .pp
2911  ** If \fIset\fP, signed and encrypted messages will consist of nested
2912  ** \fCmultipart/signed\fP and \fCmultipart/encrypted\fP body parts.
2913  ** .pp
2914  ** This is useful for applications like encrypted and signed mailing
2915  ** lists, where the outer layer (\fCmultipart/encrypted\fP) can be easily
2916  ** removed, while the inner \fCmultipart/signed\fP part is retained.
2917  ** (PGP only)
2918  */
2919  { "pgp_self_encrypt", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpSelfEncrypt, true },
2920  /*
2921  ** .pp
2922  ** When \fIset\fP, PGP encrypted messages will also be encrypted
2923  ** using the key in $$pgp_default_key.
2924  ** (PGP only)
2925  */
2926  { "pgp_show_unusable", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpShowUnusable, true },
2927  /*
2928  ** .pp
2929  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will display non-usable keys on the PGP key selection
2930  ** menu. This includes keys which have been revoked, have expired, or
2931  ** have been marked as "disabled" by the user.
2932  ** (PGP only)
2933  */
2934  { "pgp_sign_as", DT_STRING, &C_PgpSignAs, 0 },
2935  /*
2936  ** .pp
2937  ** If you have a different key pair to use for signing, you should
2938  ** set this to the signing key. Most people will only need to set
2939  ** $$pgp_default_key. It is recommended that you use the keyid form
2940  ** to specify your key (e.g. \fC0x00112233\fP).
2941  ** (PGP only)
2942  */
2943 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_PGP
2944  { "pgp_sign_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpSignCommand, 0 },
2945  /*
2946  ** .pp
2947  ** This command is used to create the detached PGP signature for a
2948  ** \fCmultipart/signed\fP PGP/MIME body part.
2949  ** .pp
2950  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
2951  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
2952  ** (PGP only)
2953  */
2954 #endif
2955  { "pgp_sort_keys", DT_SORT|DT_SORT_KEYS, &C_PgpSortKeys, SORT_ADDRESS },
2956  /*
2957  ** .pp
2958  ** Specifies how the entries in the pgp menu are sorted. The
2959  ** following are legal values:
2960  ** .dl
2961  ** .dt address .dd sort alphabetically by user id
2962  ** .dt keyid .dd sort alphabetically by key id
2963  ** .dt date .dd sort by key creation date
2964  ** .dt trust .dd sort by the trust of the key
2965  ** .de
2966  ** .pp
2967  ** If you prefer reverse order of the above values, prefix it with
2968  ** "reverse-".
2969  ** (PGP only)
2970  */
2971  { "pgp_strict_enc", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpStrictEnc, true },
2972  /*
2973  ** .pp
2974  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will automatically encode PGP/MIME signed messages as
2975  ** quoted-printable. Please note that unsetting this variable may
2976  ** lead to problems with non-verifyable PGP signatures, so only change
2977  ** this if you know what you are doing.
2978  ** (PGP only)
2979  */
2980 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_PGP
2981  { "pgp_timeout", DT_LONG|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_PgpTimeout, 300 },
2982  /*
2983  ** .pp
2984  ** The number of seconds after which a cached passphrase will expire if
2985  ** not used.
2986  ** (PGP only)
2987  */
2988  { "pgp_use_gpg_agent", DT_BOOL, &C_PgpUseGpgAgent, true },
2989  /*
2990  ** .pp
2991  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt expects a \fCgpg-agent(1)\fP process will handle
2992  ** private key passphrase prompts. If \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will prompt
2993  ** for the passphrase and pass it via stdin to the pgp command.
2994  ** .pp
2995  ** Note that as of version 2.1, GnuPG automatically spawns an agent
2996  ** and requires the agent be used for passphrase management. Since
2997  ** that version is increasingly prevalent, this variable now
2998  ** defaults \fIset\fP.
2999  ** .pp
3000  ** NeoMutt works with a GUI or curses pinentry program. A TTY pinentry
3001  ** should not be used.
3002  ** .pp
3003  ** If you are using an older version of GnuPG without an agent running,
3004  ** or another encryption program without an agent, you will need to
3005  ** \fIunset\fP this variable.
3006  ** (PGP only)
3007  */
3008  { "pgp_verify_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpVerifyCommand, 0 },
3009  /*
3010  ** .pp
3011  ** This command is used to verify PGP signatures.
3012  ** .pp
3013  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
3014  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
3015  ** (PGP only)
3016  */
3017  { "pgp_verify_key_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PgpVerifyKeyCommand, 0 },
3018  /*
3019  ** .pp
3020  ** This command is used to verify key information from the key selection
3021  ** menu.
3022  ** .pp
3023  ** This is a format string, see the $$pgp_decode_command command for
3024  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
3025  ** (PGP only)
3026  */
3027 #endif
3028  { "pipe_decode", DT_BOOL, &C_PipeDecode, false },
3029  /*
3030  ** .pp
3031  ** Used in connection with the \fC<pipe-message>\fP command. When \fIunset\fP,
3032  ** NeoMutt will pipe the messages without any preprocessing. When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt
3033  ** will weed headers and will attempt to decode the messages
3034  ** first.
3035  */
3036  { "pipe_sep", DT_STRING, &C_PipeSep, IP "\n" },
3037  /*
3038  ** .pp
3039  ** The separator to add between messages when piping a list of tagged
3040  ** messages to an external Unix command.
3041  */
3042  { "pipe_split", DT_BOOL, &C_PipeSplit, false },
3043  /*
3044  ** .pp
3045  ** Used in connection with the \fC<pipe-message>\fP function following
3046  ** \fC<tag-prefix>\fP. If this variable is \fIunset\fP, when piping a list of
3047  ** tagged messages NeoMutt will concatenate the messages and will pipe them
3048  ** all concatenated. When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will pipe the messages one by one.
3049  ** In both cases the messages are piped in the current sorted order,
3050  ** and the $$pipe_sep separator is added after each message.
3051  */
3052 #ifdef USE_POP
3053  { "pop_auth_try_all", DT_BOOL, &C_PopAuthTryAll, true },
3054  /*
3055  ** .pp
3056  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will try all available authentication methods.
3057  ** When \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will only fall back to other authentication
3058  ** methods if the previous methods are unavailable. If a method is
3059  ** available but authentication fails, NeoMutt will not connect to the POP server.
3060  */
3061  { "pop_authenticators", DT_SLIST|SLIST_SEP_COLON, &C_PopAuthenticators, 0 },
3062  /*
3063  ** .pp
3064  ** This is a colon-delimited list of authentication methods NeoMutt may
3065  ** attempt to use to log in to an POP server, in the order NeoMutt should
3066  ** try them. Authentication methods are either "user", "apop" or any
3067  ** SASL mechanism, e.g. "digest-md5", "gssapi" or "cram-md5".
3068  ** This option is case-insensitive. If this option is \fIunset\fP
3069  ** (the default) NeoMutt will try all available methods, in order from
3070  ** most-secure to least-secure.
3071  ** .pp
3072  ** Example:
3073  ** .ts
3074  ** set pop_authenticators="digest-md5:apop:user"
3075  ** .te
3076  */
3077  { "pop_checkinterval", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_PopCheckinterval, 60 },
3078  /*
3079  ** .pp
3080  ** This variable configures how often (in seconds) NeoMutt should look for
3081  ** new mail in the currently selected mailbox if it is a POP mailbox.
3082  */
3083  { "pop_delete", DT_QUAD, &C_PopDelete, MUTT_ASKNO },
3084  /*
3085  ** .pp
3086  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will delete successfully downloaded messages from the POP
3087  ** server when using the \fC$<fetch-mail>\fP function. When \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will
3088  ** download messages but also leave them on the POP server.
3089  */
3090  { "pop_host", DT_STRING, &C_PopHost, 0 },
3091  /*
3092  ** .pp
3093  ** The name of your POP server for the \fC$<fetch-mail>\fP function. You
3094  ** can also specify an alternative port, username and password, i.e.:
3095  ** .ts
3096  ** [pop[s]://][username[:password]@]popserver[:port]
3097  ** .te
3098  ** .pp
3099  ** where "[...]" denotes an optional part.
3100  */
3101  { "pop_last", DT_BOOL, &C_PopLast, false },
3102  /*
3103  ** .pp
3104  ** If this variable is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will try to use the "\fCLAST\fP" POP command
3105  ** for retrieving only unread messages from the POP server when using
3106  ** the \fC$<fetch-mail>\fP function.
3107  */
3108  { "pop_oauth_refresh_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_PopOauthRefreshCommand, 0 },
3109  /*
3110  ** .pp
3111  ** The command to run to generate an OAUTH refresh token for
3112  ** authorizing your connection to your POP server. This command will be
3113  ** run on every connection attempt that uses the OAUTHBEARER authentication
3114  ** mechanism. See "$oauth" for details.
3115  */
3116  { "pop_pass", DT_STRING|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_PopPass, 0 },
3117  /*
3118  ** .pp
3119  ** Specifies the password for your POP account. If \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will
3120  ** prompt you for your password when you open a POP mailbox.
3121  ** .pp
3122  ** \fBWarning\fP: you should only use this option when you are on a
3123  ** fairly secure machine, because the superuser can read your neomuttrc
3124  ** even if you are the only one who can read the file.
3125  */
3126  { "pop_reconnect", DT_QUAD, &C_PopReconnect, MUTT_ASKYES },
3127  /*
3128  ** .pp
3129  ** Controls whether or not NeoMutt will try to reconnect to the POP server if
3130  ** the connection is lost.
3131  */
3132  { "pop_user", DT_STRING|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_PopUser, 0 },
3133  /*
3134  ** .pp
3135  ** Your login name on the POP server.
3136  ** .pp
3137  ** This variable defaults to your user name on the local machine.
3138  */
3139 #endif /* USE_POP */
3140  { "post_indent_string", DT_STRING, &C_PostIndentString, 0 },
3141  /*
3142  ** .pp
3143  ** Similar to the $$attribution variable, NeoMutt will append this
3144  ** string after the inclusion of a message which is being replied to.
3145  */
3146 #ifdef USE_NNTP
3147  { "post_moderated", DT_QUAD, &C_PostModerated, MUTT_ASKYES },
3148  /*
3149  ** .pp
3150  ** If set to \fIyes\fP, NeoMutt will post article to newsgroup that have
3151  ** not permissions to posting (e.g. moderated). \fBNote:\fP if news server
3152  ** does not support posting to that newsgroup or totally read-only, that
3153  ** posting will not have an effect.
3154  */
3155 #endif
3156  { "postpone", DT_QUAD, &C_Postpone, MUTT_ASKYES },
3157  /*
3158  ** .pp
3159  ** Controls whether or not messages are saved in the $$postponed
3160  ** mailbox when you elect not to send immediately. If set to
3161  ** \fIask-yes\fP or \fIask-no\fP, you will be prompted with "Save
3162  ** (postpone) draft message?" when quitting from the "compose"
3163  ** screen.
3164  ** .pp
3165  ** Also see the $$recall variable.
3166  */
3167  { "postpone_encrypt", DT_BOOL, &C_PostponeEncrypt, false },
3168  /*
3169  ** .pp
3170  ** When \fIset\fP, postponed messages that are marked for encryption will be
3171  ** self-encrypted. NeoMutt will first try to encrypt using the value specified
3172  ** in $$pgp_default_key or $$smime_default_key. If those are not
3173  ** set, it will try the deprecated $$postpone_encrypt_as.
3174  ** (Crypto only)
3175  */
3176  { "postpone_encrypt_as", DT_STRING, &C_PostponeEncryptAs, 0 },
3177  /*
3178  ** .pp
3179  ** This is a deprecated fall-back variable for $$postpone_encrypt.
3180  ** Please use $$pgp_default_key or $$smime_default_key.
3181  ** (Crypto only)
3182  */
3183  { "postponed", DT_STRING|DT_MAILBOX|R_INDEX, &C_Postponed, IP "~/postponed" },
3184  /*
3185  ** .pp
3186  ** NeoMutt allows you to indefinitely "$postpone sending a message" which
3187  ** you are editing. When you choose to postpone a message, NeoMutt saves it
3188  ** in the mailbox specified by this variable.
3189  ** .pp
3190  ** Also see the $$postpone variable.
3191  */
3192 #ifdef USE_SOCKET
3193  { "preconnect", DT_STRING, &C_Preconnect, 0 },
3194  /*
3195  ** .pp
3196  ** If \fIset\fP, a shell command to be executed if NeoMutt fails to establish
3197  ** a connection to the server. This is useful for setting up secure
3198  ** connections, e.g. with \fCssh(1)\fP. If the command returns a nonzero
3199  ** status, NeoMutt gives up opening the server. Example:
3200  ** .ts
3201  ** set preconnect="ssh -f -q -L 1234:mailhost.net:143 mailhost.net \(rs
3202  ** sleep 20 < /dev/null > /dev/null"
3203  ** .te
3204  ** .pp
3205  ** Mailbox "foo" on "mailhost.net" can now be reached
3206  ** as "{localhost:1234}foo".
3207  ** .pp
3208  ** Note: For this example to work, you must be able to log in to the
3209  ** remote machine without having to enter a password.
3210  */
3211 #endif /* USE_SOCKET */
3212  { "preferred_languages", DT_SLIST|SLIST_SEP_COMMA, &C_PreferredLanguages, 0 },
3213  /*
3214  ** .pp
3215  ** RFC8255 : user preferred languages to be search in parts and display
3216  ** Ex. : set preferred_languages="en,fr,de"
3217  */
3218  { "print", DT_QUAD, &C_Print, MUTT_ASKNO },
3219  /*
3220  ** .pp
3221  ** Controls whether or not NeoMutt really prints messages.
3222  ** This is set to "ask-no" by default, because some people
3223  ** accidentally hit "p" often.
3224  */
3225  { "print_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_PrintCommand, IP "lpr" },
3226  /*
3227  ** .pp
3228  ** This specifies the command pipe that should be used to print messages.
3229  */
3230  { "print_decode", DT_BOOL, &C_PrintDecode, true },
3231  /*
3232  ** .pp
3233  ** Used in connection with the \fC<print-message>\fP command. If this
3234  ** option is \fIset\fP, the message is decoded before it is passed to the
3235  ** external command specified by $$print_command. If this option
3236  ** is \fIunset\fP, no processing will be applied to the message when
3237  ** printing it. The latter setting may be useful if you are using
3238  ** some advanced printer filter which is able to properly format
3239  ** e-mail messages for printing.
3240  */
3241  { "print_split", DT_BOOL, &C_PrintSplit, false },
3242  /*
3243  ** .pp
3244  ** Used in connection with the \fC<print-message>\fP command. If this option
3245  ** is \fIset\fP, the command specified by $$print_command is executed once for
3246  ** each message which is to be printed. If this option is \fIunset\fP,
3247  ** the command specified by $$print_command is executed only once, and
3248  ** all the messages are concatenated, with a form feed as the message
3249  ** separator.
3250  ** .pp
3251  ** Those who use the \fCenscript\fP(1) program's mail-printing mode will
3252  ** most likely want to \fIset\fP this option.
3253  */
3254  { "prompt_after", DT_BOOL, &C_PromptAfter, true },
3255  /*
3256  ** .pp
3257  ** If you use an \fIexternal\fP $$pager, setting this variable will
3258  ** cause NeoMutt to prompt you for a command when the pager exits rather
3259  ** than returning to the index menu. If \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will return to the
3260  ** index menu when the external pager exits.
3261  */
3262  { "query_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_QueryCommand, 0 },
3263  /*
3264  ** .pp
3265  ** This specifies the command NeoMutt will use to make external address
3266  ** queries. The string may contain a "%s", which will be substituted
3267  ** with the query string the user types. NeoMutt will add quotes around the
3268  ** string substituted for "%s" automatically according to shell quoting
3269  ** rules, so you should avoid adding your own. If no "%s" is found in
3270  ** the string, NeoMutt will append the user's query to the end of the string.
3271  ** See "$query" for more information.
3272  */
3273  { "query_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_QueryFormat, IP "%3c %t %-25.25n %-25.25a | %e" },
3274  /*
3275  ** .pp
3276  ** This variable describes the format of the "query" menu. The
3277  ** following \fCprintf(3)\fP-style sequences are understood:
3278  ** .dl
3279  ** .dt %a .dd .dd Destination address
3280  ** .dt %c .dd .dd Current entry number
3281  ** .dt %e .dd * .dd Extra information
3282  ** .dt %n .dd .dd Destination name
3283  ** .dt %t .dd .dd "*" if current entry is tagged, a space otherwise
3284  ** .dt %>X .dd .dd Right justify the rest of the string and pad with "X"
3285  ** .dt %|X .dd .dd Pad to the end of the line with "X"
3286  ** .dt %*X .dd .dd Soft-fill with character "X" as pad
3287  ** .de
3288  ** .pp
3289  ** For an explanation of "soft-fill", see the $$index_format documentation.
3290  ** .pp
3291  ** * = can be optionally printed if nonzero, see the $$status_format documentation.
3292  */
3293  { "quit", DT_QUAD, &C_Quit, MUTT_YES },
3294  /*
3295  ** .pp
3296  ** This variable controls whether "quit" and "exit" actually quit
3297  ** from NeoMutt. If this option is \fIset\fP, they do quit, if it is \fIunset\fP, they
3298  ** have no effect, and if it is set to \fIask-yes\fP or \fIask-no\fP, you are
3299  ** prompted for confirmation when you try to quit.
3300  */
3301  { "quote_regex", DT_REGEX|R_PAGER, &C_QuoteRegex, IP "^([ \t]*[|>:}#])+" },
3302  /*
3303  ** .pp
3304  ** A regular expression used in the internal pager to determine quoted
3305  ** sections of text in the body of a message. Quoted text may be filtered
3306  ** out using the \fC<toggle-quoted>\fP command, or colored according to the
3307  ** "color quoted" family of directives.
3308  ** .pp
3309  ** Higher levels of quoting may be colored differently ("color quoted1",
3310  ** "color quoted2", etc.). The quoting level is determined by removing
3311  ** the last character from the matched text and recursively reapplying
3312  ** the regular expression until it fails to produce a match.
3313  ** .pp
3314  ** Match detection may be overridden by the $$smileys regular expression.
3315  */
3316  { "toggle_quoted_show_levels", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_ToggleQuotedShowLevels, 0 },
3317  /*
3318  ** .pp
3319  ** Quoted text may be filtered out using the \fC<toggle-quoted>\fP command.
3320  ** If set to a number greater than 0, then the \fC<toggle-quoted>\fP
3321  ** command will only filter out quote levels above this number.
3322  */
3323  { "read_inc", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_ReadInc, 10 },
3324  /*
3325  ** .pp
3326  ** If set to a value greater than 0, NeoMutt will display which message it
3327  ** is currently on when reading a mailbox or when performing search actions
3328  ** such as search and limit. The message is printed after
3329  ** this many messages have been read or searched (e.g., if set to 25, NeoMutt will
3330  ** print a message when it is at message 25, and then again when it gets
3331  ** to message 50). This variable is meant to indicate progress when
3332  ** reading or searching large mailboxes which may take some time.
3333  ** When set to 0, only a single message will appear before the reading
3334  ** the mailbox.
3335  ** .pp
3336  ** Also see the $$write_inc, $$net_inc and $$time_inc variables and the
3337  ** "$tuning" section of the manual for performance considerations.
3338  */
3339  { "read_only", DT_BOOL, &C_ReadOnly, false },
3340  /*
3341  ** .pp
3342  ** If \fIset\fP, all folders are opened in read-only mode.
3343  */
3344  { "realname", DT_STRING|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_Realname, 0 },
3345  /*
3346  ** .pp
3347  ** This variable specifies what "real" or "personal" name should be used
3348  ** when sending messages.
3349  ** .pp
3350  ** If not specified, then the user's "real name" will be read from \fC/etc/passwd\fP.
3351  ** This option will not be used, if "$$from" is set.
3352  */
3353  { "recall", DT_QUAD, &C_Recall, MUTT_ASKYES },
3354  /*
3355  ** .pp
3356  ** Controls whether or not NeoMutt recalls postponed messages
3357  ** when composing a new message.
3358  ** .pp
3359  ** Setting this variable to \fIyes\fP is not generally useful, and thus not
3360  ** recommended. Note that the \fC<recall-message>\fP function can be used
3361  ** to manually recall postponed messages.
3362  ** .pp
3363  ** Also see $$postponed variable.
3364  */
3365  { "record", DT_STRING|DT_MAILBOX, &C_Record, IP "~/sent" },
3366  /*
3367  ** .pp
3368  ** This specifies the file into which your outgoing messages should be
3369  ** appended. (This is meant as the primary method for saving a copy of
3370  ** your messages, but another way to do this is using the "$my_hdr"
3371  ** command to create a "Bcc:" field with your email address in it.)
3372  ** .pp
3373  ** The value of \fI$$record\fP is overridden by the $$force_name and
3374  ** $$save_name variables, and the "$fcc-hook" command. Also see $$copy
3375  ** and $$write_bcc.
3376  */
3377  { "reflow_space_quotes", DT_BOOL, &C_ReflowSpaceQuotes, true },
3378  /*
3379  ** .pp
3380  ** This option controls how quotes from format=flowed messages are displayed
3381  ** in the pager and when replying (with $$text_flowed \fIunset\fP).
3382  ** When set, this option adds spaces after each level of quote marks, turning
3383  ** ">>>foo" into "> > > foo".
3384  ** .pp
3385  ** \fBNote:\fP If $$reflow_text is \fIunset\fP, this option has no effect.
3386  ** Also, this option does not affect replies when $$text_flowed is \fIset\fP.
3387  */
3388  { "reflow_text", DT_BOOL, &C_ReflowText, true },
3389  /*
3390  ** .pp
3391  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will reformat paragraphs in text/plain
3392  ** parts marked format=flowed. If \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will display paragraphs
3393  ** unaltered from how they appear in the message body. See RFC3676 for
3394  ** details on the \fIformat=flowed\fP format.
3395  ** .pp
3396  ** Also see $$reflow_wrap, and $$wrap.
3397  */
3398  { "reflow_wrap", DT_NUMBER, &C_ReflowWrap, 78 },
3399  /*
3400  ** .pp
3401  ** This variable controls the maximum paragraph width when reformatting text/plain
3402  ** parts when $$reflow_text is \fIset\fP. When the value is 0, paragraphs will
3403  ** be wrapped at the terminal's right margin. A positive value sets the
3404  ** paragraph width relative to the left margin. A negative value set the
3405  ** paragraph width relative to the right margin.
3406  ** .pp
3407  ** Also see $$wrap.
3408  */
3409  { "reply_regex", DT_REGEX|R_INDEX|R_RESORT, &C_ReplyRegex, IP "^((re|aw|sv)(\\[[0-9]+\\])*:[ \t]*)*", 0, reply_validator },
3410  /*
3411  ** .pp
3412  ** A regular expression used to recognize reply messages when threading
3413  ** and replying. The default value corresponds to the English "Re:", the
3414  ** German "Aw:" and the Swedish "Sv:".
3415  */
3416  { "reply_self", DT_BOOL, &C_ReplySelf, false },
3417  /*
3418  ** .pp
3419  ** If \fIunset\fP and you are replying to a message sent by you, NeoMutt will
3420  ** assume that you want to reply to the recipients of that message rather
3421  ** than to yourself.
3422  ** .pp
3423  ** Also see the "$alternates" command.
3424  */
3425  { "reply_to", DT_QUAD, &C_ReplyTo, MUTT_ASKYES },
3426  /*
3427  ** .pp
3428  ** If \fIset\fP, when replying to a message, NeoMutt will use the address listed
3429  ** in the Reply-to: header as the recipient of the reply. If \fIunset\fP,
3430  ** it will use the address in the From: header field instead. This
3431  ** option is useful for reading a mailing list that sets the Reply-To:
3432  ** header field to the list address and you want to send a private
3433  ** message to the author of a message.
3434  */
3435  { "reply_with_xorig", DT_BOOL, &C_ReplyWithXorig, false },
3436  /*
3437  ** .pp
3438  ** This variable provides a toggle. When active, the From: header will be
3439  ** extracted from the current mail's 'X-Original-To:' header. This setting
3440  ** does not have precedence over "$reverse_realname".
3441  ** .pp
3442  ** Assuming 'fast_reply' is disabled, this option will prompt the user with a
3443  ** prefilled From: header.
3444  */
3445  { "resolve", DT_BOOL, &C_Resolve, true },
3446  /*
3447  ** .pp
3448  ** When \fIset\fP, the cursor will be automatically advanced to the next
3449  ** (possibly undeleted) message whenever a command that modifies the
3450  ** current message is executed.
3451  */
3452  { "resume_draft_files", DT_BOOL, &C_ResumeDraftFiles, false },
3453  /*
3454  ** .pp
3455  ** If \fIset\fP, draft files (specified by \fC-H\fP on the command
3456  ** line) are processed similarly to when resuming a postponed
3457  ** message. Recipients are not prompted for; send-hooks are not
3458  ** evaluated; no alias expansion takes place; user-defined headers
3459  ** and signatures are not added to the message.
3460  */
3461  { "resume_edited_draft_files", DT_BOOL, &C_ResumeEditedDraftFiles, true },
3462  /*
3463  ** .pp
3464  ** If \fIset\fP, draft files previously edited (via \fC-E -H\fP on
3465  ** the command line) will have $$resume_draft_files automatically
3466  ** set when they are used as a draft file again.
3467  ** .pp
3468  ** The first time a draft file is saved, NeoMutt will add a header,
3469  ** X-Mutt-Resume-Draft to the saved file. The next time the draft
3470  ** file is read in, if NeoMutt sees the header, it will set
3471  ** $$resume_draft_files.
3472  ** .pp
3473  ** This option is designed to prevent multiple signatures,
3474  ** user-defined headers, and other processing effects from being
3475  ** made multiple times to the draft file.
3476  */
3477  { "reverse_alias", DT_BOOL|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_ReverseAlias, false },
3478  /*
3479  ** .pp
3480  ** This variable controls whether or not NeoMutt will display the "personal"
3481  ** name from your aliases in the index menu if it finds an alias that
3482  ** matches the message's sender. For example, if you have the following
3483  ** alias:
3484  ** .ts
3485  ** alias juser abd30425@somewhere.net (Joe User)
3486  ** .te
3487  ** .pp
3488  ** and then you receive mail which contains the following header:
3489  ** .ts
3490  ** From: abd30425@somewhere.net
3491  ** .te
3492  ** .pp
3493  ** It would be displayed in the index menu as "Joe User" instead of
3494  ** "abd30425@somewhere.net." This is useful when the person's e-mail
3495  ** address is not human friendly.
3496  */
3497  { "reverse_name", DT_BOOL|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_ReverseName, false },
3498  /*
3499  ** .pp
3500  ** It may sometimes arrive that you receive mail to a certain machine,
3501  ** move the messages to another machine, and reply to some the messages
3502  ** from there. If this variable is \fIset\fP, the default \fIFrom:\fP line of
3503  ** the reply messages is built using the address where you received the
3504  ** messages you are replying to \fBif\fP that address matches your
3505  ** "$alternates". If the variable is \fIunset\fP, or the address that would be
3506  ** used doesn't match your "$alternates", the \fIFrom:\fP line will use
3507  ** your address on the current machine.
3508  ** .pp
3509  ** Also see the "$alternates" command and $$reverse_realname.
3510  */
3511  { "reverse_realname", DT_BOOL|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_ReverseRealname, true },
3512  /*
3513  ** .pp
3514  ** This variable fine-tunes the behavior of the $$reverse_name feature.
3515  ** .pp
3516  ** When it is \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will remove the real name part of a
3517  ** matching address. This allows the use of the email address
3518  ** without having to also use what the sender put in the real name
3519  ** field.
3520  ** .pp
3521  ** When it is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will use the matching address as-is.
3522  ** .pp
3523  ** In either case, a missing real name will be filled in afterwards
3524  ** using the value of $$realname.
3525  */
3526  { "rfc2047_parameters", DT_BOOL, &C_Rfc2047Parameters, false },
3527  /*
3528  ** .pp
3529  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will decode RFC2047-encoded MIME
3530  ** parameters. You want to set this variable when NeoMutt suggests you
3531  ** to save attachments to files named like:
3532  ** .ts
3533  ** =?iso-8859-1?Q?file=5F=E4=5F991116=2Ezip?=
3534  ** .te
3535  ** .pp
3536  ** When this variable is \fIset\fP interactively, the change won't be
3537  ** active until you change folders.
3538  ** .pp
3539  ** Note that this use of RFC2047's encoding is explicitly
3540  ** prohibited by the standard, but nevertheless encountered in the
3541  ** wild.
3542  ** .pp
3543  ** Also note that setting this parameter will \fInot\fP have the effect
3544  ** that NeoMutt \fIgenerates\fP this kind of encoding. Instead, NeoMutt will
3545  ** unconditionally use the encoding specified in RFC2231.
3546  */
3547  { "save_address", DT_BOOL, &C_SaveAddress, false },
3548  /*
3549  ** .pp
3550  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will take the sender's full address when choosing a
3551  ** default folder for saving a mail. If $$save_name or $$force_name
3552  ** is \fIset\fP too, the selection of the Fcc folder will be changed as well.
3553  */
3554  { "save_empty", DT_BOOL, &C_SaveEmpty, true },
3555  /*
3556  ** .pp
3557  ** When \fIunset\fP, mailboxes which contain no saved messages will be removed
3558  ** when closed (the exception is $$spoolfile which is never removed).
3559  ** If \fIset\fP, mailboxes are never removed.
3560  ** .pp
3561  ** \fBNote:\fP This only applies to mbox and MMDF folders, NeoMutt does not
3562  ** delete MH and Maildir directories.
3563  */
3564  { "save_history", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_SaveHistory, 0 },
3565  /*
3566  ** .pp
3567  ** This variable controls the size of the history (per category) saved in the
3568  ** $$history_file file.
3569  */
3570  { "save_name", DT_BOOL, &C_SaveName, false },
3571  /*
3572  ** .pp
3573  ** This variable controls how copies of outgoing messages are saved.
3574  ** When \fIset\fP, a check is made to see if a mailbox specified by the
3575  ** recipient address exists (this is done by searching for a mailbox in
3576  ** the $$folder directory with the \fIusername\fP part of the
3577  ** recipient address). If the mailbox exists, the outgoing message will
3578  ** be saved to that mailbox, otherwise the message is saved to the
3579  ** $$record mailbox.
3580  ** .pp
3581  ** Also see the $$force_name variable.
3582  */
3583 #ifdef USE_NNTP
3584  { "save_unsubscribed", DT_BOOL, &C_SaveUnsubscribed, false },
3585  /*
3586  ** .pp
3587  ** When \fIset\fP, info about unsubscribed newsgroups will be saved into
3588  ** "newsrc" file and into cache.
3589  */
3590 #endif
3591  { "score", DT_BOOL, &C_Score, true },
3592  /*
3593  ** .pp
3594  ** When this variable is \fIunset\fP, scoring is turned off. This can
3595  ** be useful to selectively disable scoring for certain folders when the
3596  ** $$score_threshold_delete variable and related are used.
3597  */
3598  { "score_threshold_delete", DT_NUMBER, &C_ScoreThresholdDelete, -1 },
3599  /*
3600  ** .pp
3601  ** Messages which have been assigned a score equal to or lower than the value
3602  ** of this variable are automatically marked for deletion by NeoMutt. Since
3603  ** NeoMutt scores are always greater than or equal to zero, the default setting
3604  ** of this variable will never mark a message for deletion.
3605  */
3606  { "score_threshold_flag", DT_NUMBER, &C_ScoreThresholdFlag, 9999 },
3607  /*
3608  ** .pp
3609  ** Messages which have been assigned a score greater than or equal to this
3610  ** variable's value are automatically marked "flagged".
3611  */
3612  { "score_threshold_read", DT_NUMBER, &C_ScoreThresholdRead, -1 },
3613  /*
3614  ** .pp
3615  ** Messages which have been assigned a score equal to or lower than the value
3616  ** of this variable are automatically marked as read by NeoMutt. Since
3617  ** NeoMutt scores are always greater than or equal to zero, the default setting
3618  ** of this variable will never mark a message read.
3619  */
3620  { "search_context", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_SearchContext, 0 },
