3621  /*
3622  ** .pp
3623  ** For the pager, this variable specifies the number of lines shown
3624  ** before search results. By default, search results will be top-aligned.
3625  */
3626  { "send_charset", DT_STRING, &C_SendCharset, IP "us-ascii:iso-8859-1:utf-8", 0, charset_validator },
3627  /*
3628  ** .pp
3629  ** A colon-delimited list of character sets for outgoing messages. NeoMutt will use the
3630  ** first character set into which the text can be converted exactly.
3631  ** If your $$charset is not "iso-8859-1" and recipients may not
3632  ** understand "UTF-8", it is advisable to include in the list an
3633  ** appropriate widely used standard character set (such as
3634  ** "iso-8859-2", "koi8-r" or "iso-2022-jp") either instead of or after
3635  ** "iso-8859-1".
3636  ** .pp
3637  ** In case the text can't be converted into one of these exactly,
3638  ** NeoMutt uses $$charset as a fallback.
3639  */
3640  { "sendmail", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_Sendmail, IP SENDMAIL " -oem -oi" },
3641  /*
3642  ** .pp
3643  ** Specifies the program and arguments used to deliver mail sent by NeoMutt.
3644  ** NeoMutt expects that the specified program interprets additional
3645  ** arguments as recipient addresses. NeoMutt appends all recipients after
3646  ** adding a \fC--\fP delimiter (if not already present). Additional
3647  ** flags, such as for $$use_8bitmime, $$use_envelope_from,
3648  ** $$dsn_notify, or $$dsn_return will be added before the delimiter.
3649  ** .pp
3650  ** \fBSee also:\fP $$write_bcc.
3651  */
3652  { "sendmail_wait", DT_NUMBER, &C_SendmailWait, 0 },
3653  /*
3654  ** .pp
3655  ** Specifies the number of seconds to wait for the $$sendmail process
3656  ** to finish before giving up and putting delivery in the background.
3657  ** .pp
3658  ** NeoMutt interprets the value of this variable as follows:
3659  ** .dl
3660  ** .dt >0 .dd number of seconds to wait for sendmail to finish before continuing
3661  ** .dt 0 .dd wait forever for sendmail to finish
3662  ** .dt <0 .dd always put sendmail in the background without waiting
3663  ** .de
3664  ** .pp
3665  ** Note that if you specify a value other than 0, the output of the child
3666  ** process will be put in a temporary file. If there is some error, you
3667  ** will be informed as to where to find the output.
3668  */
3669  { "shell", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_Shell, IP "/bin/sh" },
3670  /*
3671  ** .pp
3672  ** Command to use when spawning a subshell.
3673  ** If not specified, then the user's login shell from \fC/etc/passwd\fP is used.
3674  */
3675  { "show_multipart_alternative", DT_STRING, &C_ShowMultipartAlternative, 0, 0, multipart_validator },
3676  /*
3677  ** .pp
3678  ** When \fIset\fP to \fCinfo\fP, the multipart/alternative information is shown.
3679  ** When \fIset\fP to \fCinline\fP, all of the alternatives are displayed.
3680  ** When not set, the default behavior is to show only the chosen alternative.
3681  */
3682 #ifdef USE_NNTP
3683  { "show_new_news", DT_BOOL, &C_ShowNewNews, true },
3684  /*
3685  ** .pp
3686  ** If \fIset\fP, news server will be asked for new newsgroups on entering
3687  ** the browser. Otherwise, it will be done only once for a news server.
3688  ** Also controls whether or not number of new articles of subscribed
3689  ** newsgroups will be then checked.
3690  */
3691  { "show_only_unread", DT_BOOL, &C_ShowOnlyUnread, false },
3692  /*
3693  ** .pp
3694  ** If \fIset\fP, only subscribed newsgroups that contain unread articles
3695  ** will be displayed in browser.
3696  */
3697 #endif
3698 #ifdef USE_SIDEBAR
3699  { "sidebar_component_depth", DT_NUMBER|R_SIDEBAR, &C_SidebarComponentDepth, 0 },
3700  /*
3701  ** .pp
3702  ** By default the sidebar will show the mailbox's path, relative to the
3703  ** $$folder variable. This specifies the number of parent directories to hide
3704  ** from display in the sidebar. For example: If a maildir is normally
3705  ** displayed in the sidebar as dir1/dir2/dir3/maildir, setting
3706  ** \fCsidebar_component_depth=2\fP will display it as dir3/maildir, having
3707  ** truncated the 2 highest directories.
3708  ** .pp
3709  ** \fBSee also:\fP $$sidebar_short_path
3710  */
3711  { "sidebar_delim_chars", DT_STRING|R_SIDEBAR, &C_SidebarDelimChars, IP "/." },
3712  /*
3713  ** .pp
3714  ** This contains the list of characters which you would like to treat
3715  ** as folder separators for displaying paths in the sidebar.
3716  ** .pp
3717  ** Local mail is often arranged in directories: 'dir1/dir2/mailbox'.
3718  ** .ts
3719  ** set sidebar_delim_chars='/'
3720  ** .te
3721  ** .pp
3722  ** IMAP mailboxes are often named: 'folder1.folder2.mailbox'.
3723  ** .ts
3724  ** set sidebar_delim_chars='.'
3725  ** .te
3726  ** .pp
3727  ** \fBSee also:\fP $$sidebar_short_path, $$sidebar_folder_indent, $$sidebar_indent_string.
3728  */
3729  { "sidebar_divider_char", DT_STRING|R_SIDEBAR, &C_SidebarDividerChar, 0 },
3730  /*
3731  ** .pp
3732  ** This specifies the characters to be drawn between the sidebar (when
3733  ** visible) and the other NeoMutt panels. ASCII and Unicode line-drawing
3734  ** characters are supported.
3735  */
3736  { "sidebar_folder_indent", DT_BOOL|R_SIDEBAR, &C_SidebarFolderIndent, false },
3737  /*
3738  ** .pp
3739  ** Set this to indent mailboxes in the sidebar.
3740  ** .pp
3741  ** \fBSee also:\fP $$sidebar_short_path, $$sidebar_indent_string, $$sidebar_delim_chars.
3742  */
3743  { "sidebar_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY|R_SIDEBAR, &C_SidebarFormat, IP "%B%* %n" },
3744  /*
3745  ** .pp
3746  ** This variable allows you to customize the sidebar display. This string is
3747  ** similar to $$index_format, but has its own set of \fCprintf(3)\fP-like
3748  ** sequences:
3749  ** .dl
3750  ** .dt %B .dd .dd Name of the mailbox
3751  ** .dt %d .dd * @ .dd Number of deleted messages in the mailbox
3752  ** .dt %D .dd .dd Descriptive name of the mailbox
3753  ** .dt %F .dd * .dd Number of flagged messages in the mailbox
3754  ** .dt %L .dd * @ .dd Number of messages after limiting
3755  ** .dt %n .dd .dd 'N' if mailbox has new mail, ' ' (space) otherwise
3756  ** .dt %N .dd * .dd Number of unread messages in the mailbox (seen or unseen)
3757  ** .dt %o .dd * .dd Number of old messages in the mailbox (unread, seen)
3758  ** .dt %r .dd * .dd Number of read messages in the mailbox (read, seen)
3759  ** .dt %S .dd * .dd Size of mailbox (total number of messages)
3760  ** .dt %t .dd * @ .dd Number of tagged messages in the mailbox
3761  ** .dt %Z .dd * .dd Number of new messages in the mailbox (unread, unseen)
3762  ** .dt %! .dd .dd "!" : one flagged message;
3763  ** "!!" : two flagged messages;
3764  ** "n!" : n flagged messages (for n > 2).
3765  ** Otherwise prints nothing.
3766  ** .dt %>X .dd .dd Right justify the rest of the string and pad with "X"
3767  ** .dt %|X .dd .dd Pad to the end of the line with "X"
3768  ** .dt %*X .dd .dd Soft-fill with character "X" as pad
3769  ** .de
3770  ** .pp
3771  ** * = Can be optionally printed if nonzero
3772  ** .pp
3773  ** @ = Only applicable to the current folder
3774  ** .pp
3775  ** In order to use %S, %N, %F, and %!, $$mail_check_stats must
3776  ** be \fIset\fP. When thus set, a suggested value for this option is
3777  ** "%B%?F? [%F]?%* %?N?%N/?%S".
3778  */
3779  { "sidebar_indent_string", DT_STRING|R_SIDEBAR, &C_SidebarIndentString, IP " " },
3780  /*
3781  ** .pp
3782  ** This specifies the string that is used to indent mailboxes in the sidebar.
3783  ** It defaults to two spaces.
3784  ** .pp
3785  ** \fBSee also:\fP $$sidebar_short_path, $$sidebar_folder_indent, $$sidebar_delim_chars.
3786  */
3787  { "sidebar_new_mail_only", DT_BOOL|R_SIDEBAR, &C_SidebarNewMailOnly, false },
3788  /*
3789  ** .pp
3790  ** When set, the sidebar will only display mailboxes containing new, or
3791  ** flagged, mail.
3792  ** .pp
3793  ** \fBSee also:\fP $$sidebar_whitelist, $$sidebar_non_empty_mailbox_only.
3794  */
3795  { "sidebar_next_new_wrap", DT_BOOL, &C_SidebarNextNewWrap, false },
3796  /*
3797  ** .pp
3798  ** When set, the \fC<sidebar-next-new>\fP command will not stop and the end of
3799  ** the list of mailboxes, but wrap around to the beginning. The
3800  ** \fC<sidebar-prev-new>\fP command is similarly affected, wrapping around to
3801  ** the end of the list.
3802  */
3803  { "sidebar_non_empty_mailbox_only", DT_BOOL|R_SIDEBAR, &C_SidebarNonEmptyMailboxOnly, false },
3804  /*
3805  ** .pp
3806  ** When set, the sidebar will only display mailboxes that contain one or more mails.
3807  ** .pp
3808  ** \fBSee also:\fP $$sidebar_new_mail_only, $$sidebar_whitelist.
3809  */
3810  { "sidebar_on_right", DT_BOOL|R_INDEX|R_PAGER|R_REFLOW, &C_SidebarOnRight, false },
3811  /*
3812  ** .pp
3813  ** When set, the sidebar will appear on the right-hand side of the screen.
3814  */
3815  { "sidebar_short_path", DT_BOOL|R_SIDEBAR, &C_SidebarShortPath, false },
3816  /*
3817  ** .pp
3818  ** By default the sidebar will show the mailbox's path, relative to the
3819  ** $$folder variable. Setting \fCsidebar_shortpath=yes\fP will shorten the
3820  ** names relative to the previous name. Here's an example:
3821  ** .dl
3822  ** .dt \fBshortpath=no\fP .dd \fBshortpath=yes\fP .dd \fBshortpath=yes, folderindent=yes, indentstr=".."\fP
3823  ** .dt \fCfruit\fP .dd \fCfruit\fP .dd \fCfruit\fP
3824  ** .dt \fCfruit.apple\fP .dd \fCapple\fP .dd \fC..apple\fP
3825  ** .dt \fCfruit.banana\fP .dd \fCbanana\fP .dd \fC..banana\fP
3826  ** .dt \fCfruit.cherry\fP .dd \fCcherry\fP .dd \fC..cherry\fP
3827  ** .de
3828  ** .pp
3829  ** \fBSee also:\fP $$sidebar_delim_chars, $$sidebar_folder_indent,
3830  ** $$sidebar_indent_string, $$sidebar_component_depth.
3831  */
3832  { "sidebar_sort_method", DT_SORT|DT_SORT_SIDEBAR|R_SIDEBAR, &C_SidebarSortMethod, SORT_ORDER },
3833  /*
3834  ** .pp
3835  ** Specifies how to sort entries in the file browser. By default, the
3836  ** entries are sorted alphabetically. Valid values:
3837  ** .il
3838  ** .dd alpha (alphabetically)
3839  ** .dd count (all message count)
3840  ** .dd flagged (flagged message count)
3841  ** .dd name (alphabetically)
3842  ** .dd new (unread message count)
3843  ** .dd path (alphabetically)
3844  ** .dd unread (unread message count)
3845  ** .dd unsorted
3846  ** .ie
3847  ** .pp
3848  ** You may optionally use the "reverse-" prefix to specify reverse sorting
3849  ** order (example: "\fCset sort_browser=reverse-date\fP").
3850  */
3851  { "sidebar_visible", DT_BOOL|R_REFLOW, &C_SidebarVisible, false },
3852  /*
3853  ** .pp
3854  ** This specifies whether or not to show sidebar. The sidebar shows a list of
3855  ** all your mailboxes.
3856  ** .pp
3857  ** \fBSee also:\fP $$sidebar_format, $$sidebar_width
3858  */
3859  { "sidebar_width", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE|R_REFLOW, &C_SidebarWidth, 30 },
3860  /*
3861  ** .pp
3862  ** This controls the width of the sidebar. It is measured in screen columns.
3863  ** For example: sidebar_width=20 could display 20 ASCII characters, or 10
3864  ** Chinese characters.
3865  */
3866 #endif
3867  { "sig_dashes", DT_BOOL, &C_SigDashes, true },
3868  /*
3869  ** .pp
3870  ** If \fIset\fP, a line containing "-- " (note the trailing space) will be inserted before your
3871  ** $$signature. It is \fBstrongly\fP recommended that you not \fIunset\fP
3872  ** this variable unless your signature contains just your name. The
3873  ** reason for this is because many software packages use "-- \n" to
3874  ** detect your signature. For example, NeoMutt has the ability to highlight
3875  ** the signature in a different color in the built-in pager.
3876  */
3877  { "sig_on_top", DT_BOOL, &C_SigOnTop, false },
3878  /*
3879  ** .pp
3880  ** If \fIset\fP, the signature will be included before any quoted or forwarded
3881  ** text. It is \fBstrongly\fP recommended that you do not set this variable
3882  ** unless you really know what you are doing, and are prepared to take
3883  ** some heat from netiquette guardians.
3884  */
3885  { "signature", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_FILE, &C_Signature, IP "~/.signature" },
3886  /*
3887  ** .pp
3888  ** Specifies the filename of your signature, which is appended to all
3889  ** outgoing messages. If the filename ends with a pipe ("|"), it is
3890  ** assumed that filename is a shell command and input should be read from
3891  ** its standard output.
3892  */
3893  { "simple_search", DT_STRING, &C_SimpleSearch, IP "~f %s | ~s %s" },
3894  /*
3895  ** .pp
3896  ** Specifies how NeoMutt should expand a simple search into a real search
3897  ** pattern. A simple search is one that does not contain any of the "~" pattern
3898  ** operators. See "$patterns" for more information on search patterns.
3899  ** .pp
3900  ** For example, if you simply type "joe" at a search or limit prompt, NeoMutt
3901  ** will automatically expand it to the value specified by this variable by
3902  ** replacing "%s" with the supplied string.
3903  ** For the default value, "joe" would be expanded to: "~f joe | ~s joe".
3904  */
3905  { "size_show_bytes", DT_BOOL|R_MENU, &C_SizeShowBytes, false },
3906  /*
3907  ** .pp
3908  ** If \fIset\fP, message sizes will display bytes for values less than
3909  ** 1 kilobyte. See $formatstrings-size.
3910  */
3911  { "size_show_fractions", DT_BOOL|R_MENU, &C_SizeShowFractions, true },
3912  /*
3913  ** .pp
3914  ** If \fIset\fP, message sizes will be displayed with a single decimal value
3915  ** for sizes from 0 to 10 kilobytes and 1 to 10 megabytes.
3916  ** See $formatstrings-size.
3917  */
3918  { "size_show_mb", DT_BOOL|R_MENU, &C_SizeShowMb, true },
3919  /*
3920  ** .pp
3921  ** If \fIset\fP, message sizes will display megabytes for values greater than
3922  ** or equal to 1 megabyte. See $formatstrings-size.
3923  */
3924  { "size_units_on_left", DT_BOOL|R_MENU, &C_SizeUnitsOnLeft, false },
3925  /*
3926  ** .pp
3927  ** If \fIset\fP, message sizes units will be displayed to the left of the number.
3928  ** See $formatstrings-size.
3929  */
3930  { "skip_quoted_offset", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_SkipQuotedOffset, 0 },
3931  /*
3932  ** .pp
3933  ** Lines of quoted text that are displayed before the unquoted text after
3934  ** "skip to quoted" command (S)
3935  */
3936  { "sleep_time", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_SleepTime, 1 },
3937  /*
3938  ** .pp
3939  ** Specifies time, in seconds, to pause while displaying certain informational
3940  ** messages, while moving from folder to folder and after expunging
3941  ** messages from the current folder. The default is to pause one second, so
3942  ** a value of zero for this option suppresses the pause.
3943  */
3944  { "smart_wrap", DT_BOOL|R_PAGER_FLOW, &C_SmartWrap, true },
3945  /*
3946  ** .pp
3947  ** Controls the display of lines longer than the screen width in the
3948  ** internal pager. If \fIset\fP, long lines are wrapped at a word boundary. If
3949  ** \fIunset\fP, lines are simply wrapped at the screen edge. Also see the
3950  ** $$markers variable.
3951  */
3952  { "smileys", DT_REGEX|R_PAGER, &C_Smileys, IP "(>From )|(:[-^]?[][)(><}{|/DP])" },
3953  /*
3954  ** .pp
3955  ** The \fIpager\fP uses this variable to catch some common false
3956  ** positives of $$quote_regex, most notably smileys and not consider
3957  ** a line quoted text if it also matches $$smileys. This mostly
3958  ** happens at the beginning of a line.
3959  */
3960 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_SMIME
3961  { "smime_ask_cert_label", DT_BOOL, &C_SmimeAskCertLabel, true },
3962  /*
3963  ** .pp
3964  ** This flag controls whether you want to be asked to enter a label
3965  ** for a certificate about to be added to the database or not. It is
3966  ** \fIset\fP by default.
3967  ** (S/MIME only)
3968  */
3969  { "smime_ca_location", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_FILE, &C_SmimeCaLocation, 0 },
3970  /*
3971  ** .pp
3972  ** This variable contains the name of either a directory, or a file which
3973  ** contains trusted certificates for use with OpenSSL.
3974  ** (S/MIME only)
3975  */
3976  { "smime_certificates", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_DIR, &C_SmimeCertificates, 0 },
3977  /*
3978  ** .pp
3979  ** Since for S/MIME there is no pubring/secring as with PGP, NeoMutt has to handle
3980  ** storage and retrieval of keys by itself. This is very basic right
3981  ** now, and keys and certificates are stored in two different
3982  ** directories, both named as the hash-value retrieved from
3983  ** OpenSSL. There is an index file which contains mailbox-address
3984  ** keyid pairs, and which can be manually edited. This option points to
3985  ** the location of the certificates.
3986  ** (S/MIME only)
3987  */
3988  { "smime_decrypt_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_SmimeDecryptCommand, 0 },
3989  /*
3990  ** .pp
3991  ** This format string specifies a command which is used to decrypt
3992  ** \fCapplication/x-pkcs7-mime\fP attachments.
3993  ** .pp
3994  ** The OpenSSL command formats have their own set of \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences
3995  ** similar to PGP's:
3996  ** .dl
3997  ** .dt %f .dd Expands to the name of a file containing a message.
3998  ** .dt %s .dd Expands to the name of a file containing the signature part
3999  ** of a \fCmultipart/signed\fP attachment when verifying it.
4000  ** .dt %k .dd The key-pair specified with $$smime_default_key
4001  ** .dt %i .dd Intermediate certificates
4002  ** .dt %c .dd One or more certificate IDs.
4003  ** .dt %a .dd The algorithm used for encryption.
4004  ** .dt %d .dd The message digest algorithm specified with $$smime_sign_digest_alg.
4005  ** .dt %C .dd CA location: Depending on whether $$smime_ca_location
4006  ** points to a directory or file, this expands to
4007  ** "-CApath $$smime_ca_location" or "-CAfile $$smime_ca_location".
4008  ** .de
4009  ** .pp
4010  ** For examples on how to configure these formats, see the \fCsmime.rc\fP in
4011  ** the \fCsamples/\fP subdirectory which has been installed on your system
4012  ** alongside the documentation.
4013  ** (S/MIME only)
4014  */
4015  { "smime_decrypt_use_default_key", DT_BOOL, &C_SmimeDecryptUseDefaultKey, true },
4016  /*
4017  ** .pp
4018  ** If \fIset\fP (default) this tells NeoMutt to use the default key for decryption. Otherwise,
4019  ** if managing multiple certificate-key-pairs, NeoMutt will try to use the mailbox-address
4020  ** to determine the key to use. It will ask you to supply a key, if it can't find one.
4021  ** (S/MIME only)
4022  */
4023 #endif
4024  { "smime_default_key", DT_STRING, &C_SmimeDefaultKey, 0 },
4025  /*
4026  ** .pp
4027  ** This is the default key-pair to use for S/MIME operations, and must be
4028  ** set to the keyid (the hash-value that OpenSSL generates) to work properly.
4029  ** .pp
4030  ** It will be used for encryption (see $$postpone_encrypt and
4031  ** $$smime_self_encrypt).
4032  ** .pp
4033  ** It will be used for decryption unless $$smime_decrypt_use_default_key
4034  ** is \fIunset\fP.
4035  ** .pp
4036  ** It will also be used for signing unless $$smime_sign_as is set.
4037  ** .pp
4038  ** The (now deprecated) \fIsmime_self_encrypt_as\fP is an alias for this
4039  ** variable, and should no longer be used.
4040  ** (S/MIME only)
4041  */
4042 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_SMIME
4043  { "smime_encrypt_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_SmimeEncryptCommand, 0 },
4044  /*
4045  ** .pp
4046  ** This command is used to create encrypted S/MIME messages.
4047  ** .pp
4048  ** This is a format string, see the $$smime_decrypt_command command for
4049  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
4050  ** (S/MIME only)
4051  */
4052  /*
4053  ** .pp
4054  ** Encrypt the message to $$smime_default_key too.
4055  ** (S/MIME only)
4056  */
4057 #endif
4058  { "smime_encrypt_with", DT_STRING, &C_SmimeEncryptWith, IP "aes256" },
4059  /*
4060  ** .pp
4061  ** This sets the algorithm that should be used for encryption.
4062  ** Valid choices are "aes128", "aes192", "aes256", "des", "des3", "rc2-40", "rc2-64", "rc2-128".
4063  ** (S/MIME only)
4064  */
4065 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_SMIME
4066  { "smime_get_cert_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_SmimeGetCertCommand, 0 },
4067  /*
4068  ** .pp
4069  ** This command is used to extract X509 certificates from a PKCS7 structure.
4070  ** .pp
4071  ** This is a format string, see the $$smime_decrypt_command command for
4072  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
4073  ** (S/MIME only)
4074  */
4075  { "smime_get_cert_email_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_SmimeGetCertEmailCommand, 0 },
4076  /*
4077  ** .pp
4078  ** This command is used to extract the mail address(es) used for storing
4079  ** X509 certificates, and for verification purposes (to check whether the
4080  ** certificate was issued for the sender's mailbox).
4081  ** .pp
4082  ** This is a format string, see the $$smime_decrypt_command command for
4083  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
4084  ** (S/MIME only)
4085  */
4086  { "smime_get_signer_cert_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_SmimeGetSignerCertCommand, 0 },
4087  /*
4088  ** .pp
4089  ** This command is used to extract only the signers X509 certificate from a S/MIME
4090  ** signature, so that the certificate's owner may get compared to the
4091  ** email's "From:" field.
4092  ** .pp
4093  ** This is a format string, see the $$smime_decrypt_command command for
4094  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
4095  ** (S/MIME only)
4096  */
4097  { "smime_import_cert_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_SmimeImportCertCommand, 0 },
4098  /*
4099  ** .pp
4100  ** This command is used to import a certificate via smime_keys.
4101  ** .pp
4102  ** This is a format string, see the $$smime_decrypt_command command for
4103  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences. NOTE: %c and %k will default
4104  ** to $$smime_sign_as if set, otherwise $$smime_default_key.
4105  ** (S/MIME only)
4106  */
4107 #endif
4108  { "smime_is_default", DT_BOOL, &C_SmimeIsDefault, false },
4109  /*
4110  ** .pp
4111  ** The default behavior of NeoMutt is to use PGP on all auto-sign/encryption
4112  ** operations. To override and to use OpenSSL instead this must be \fIset\fP.
4113  ** However, this has no effect while replying, since NeoMutt will automatically
4114  ** select the same application that was used to sign/encrypt the original
4115  ** message. (Note that this variable can be overridden by unsetting $$crypt_autosmime.)
4116  ** (S/MIME only)
4117  */
4118 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_SMIME
4119  { "smime_keys", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_DIR, &C_SmimeKeys, 0 },
4120  /*
4121  ** .pp
4122  ** Since for S/MIME there is no pubring/secring as with PGP, NeoMutt has to handle
4123  ** storage and retrieval of keys/certs by itself. This is very basic right now,
4124  ** and stores keys and certificates in two different directories, both
4125  ** named as the hash-value retrieved from OpenSSL. There is an index file
4126  ** which contains mailbox-address keyid pair, and which can be manually
4127  ** edited. This option points to the location of the private keys.
4128  ** (S/MIME only)
4129  */
4130  { "smime_pk7out_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_SmimePk7outCommand, 0 },
4131  /*
4132  ** .pp
4133  ** This command is used to extract PKCS7 structures of S/MIME signatures,
4134  ** in order to extract the public X509 certificate(s).
4135  ** .pp
4136  ** This is a format string, see the $$smime_decrypt_command command for
4137  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
4138  ** (S/MIME only)
4139  */
4140 #endif
4141  { "smime_self_encrypt", DT_BOOL, &C_SmimeSelfEncrypt, true },
4142  /*
4143  ** .pp
4144  ** When \fIset\fP, S/MIME encrypted messages will also be encrypted
4145  ** using the certificate in $$smime_default_key.
4146  ** (S/MIME only)
4147  */
4148  { "smime_sign_as", DT_STRING, &C_SmimeSignAs, 0 },
4149  /*
4150  ** .pp
4151  ** If you have a separate key to use for signing, you should set this
4152  ** to the signing key. Most people will only need to set $$smime_default_key.
4153  ** (S/MIME only)
4154  */
4155 #ifdef CRYPT_BACKEND_CLASSIC_SMIME
4156  { "smime_sign_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_SmimeSignCommand, 0 },
4157  /*
4158  ** .pp
4159  ** This command is used to created S/MIME signatures of type
4160  ** \fCmultipart/signed\fP, which can be read by all mail clients.
4161  ** .pp
4162  ** This is a format string, see the $$smime_decrypt_command command for
4163  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
4164  ** (S/MIME only)
4165  */
4166  { "smime_sign_digest_alg", DT_STRING, &C_SmimeSignDigestAlg, IP "sha256" },
4167  /*
4168  ** .pp
4169  ** This sets the algorithm that should be used for the signature message digest.
4170  ** Valid choices are "md5", "sha1", "sha224", "sha256", "sha384", "sha512".
4171  ** (S/MIME only)
4172  */
4173  { "smime_timeout", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_SmimeTimeout, 300 },
4174  /*
4175  ** .pp
4176  ** The number of seconds after which a cached passphrase will expire if
4177  ** not used.
4178  ** (S/MIME only)
4179  */
4180  { "smime_verify_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_SmimeVerifyCommand, 0 },
4181  /*
4182  ** .pp
4183  ** This command is used to verify S/MIME signatures of type \fCmultipart/signed\fP.
4184  ** .pp
4185  ** This is a format string, see the $$smime_decrypt_command command for
4186  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
4187  ** (S/MIME only)
4188  */
4189  { "smime_verify_opaque_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_SmimeVerifyOpaqueCommand, 0 },
4190  /*
4191  ** .pp
4192  ** This command is used to verify S/MIME signatures of type
4193  ** \fCapplication/x-pkcs7-mime\fP.
4194  ** .pp
4195  ** This is a format string, see the $$smime_decrypt_command command for
4196  ** possible \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences.
4197  ** (S/MIME only)
4198  */
4199 #endif
4200 #ifdef USE_SMTP
4201  { "smtp_authenticators", DT_SLIST|SLIST_SEP_COLON, &C_SmtpAuthenticators, 0 },
4202  /*
4203  ** .pp
4204  ** This is a colon-delimited list of authentication methods NeoMutt may
4205  ** attempt to use to log in to an SMTP server, in the order NeoMutt should
4206  ** try them. Authentication methods are any SASL mechanism, e.g. "plain",
4207  ** "digest-md5", "gssapi" or "cram-md5".
4208  ** This option is case-insensitive. If it is "unset"
4209  ** (the default) NeoMutt will try all available methods, in order from
4210  ** most-secure to least-secure. Support for the "plain" mechanism is
4211  ** bundled; other mechanisms are provided by an external SASL library (look
4212  ** for +USE_SASL in the output of neomutt -v).
4213  ** .pp
4214  ** Example:
4215  ** .ts
4216  ** set smtp_authenticators="digest-md5:cram-md5"
4217  ** .te
4218  */
4219  { "smtp_oauth_refresh_command", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_SmtpOauthRefreshCommand, 0 },
4220  /*
4221  ** .pp
4222  ** The command to run to generate an OAUTH refresh token for
4223  ** authorizing your connection to your SMTP server. This command will be
4224  ** run on every connection attempt that uses the OAUTHBEARER authentication
4225  ** mechanism. See "$oauth" for details.
4226  */
4227  { "smtp_pass", DT_STRING|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_SmtpPass, 0 },
4228  /*
4229  ** .pp
4230  ** Specifies the password for your SMTP account. If \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will
4231  ** prompt you for your password when you first send mail via SMTP.
4232  ** See $$smtp_url to configure NeoMutt to send mail via SMTP.
4233  ** .pp
4234  ** \fBWarning\fP: you should only use this option when you are on a
4235  ** fairly secure machine, because the superuser can read your neomuttrc even
4236  ** if you are the only one who can read the file.
4237  */
4238  { "smtp_user", DT_STRING|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_SmtpUser, 0 },
4239  /*
4240  ** .pp
4241  ** The username for the SMTP server.
4242  ** .pp
4243  ** This variable defaults to your user name on the local machine.
4244  */
4245  { "smtp_url", DT_STRING|DT_SENSITIVE, &C_SmtpUrl, 0 },
4246  /*
4247  ** .pp
4248  ** Defines the SMTP smarthost where sent messages should relayed for
4249  ** delivery. This should take the form of an SMTP URL, e.g.:
4250  ** .ts
4251  ** smtp[s]://[user[:pass]@]host[:port]
4252  ** .te
4253  ** .pp
4254  ** where "[...]" denotes an optional part.
4255  ** Setting this variable overrides the value of the $$sendmail
4256  ** variable.
4257  ** .pp
4258  ** Also see $$write_bcc.
4259  */
4260 #endif /* USE_SMTP */
4261  { "sort", DT_SORT|R_INDEX|R_RESORT, &C_Sort, SORT_DATE, 0, pager_validator },
4262  /*
4263  ** .pp
4264  ** Specifies how to sort messages in the "index" menu. Valid values
4265  ** are:
4266  ** .il
4267  ** .dd date or date-sent
4268  ** .dd date-received
4269  ** .dd from
4270  ** .dd mailbox-order (unsorted)
4271  ** .dd score
4272  ** .dd size
4273  ** .dd spam
4274  ** .dd subject
4275  ** .dd threads
4276  ** .dd to
4277  ** .ie
4278  ** .pp
4279  ** You may optionally use the "reverse-" prefix to specify reverse sorting
4280  ** order.
4281  ** .pp
4282  ** Example:
4283  ** .ts
4284  ** set sort=reverse-date-sent
4285  ** .te
4286  */
4287  { "sort_alias", DT_SORT|DT_SORT_ALIAS, &C_SortAlias, SORT_ALIAS },
4288  /*
4289  ** .pp
4290  ** Specifies how the entries in the "alias" menu are sorted. The
4291  ** following are legal values:
4292  ** .il
4293  ** .dd address (sort alphabetically by email address)
4294  ** .dd alias (sort alphabetically by alias name)
4295  ** .dd unsorted (leave in order specified in .neomuttrc)
4296  ** .ie
4297  */
4298  { "sort_aux", DT_SORT|DT_SORT_AUX|R_INDEX|R_RESORT|R_RESORT_SUB, &C_SortAux, SORT_DATE },
4299  /*
4300  ** .pp
4301  ** This provides a secondary sort for messages in the "index" menu, used
4302  ** when the $$sort value is equal for two messages.
4303  ** .pp
4304  ** When sorting by threads, this variable controls how threads are sorted
4305  ** in relation to other threads, and how the branches of the thread trees
4306  ** are sorted. This can be set to any value that $$sort can, except
4307  ** "threads" (in that case, NeoMutt will just use "date-sent"). You can also
4308  ** specify the "last-" prefix in addition to the "reverse-" prefix, but "last-"
4309  ** must come after "reverse-". The "last-" prefix causes messages to be
4310  ** sorted against its siblings by which has the last descendant, using
4311  ** the rest of $$sort_aux as an ordering. For instance,
4312  ** .ts
4313  ** set sort_aux=last-date-received
4314  ** .te
4315  ** .pp
4316  ** would mean that if a new message is received in a
4317  ** thread, that thread becomes the last one displayed (or the first, if
4318  ** you have "\fCset sort=reverse-threads\fP".)
4319  ** .pp
4320  ** Note: For reversed-threads $$sort
4321  ** order, $$sort_aux is reversed again (which is not the right thing to do,
4322  ** but kept to not break any existing configuration setting).
4323  */
4324  { "sort_browser", DT_SORT|DT_SORT_BROWSER, &C_SortBrowser, SORT_ALPHA },
4325  /*
4326  ** .pp
4327  ** Specifies how to sort entries in the file browser. By default, the
4328  ** entries are sorted alphabetically. Valid values:
4329  ** .il
4330  ** .dd alpha (alphabetically)
4331  ** .dd count (all message count)
4332  ** .dd date
4333  ** .dd desc (description)
4334  ** .dd new (new message count)
4335  ** .dd size
4336  ** .dd unsorted
4337  ** .ie
4338  ** .pp
4339  ** You may optionally use the "reverse-" prefix to specify reverse sorting
4340  ** order (example: "\fCset sort_browser=reverse-date\fP").
4341  */
4342  { "sort_re", DT_BOOL|R_INDEX|R_RESORT|R_RESORT_INIT, &C_SortRe, true, 0, pager_validator },
4343  /*
4344  ** .pp
4345  ** This variable is only useful when sorting by mailboxes in sidebar. By default,
4346  ** entries are unsorted. Valid values:
4347  ** .il
4348  ** .dd count (all message count)
4349  ** .dd desc (virtual mailbox description)
4350  ** .dd new (new message count)
4351  ** .dd path
4352  ** .dd unsorted
4353  ** .ie
4354  */
4355  { "spam_separator", DT_STRING, &C_SpamSeparator, IP "," },
4356  /*
4357  ** .pp
4358  ** This variable controls what happens when multiple spam headers
4359  ** are matched: if \fIunset\fP, each successive header will overwrite any
4360  ** previous matches value for the spam label. If \fIset\fP, each successive
4361  ** match will append to the previous, using this variable's value as a
4362  ** separator.
4363  */
4364  { "spoolfile", DT_STRING|DT_MAILBOX, &C_Spoolfile, 0 },
4365  /*
4366  ** .pp
4367  ** If your spool mailbox is in a non-default place where NeoMutt can't find
4368  ** it, you can specify its location with this variable. The description from
4369  ** "named-mailboxes" or "virtual-mailboxes" may be used for the spoolfile.
4370  ** .pp
4371  ** If not specified, then the environment variables \fC$$$MAIL\fP and
4372  ** \fC$$$MAILDIR\fP will be checked.
4373  */
4374 #ifdef USE_SSL
4375 #ifdef USE_SSL_GNUTLS
4376  { "ssl_ca_certificates_file", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_FILE, &C_SslCaCertificatesFile, 0 },
4377  /*
4378  ** .pp
4379  ** This variable specifies a file containing trusted CA certificates.
4380  ** Any server certificate that is signed with one of these CA
4381  ** certificates is also automatically accepted. (GnuTLS only)
4382  ** .pp
4383  ** Example:
4384  ** .ts
4385  ** set ssl_ca_certificates_file=/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
4386  ** .te
4387  */
4388 #endif /* USE_SSL_GNUTLS */
4389  { "ssl_ciphers", DT_STRING, &C_SslCiphers, 0 },
4390  /*
4391  ** .pp
4392  ** Contains a colon-separated list of ciphers to use when using SSL.
4393  ** For OpenSSL, see ciphers(1) for the syntax of the string.
4394  ** .pp
4395  ** For GnuTLS, this option will be used in place of "NORMAL" at the
4396  ** start of the priority string. See gnutls_priority_init(3) for the
4397  ** syntax and more details. (Note: GnuTLS version 2.1.7 or higher is
4398  ** required.)
4399  */
4400  { "ssl_client_cert", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_FILE, &C_SslClientCert, 0 },
4401  /*
4402  ** .pp
4403  ** The file containing a client certificate and its associated private
4404  ** key.
4405  */
4406  { "ssl_force_tls", DT_BOOL, &C_SslForceTls, false },
4407  /*
4408  ** .pp
4409  ** If this variable is \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will require that all connections
4410  ** to remote servers be encrypted. Furthermore it will attempt to
4411  ** negotiate TLS even if the server does not advertise the capability,
4412  ** since it would otherwise have to abort the connection anyway. This
4413  ** option supersedes $$ssl_starttls.
4414  */
4415 #ifdef USE_SSL_GNUTLS
4416  { "ssl_min_dh_prime_bits", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_SslMinDhPrimeBits, 0 },
4417  /*
4418  ** .pp
4419  ** This variable specifies the minimum acceptable prime size (in bits)
4420  ** for use in any Diffie-Hellman key exchange. A value of 0 will use
4421  ** the default from the GNUTLS library. (GnuTLS only)
4422  */
4423 #endif /* USE_SSL_GNUTLS */
4424  { "ssl_starttls", DT_QUAD, &C_SslStarttls, MUTT_YES },
4425  /*
4426  ** .pp
4427  ** If \fIset\fP (the default), NeoMutt will attempt to use \fCSTARTTLS\fP on servers
4428  ** advertising the capability. When \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will not attempt to
4429  ** use \fCSTARTTLS\fP regardless of the server's capabilities.
4430  ** .pp
4431  ** \fBNote\fP that \fCSTARTTLS\fP is subject to many kinds of
4432  ** attacks, including the ability of a machine-in-the-middle to
4433  ** suppress the advertising of support. Setting $$ssl_force_tls is
4434  ** recommended if you rely on \fCSTARTTLS\fP.
4435  */
4436 #ifdef USE_SSL_OPENSSL
4437  { "ssl_use_sslv2", DT_BOOL, &C_SslUseSslv2, false },
4438  /*
4439  ** .pp
4440  ** If \fIset\fP , NeoMutt will use SSLv2 when communicating with servers that
4441  ** request it. \fBN.B. As of 2011, SSLv2 is considered insecure, and using
4442  ** is inadvisable. See https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6176 .\fP
4443  ** (OpenSSL only)
4444  */
4445 #endif /* defined USE_SSL_OPENSSL */
4446  { "ssl_use_sslv3", DT_BOOL, &C_SslUseSslv3, false },
4447  /*
4448  ** .pp
4449  ** If \fIset\fP , NeoMutt will use SSLv3 when communicating with servers that
4450  ** request it. \fBN.B. As of 2015, SSLv3 is considered insecure, and using
4451  ** it is inadvisable. See https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7525 .\fP
4452  */
4453  { "ssl_use_tlsv1", DT_BOOL, &C_SslUseTlsv1, false },
4454  /*
4455  ** .pp
4456  ** If \fIset\fP , NeoMutt will use TLSv1.0 when communicating with servers that
4457  ** request it. \fBN.B. As of 2015, TLSv1.0 is considered insecure, and using
4458  ** it is inadvisable. See https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7525 .\fP
4459  */
4460  { "ssl_use_tlsv1_1", DT_BOOL, &C_SslUseTlsv11, false },
4461  /*
4462  ** .pp
4463  ** If \fIset\fP , NeoMutt will use TLSv1.1 when communicating with servers that
4464  ** request it. \fBN.B. As of 2015, TLSv1.1 is considered insecure, and using
4465  ** it is inadvisable. See https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7525 .\fP
4466  */
4467  { "ssl_use_tlsv1_2", DT_BOOL, &C_SslUseTlsv12, true },
4468  /*
4469  ** .pp
4470  ** If \fIset\fP , NeoMutt will use TLSv1.2 when communicating with servers that
4471  ** request it.
4472  */
4473  { "ssl_use_tlsv1_3", DT_BOOL, &C_SslUseTlsv13, true },
4474  /*
4475  ** .pp
4476  ** If \fIset\fP , NeoMutt will use TLSv1.3 when communicating with servers that
4477  ** request it.
4478  */
4479 #ifdef USE_SSL_OPENSSL
4480  { "ssl_usesystemcerts", DT_BOOL, &C_SslUsesystemcerts, true },
4481  /*
4482  ** .pp
4483  ** If set to \fIyes\fP, NeoMutt will use CA certificates in the
4484  ** system-wide certificate store when checking if a server certificate
4485  ** is signed by a trusted CA. (OpenSSL only)
4486  */
4487 #endif
4488  { "ssl_verify_dates", DT_BOOL, &C_SslVerifyDates, true },
4489  /*
4490  ** .pp
4491  ** If \fIset\fP (the default), NeoMutt will not automatically accept a server
4492  ** certificate that is either not yet valid or already expired. You should
4493  ** only unset this for particular known hosts, using the
4494  ** \fC$<account-hook>\fP function.
4495  */
4496  { "ssl_verify_host", DT_BOOL, &C_SslVerifyHost, true },
4497  /*
4498  ** .pp
4499  ** If \fIset\fP (the default), NeoMutt will not automatically accept a server
4500  ** certificate whose host name does not match the host used in your folder
4501  ** URL. You should only unset this for particular known hosts, using
4502  ** the \fC$<account-hook>\fP function.
4503  */
4504 #ifdef USE_SSL_OPENSSL
4505 #ifdef HAVE_SSL_PARTIAL_CHAIN
4506  { "ssl_verify_partial_chains", DT_BOOL, &C_SslVerifyPartialChains, false },
4507  /*
4508  ** .pp
4509  ** This option should not be changed from the default unless you understand
4510  ** what you are doing.
4511  ** .pp
4512  ** Setting this variable to \fIyes\fP will permit verifying partial
4513  ** certification chains, i. e. a certificate chain where not the root,
4514  ** but an intermediate certificate CA, or the host certificate, are
4515  ** marked trusted (in $$certificate_file), without marking the root
4516  ** signing CA as trusted.
4517  ** .pp
4518  ** (OpenSSL 1.0.2b and newer only).
4519  */
4520 #endif /* defined HAVE_SSL_PARTIAL_CHAIN */
4521 #endif /* defined USE_SSL_OPENSSL */
4522 #endif /* defined(USE_SSL) */
4523  { "status_chars", DT_MBTABLE|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_StatusChars, IP "-*%A" },
4524  /*
4525  ** .pp
4526  ** Controls the characters used by the "%r" indicator in $$status_format.
4527  ** .dl
4528  ** .dt \fBCharacter\fP .dd \fBDefault\fP .dd \fBDescription\fP
4529  ** .dt 1 .dd - .dd Mailbox is unchanged
4530  ** .dt 2 .dd * .dd Mailbox has been changed and needs to be resynchronized
4531  ** .dt 3 .dd % .dd Mailbox is read-only, or will not be written when exiting.
4532  ** (You can toggle whether to write changes to a mailbox
4533  ** with the \fC<toggle-write>\fP operation, bound by default
4534  ** to "%")
4535  ** .dt 4 .dd A .dd Folder opened in attach-message mode.
4536  ** (Certain operations like composing a new mail, replying,
4537  ** forwarding, etc. are not permitted in this mode)
4538  ** .de
4539  */
4540  { "status_format", DT_STRING|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_StatusFormat, IP "-%r-NeoMutt: %D [Msgs:%?M?%M/?%m%?n? New:%n?%?o? Old:%o?%?d? Del:%d?%?F? Flag:%F?%?t? Tag:%t?%?p? Post:%p?%?b? Inc:%b?%?l? %l?]---(%s/%S)-%>-(%P)---" },
4541  /*
4542  ** .pp
4543  ** Controls the format of the status line displayed in the "index"
4544  ** menu. This string is similar to $$index_format, but has its own
4545  ** set of \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequences:
4546  ** .dl
4547  ** .dt %b .dd * .dd Number of mailboxes with new mail
4548  ** .dt %d .dd * .dd Number of deleted messages
4549  ** .dt %D .dd .dd Description of the mailbox
4550  ** .dt %f .dd .dd The full pathname of the current mailbox
4551  ** .dt %F .dd * .dd Number of flagged messages
4552  ** .dt %h .dd .dd Local hostname
4553  ** .dt %l .dd * .dd Size (in bytes) of the current mailbox (see $formatstrings-size)
4554  ** .dt %L .dd * .dd Size (in bytes) of the messages shown
4555  ** (i.e., which match the current limit) (see $formatstrings-size)
4556  ** .dt %m .dd * .dd The number of messages in the mailbox
4557  ** .dt %M .dd * .dd The number of messages shown (i.e., which match the current limit)
4558  ** .dt %n .dd * .dd Number of new messages in the mailbox (unread, unseen)
4559  ** .dt %o .dd * .dd Number of old messages in the mailbox (unread, seen)
4560  ** .dt %p .dd * .dd Number of postponed messages
4561  ** .dt %P .dd .dd Percentage of the way through the index
4562  ** .dt %r .dd .dd Modified/read-only/won't-write/attach-message indicator,
4563  ** According to $$status_chars
4564  ** .dt %R .dd * .dd Number of read messages in the mailbox (read, seen)
4565  ** .dt %s .dd .dd Current sorting mode ($$sort)
4566  ** .dt %S .dd .dd Current aux sorting method ($$sort_aux)
4567  ** .dt %t .dd * .dd Number of tagged messages in the mailbox
4568  ** .dt %u .dd * .dd Number of unread messages in the mailbox (seen or unseen)
4569  ** .dt %v .dd .dd NeoMutt version string
4570  ** .dt %V .dd * .dd Currently active limit pattern, if any
4571  ** .dt %>X .dd .dd Right justify the rest of the string and pad with "X"
4572  ** .dt %|X .dd .dd Pad to the end of the line with "X"
4573  ** .dt %*X .dd .dd Soft-fill with character "X" as pad
4574  ** .de
4575  ** .pp
4576  ** For an explanation of "soft-fill", see the $$index_format documentation.
4577  ** .pp
4578  ** * = can be optionally printed if nonzero
4579  ** .pp
4580  ** Some of the above sequences can be used to optionally print a string
4581  ** if their value is nonzero. For example, you may only want to see the
4582  ** number of flagged messages if such messages exist, since zero is not
4583  ** particularly meaningful. To optionally print a string based upon one
4584  ** of the above sequences, the following construct is used:
4585  ** .pp
4586  ** \fC%?<sequence_char>?<optional_string>?\fP
4587  ** .pp
4588  ** where \fIsequence_char\fP is a character from the table above, and
4589  ** \fIoptional_string\fP is the string you would like printed if
4590  ** \fIsequence_char\fP is nonzero. \fIoptional_string\fP \fBmay\fP contain
4591  ** other sequences as well as normal text, but you may \fBnot\fP nest
4592  ** optional strings.
4593  ** .pp
4594  ** Here is an example illustrating how to optionally print the number of
4595  ** new messages in a mailbox:
4596  ** .pp
4597  ** \fC%?n?%n new messages.?\fP
4598  ** .pp
4599  ** You can also switch between two strings using the following construct:
4600  ** .pp
4601  ** \fC%?<sequence_char>?<if_string>&<else_string>?\fP
4602  ** .pp
4603  ** If the value of \fIsequence_char\fP is non-zero, \fIif_string\fP will
4604  ** be expanded, otherwise \fIelse_string\fP will be expanded.
4605  ** .pp
4606  ** You can force the result of any \fCprintf(3)\fP-like sequence to be lowercase
4607  ** by prefixing the sequence character with an underscore ("_") sign.
4608  ** For example, if you want to display the local hostname in lowercase,
4609  ** you would use: "\fC%_h\fP".
4610  ** .pp
4611  ** If you prefix the sequence character with a colon (":") character, NeoMutt
4612  ** will replace any dots in the expansion by underscores. This might be helpful
4613  ** with IMAP folders that don't like dots in folder names.
4614  */
4615  { "status_on_top", DT_BOOL|R_REFLOW, &C_StatusOnTop, false },
4616  /*
4617  ** .pp
4618  ** Setting this variable causes the "status bar" to be displayed on
4619  ** the first line of the screen rather than near the bottom. If $$help
4620  ** is \fIset\fP, too it'll be placed at the bottom.
4621  */
4622  { "strict_threads", DT_BOOL|R_RESORT|R_RESORT_INIT|R_INDEX, &C_StrictThreads, false, 0, pager_validator },
4623  /*
4624  ** .pp
4625  ** If \fIset\fP, threading will only make use of the "In-Reply-To" and
4626  ** "References:" fields when you $$sort by message threads. By
4627  ** default, messages with the same subject are grouped together in
4628  ** "pseudo threads.". This may not always be desirable, such as in a
4629  ** personal mailbox where you might have several unrelated messages with
4630  ** the subjects like "hi" which will get grouped together. See also
4631  ** $$sort_re for a less drastic way of controlling this
4632  ** behavior.
4633  */
4634  { "suspend", DT_BOOL, &C_Suspend, true },
4635  /*
4636  ** .pp
4637  ** When \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt won't stop when the user presses the terminal's
4638  ** \fIsusp\fP key, usually "^Z". This is useful if you run NeoMutt
4639  ** inside an xterm using a command like "\fCxterm -e neomutt\fP".
4640  */
4641  { "text_flowed", DT_BOOL, &C_TextFlowed, false },
4642  /*
4643  ** .pp
4644  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will generate "format=flowed" bodies with a content type
4645  ** of "\fCtext/plain; format=flowed\fP".
4646  ** This format is easier to handle for some mailing software, and generally
4647  ** just looks like ordinary text. To actually make use of this format's
4648  ** features, you'll need support in your editor.
4649  ** .pp
4650  ** The option only controls newly composed messages. Postponed messages,
4651  ** resent messages, and draft messages (via -H on the command line) will
4652  ** use the content-type of the source message.
4653  ** .pp
4654  ** Note that $$indent_string is ignored when this option is \fIset\fP.
4655  */
4656  { "thorough_search", DT_BOOL, &C_ThoroughSearch, true },
4657  /*
4658  ** .pp
4659  ** Affects the \fC~b\fP and \fC~h\fP search operations described in
4660  ** section "$patterns". If \fIset\fP, the headers and body/attachments of
4661  ** messages to be searched are decoded before searching. If \fIunset\fP,
4662  ** messages are searched as they appear in the folder.
4663  ** .pp
4664  ** Users searching attachments or for non-ASCII characters should \fIset\fP
4665  ** this value because decoding also includes MIME parsing/decoding and possible
4666  ** character set conversions. Otherwise NeoMutt will attempt to match against the
4667  ** raw message received (for example quoted-printable encoded or with encoded
4668  ** headers) which may lead to incorrect search results.
4669  */
4670  { "thread_received", DT_BOOL|R_RESORT|R_RESORT_INIT|R_INDEX, &C_ThreadReceived, false, 0, pager_validator },
4671  /*
4672  ** .pp
4673  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt uses the date received rather than the date sent
4674  ** to thread messages by subject.
4675  */
4676  { "tilde", DT_BOOL|R_PAGER, &C_Tilde, false },
4677  /*
4678  ** .pp
4679  ** When \fIset\fP, the internal-pager will pad blank lines to the bottom of the
4680  ** screen with a tilde ("~").
4681  */
4682  { "time_inc", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_TimeInc, 0 },
4683  /*
4684  ** .pp
4685  ** Along with $$read_inc, $$write_inc, and $$net_inc, this
4686  ** variable controls the frequency with which progress updates are
4687  ** displayed. It suppresses updates less than $$time_inc milliseconds
4688  ** apart. This can improve throughput on systems with slow terminals,
4689  ** or when running NeoMutt on a remote system.
4690  ** .pp
4691  ** Also see the "$tuning" section of the manual for performance considerations.
4692  */
4693  { "timeout", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_Timeout, 600 },
4694  /*
4695  ** .pp
4696  ** When NeoMutt is waiting for user input either idling in menus or
4697  ** in an interactive prompt, NeoMutt would block until input is
4698  ** present. Depending on the context, this would prevent certain
4699  ** operations from working, like checking for new mail or keeping
4700  ** an IMAP connection alive.
4701  ** .pp
4702  ** This variable controls how many seconds NeoMutt will at most wait
4703  ** until it aborts waiting for input, performs these operations and
4704  ** continues to wait for input.
4705  ** .pp
4706  ** A value of zero or less will cause NeoMutt to never time out.
4707  */
4708  { "tmpdir", DT_PATH|DT_PATH_DIR|DT_NOT_EMPTY, &C_Tmpdir, IP "/tmp" },
4709  /*
4710  ** .pp
4711  ** This variable allows you to specify where NeoMutt will place its
4712  ** temporary files needed for displaying and composing messages.
4713  ** .pp
4714  ** If this variable is not set, the environment variable \fC$$$TMPDIR\fP is
4715  ** used. Failing that, then "\fC/tmp\fP" is used.
4716  */
4717  { "to_chars", DT_MBTABLE|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_ToChars, IP " +TCFLR" },
4718  /*
4719  ** .pp
4720  ** Controls the character used to indicate mail addressed to you.
4721  ** .dl
4722  ** .dt \fBCharacter\fP .dd \fBDefault\fP .dd \fBDescription\fP
4723  ** .dt 1 .dd <space> .dd The mail is \fInot\fP addressed to your address.
4724  ** .dt 2 .dd + .dd You are the only recipient of the message.
4725  ** .dt 3 .dd T .dd Your address appears in the "To:" header field, but you are not the only recipient of the message.
4726  ** .dt 4 .dd C .dd Your address is specified in the "Cc:" header field, but you are not the only recipient.
4727  ** .dt 5 .dd F .dd Indicates the mail that was sent by \fIyou\fP.
4728  ** .dt 6 .dd L .dd Indicates the mail was sent to a mailing-list you subscribe to.
4729  ** .dt 7 .dd R .dd Your address appears in the "Reply-To:" header field but none of the above applies.
4730  ** .de
4731  */
4732  { "trash", DT_STRING|DT_MAILBOX, &C_Trash, 0 },
4733  /*
4734  ** .pp
4735  ** If set, this variable specifies the path of the trash folder where the
4736  ** mails marked for deletion will be moved, instead of being irremediably
4737  ** purged.
4738  ** .pp
4739  ** NOTE: When you delete a message in the trash folder, it is really
4740  ** deleted, so that you have a way to clean the trash.
4741  */
4742  { "ts_enabled", DT_BOOL|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_TsEnabled, false },
4743  /* The default must be off to force in the validity checking. */
4744  /*
4745  ** .pp
4746  ** Controls whether NeoMutt tries to set the terminal status line and icon name.
4747  ** Most terminal emulators emulate the status line in the window title.
4748  */
4749  { "ts_icon_format", DT_STRING|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_TsIconFormat, IP "M%?n?AIL&ail?" },
4750  /*
4751  ** .pp
4752  ** Controls the format of the icon title, as long as "$$ts_enabled" is set.
4753  ** This string is identical in formatting to the one used by
4754  ** "$$status_format".
4755  */
4756  { "ts_status_format", DT_STRING|R_INDEX|R_PAGER, &C_TsStatusFormat, IP "NeoMutt with %?m?%m messages&no messages?%?n? [%n NEW]?" },
4757  /*
4758  ** .pp
4759  ** Controls the format of the terminal status line (or window title),
4760  ** provided that "$$ts_enabled" has been set. This string is identical in
4761  ** formatting to the one used by "$$status_format".
4762  */
4763 #ifdef USE_SOCKET
4764  { "tunnel", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_Tunnel, 0 },
4765  /*
4766  ** .pp
4767  ** Setting this variable will cause NeoMutt to open a pipe to a command
4768  ** instead of a raw socket. You may be able to use this to set up
4769  ** preauthenticated connections to your IMAP/POP3/SMTP server. Example:
4770  ** .ts
4771  ** set tunnel="ssh -q mailhost.net /usr/local/libexec/imapd"
4772  ** .te
4773  ** .pp
4774  ** Note: For this example to work you must be able to log in to the remote
4775  ** machine without having to enter a password.
4776  ** .pp
4777  ** When set, NeoMutt uses the tunnel for all remote connections.
4778  ** Please see "$account-hook" in the manual for how to use different
4779  ** tunnel commands per connection.
4780  */
4781  { "tunnel_is_secure", DT_BOOL, &C_TunnelIsSecure, true },
4782  /*
4783  ** .pp
4784  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will assume the $$tunnel connection does not need
4785  ** STARTTLS to be enabled. It will also allow IMAP PREAUTH server
4786  ** responses inside a $tunnel to proceed. This is appropriate if $$tunnel
4787  ** uses ssh or directly invokes the server locally.
4788  ** .pp
4789  ** When \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will negotiate STARTTLS according to the
4790  ** $ssl_starttls and $ssl_force_tls variables. If $ssl_force_tls is
4791  ** set, NeoMutt will abort connecting if an IMAP server responds with PREAUTH.
4792  ** This setting is appropriate if $$tunnel does not provide security and
4793  ** could be tampered with by attackers.
4794  */
4795 #endif
4796  { "uncollapse_jump", DT_BOOL, &C_UncollapseJump, false },
4797  /*
4798  ** .pp
4799  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will jump to the next unread message, if any,
4800  ** when the current thread is \fIun\fPcollapsed.
4801  */
4802  { "uncollapse_new", DT_BOOL, &C_UncollapseNew, true },
4803  /*
4804  ** .pp
4805  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will automatically uncollapse any collapsed thread
4806  ** that receives a new message. When \fIunset\fP, collapsed threads will
4807  ** remain collapsed. the presence of the new message will still affect
4808  ** index sorting, though.
4809  */
4810  { "use_8bitmime", DT_BOOL, &C_Use8bitmime, false },
4811  /*
4812  ** .pp
4813  ** \fBWarning:\fP do not set this variable unless you are using a version
4814  ** of sendmail which supports the \fC-B8BITMIME\fP flag (such as sendmail
4815  ** 8.8.x) or you may not be able to send mail.
4816  ** .pp
4817  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will invoke $$sendmail with the \fC-B8BITMIME\fP
4818  ** flag when sending 8-bit messages to enable ESMTP negotiation.
4819  */
4820  { "use_domain", DT_BOOL, &C_UseDomain, true },
4821  /*
4822  ** .pp
4823  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will qualify all local addresses (ones without the
4824  ** "@host" portion) with the value of $$hostname. If \fIunset\fP, no
4825  ** addresses will be qualified.
4826  */
4827  { "use_envelope_from", DT_BOOL, &C_UseEnvelopeFrom, false },
4828  /*
4829  ** .pp
4830  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will set the \fIenvelope\fP sender of the message.
4831  ** If $$envelope_from_address is \fIset\fP, it will be used as the sender
4832  ** address. If \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will attempt to derive the sender from the
4833  ** "From:" header.
4834  ** .pp
4835  ** Note that this information is passed to sendmail command using the
4836  ** \fC-f\fP command line switch. Therefore setting this option is not useful
4837  ** if the $$sendmail variable already contains \fC-f\fP or if the
4838  ** executable pointed to by $$sendmail doesn't support the \fC-f\fP switch.
4839  */
4840  { "use_from", DT_BOOL, &C_UseFrom, true },
4841  /*
4842  ** .pp
4843  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will generate the "From:" header field when
4844  ** sending messages. If \fIunset\fP, no "From:" header field will be
4845  ** generated unless the user explicitly sets one using the "$my_hdr"
4846  ** command.
4847  */
4848 #ifdef HAVE_GETADDRINFO
4849  { "use_ipv6", DT_BOOL, &C_UseIpv6, true },
4850  /*
4851  ** .pp
4852  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will look for IPv6 addresses of hosts it tries to
4853  ** contact. If this option is \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will restrict itself to IPv4 addresses.
4854  ** Normally, the default should work.
4855  */
4856 #endif /* HAVE_GETADDRINFO */
4857  { "user_agent", DT_BOOL, &C_UserAgent, false },
4858  /*
4859  ** .pp
4860  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will add a "User-Agent:" header to outgoing
4861  ** messages, indicating which version of NeoMutt was used for composing
4862  ** them.
4863  */
4864 #ifdef USE_NOTMUCH
4865  { "vfolder_format", DT_STRING|DT_NOT_EMPTY|R_INDEX, &C_VfolderFormat, IP "%2C %?n?%4n/& ?%4m %f" },
4866  /*
4867  ** .pp
4868  ** This variable allows you to customize the file browser display for virtual
4869  ** folders to your personal taste. This string uses many of the same
4870  ** expandos as $$folder_format.
4871  */
4872  { "virtual_spoolfile", DT_BOOL, &C_VirtualSpoolfile, false },
4873  /*
4874  ** .pp
4875  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will use the first defined virtual mailbox (see
4876  ** virtual-mailboxes) as a spool file.
4877  ** .pp
4878  ** This command is now unnecessary. $$spoolfile has been extended to support
4879  ** mailbox descriptions as a value.
4880  */
4881 #endif
4882  { "visual", DT_STRING|DT_COMMAND, &C_Visual, IP "vi" },
4883  /*
4884  ** .pp
4885  ** Specifies the visual editor to invoke when the "\fC~v\fP" command is
4886  ** given in the built-in editor.
4887  ** .pp
4888  ** $$visual is overridden by the environment variable \fC$$$VISUAL\fP or \fC$$$EDITOR\fP.
4889  */
4890  { "wait_key", DT_BOOL, &C_WaitKey, true },
4891  /*
4892  ** .pp
4893  ** Controls whether NeoMutt will ask you to press a key after an external command
4894  ** has been invoked by these functions: \fC<shell-escape>\fP,
4895  ** \fC<pipe-message>\fP, \fC<pipe-entry>\fP, \fC<print-message>\fP,
4896  ** and \fC<print-entry>\fP commands.
4897  ** .pp
4898  ** It is also used when viewing attachments with "$auto_view", provided
4899  ** that the corresponding mailcap entry has a \fIneedsterminal\fP flag,
4900  ** and the external program is interactive.
4901  ** .pp
4902  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will always ask for a key. When \fIunset\fP, NeoMutt will wait
4903  ** for a key only if the external command returned a non-zero status.
4904  */
4905  { "weed", DT_BOOL, &C_Weed, true },
4906  /*
4907  ** .pp
4908  ** When \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will weed headers when displaying, forwarding,
4909  ** printing, or replying to messages.
4910  */
4911  { "wrap", DT_NUMBER|R_PAGER_FLOW, &C_Wrap, 0 },
4912  /*
4913  ** .pp
4914  ** When set to a positive value, NeoMutt will wrap text at $$wrap characters.
4915  ** When set to a negative value, NeoMutt will wrap text so that there are $$wrap
4916  ** characters of empty space on the right side of the terminal. Setting it
4917  ** to zero makes NeoMutt wrap at the terminal width.
4918  ** .pp
4919  ** Also see $$reflow_wrap.
4920  */
4921  { "wrap_headers", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE|R_PAGER, &C_WrapHeaders, 78, 0, wrapheaders_validator },
4922  /*
4923  ** .pp
4924  ** This option specifies the number of characters to use for wrapping
4925  ** an outgoing message's headers. Allowed values are between 78 and 998
4926  ** inclusive.
4927  ** .pp
4928  ** \fBNote:\fP This option usually shouldn't be changed. RFC5233
4929  ** recommends a line length of 78 (the default), so \fBplease only change
4930  ** this setting when you know what you're doing\fP.
4931  */
4932  { "wrap_search", DT_BOOL, &C_WrapSearch, true },
4933  /*
4934  ** .pp
4935  ** Controls whether searches wrap around the end.
4936  ** .pp
4937  ** When \fIset\fP, searches will wrap around the first (or last) item. When
4938  ** \fIunset\fP, incremental searches will not wrap.
4939  */
4940  { "write_bcc", DT_BOOL, &C_WriteBcc, false },
4941  /*
4942  ** .pp
4943  ** Controls whether mutt writes out the ``Bcc:'' header when
4944  ** preparing messages to be sent. Some MTAs, such as Exim and
4945  ** Courier, do not strip the ``Bcc:'' header; so it is advisable to
4946  ** leave this unset unless you have a particular need for the header
4947  ** to be in the sent message.
4948  ** .pp
4949  ** If mutt is set to deliver directly via SMTP(see $$smtp_url),
4950  ** this option does nothing: mutt will never write out the ``Bcc:''
4951  ** header in this case.
4952  ** .pp
4953  ** Note this option only affects the sending of messages. Fcc'ed
4954  ** copies of a message will always contain the ``Bcc:'' header if
4955  ** one exists.
4956  */
4957  { "write_inc", DT_NUMBER|DT_NOT_NEGATIVE, &C_WriteInc, 10 },
4958  /*
4959  ** .pp
4960  ** When writing a mailbox, a message will be printed every
4961  ** $$write_inc messages to indicate progress. If set to 0, only a
4962  ** single message will be displayed before writing a mailbox.
4963  ** .pp
4964  ** Also see the $$read_inc, $$net_inc and $$time_inc variables and the
4965  ** "$tuning" section of the manual for performance considerations.
4966  */
4967 #ifdef USE_NNTP
4968  { "x_comment_to", DT_BOOL, &C_XCommentTo, false },
4969  /*
4970  ** .pp
4971  ** If \fIset\fP, NeoMutt will add "X-Comment-To:" field (that contains full
4972  ** name of original article author) to article that followuped to newsgroup.
4973  */
4974 #endif
4975  /*--*/
4976 
4977 #if defined(HAVE_QDBM) || defined(HAVE_TC) || defined(HAVE_KC)
4978  { "header_cache_compress", DT_DEPRECATED|DT_BOOL, &C_HeaderCacheCompress, false },
4979 #endif
4980 #if defined(HAVE_GDBM) || defined(HAVE_BDB)
4981  { "header_cache_pagesize", DT_DEPRECATED|DT_LONG, &C_HeaderCachePagesize, 0 },
4982 #endif
4983  { "ignore_linear_white_space", DT_DEPRECATED|DT_BOOL, &C_IgnoreLinearWhiteSpace, false },
4984  { "pgp_encrypt_self", DT_DEPRECATED|DT_QUAD, &C_PgpEncryptSelf, MUTT_NO },
4985  { "smime_encrypt_self", DT_DEPRECATED|DT_QUAD, &C_SmimeEncryptSelf, MUTT_NO },
4986 
4987  { "abort_noattach_regexp", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "abort_noattach_regex", },
4988  { "attach_keyword", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "abort_noattach_regex", },
4989  { "edit_hdrs", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "edit_headers", },
4990  { "envelope_from", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "use_envelope_from", },
4991  { "forw_decode", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "forward_decode", },
4992  { "forw_decrypt", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "forward_decrypt", },
4993  { "forw_format", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "forward_format", },
4994  { "forw_quote", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "forward_quote", },
4995  { "hdr_format", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "index_format", },
4996  { "indent_str", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "indent_string", },
4997  { "mime_fwd", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "mime_forward", },
4998  { "msg_format", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "message_format", },
4999 #ifdef USE_NOTMUCH
5000  { "nm_default_uri", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "nm_default_url", },
5001 #endif
5002  { "pgp_autoencrypt", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "crypt_autoencrypt", },
5003  { "pgp_autosign", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "crypt_autosign", },
5004  { "pgp_auto_traditional", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "pgp_replyinline", },
5005  { "pgp_create_traditional", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "pgp_autoinline", },
5006  { "pgp_replyencrypt", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "crypt_replyencrypt", },
5007  { "pgp_replysign", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "crypt_replysign", },
5008  { "pgp_replysignencrypted", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "crypt_replysignencrypted", },
5009  { "pgp_self_encrypt_as", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "pgp_default_key", },
5010  { "pgp_verify_sig", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "crypt_verify_sig", },
5011  { "post_indent_str", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "post_indent_string", },
5012  { "print_cmd", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "print_command", },
5013  { "quote_regexp", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "quote_regex", },
5014  { "reply_regexp", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "reply_regex", },
5015  { "smime_self_encrypt_as", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "smime_default_key", },
5016  { "xterm_icon", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "ts_icon_format", },
5017  { "xterm_set_titles", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "ts_enabled", },
5018  { "xterm_title", DT_SYNONYM, NULL, IP "ts_status_format", },
5019 
5020  { NULL, 0, NULL, 0, 0, NULL },
5021 };
5022 // clang-format on
